title: Masked Neural Detection for Constrained Channel Coding in Molecular Communication
summary: Molecular communication (MC) suffers from severe diffusion memory because molecules released for one symbol may arrive during later symbols. Neural sequence detectors, especially sliding bidirectional recurrent neural networks (SBRNNs), can substantially outperform threshold detectors in such channels. This raises a central question for MC channel coding: does a code whose advantage was established under threshold detection retain it when both coded and uncoded transmission are evaluated with neural detection? This letter answers this question for run-length-limited ISI-mitigation (RLIM) codes, a class of constrained codes previously shown to provide large BER gains in MC. Across the tested operating points, the best RLIM-SBRNN receiver beats the best uncoded receiver, chosen between threshold and SBRNN detection, in $46$ of $59$ cases, with a mean gain of $10.36\times$ over those wins. We also propose an RLIM-tailored training mask for compact SBRNN detectors, improving the unmasked RLIM-SBRNN in $227$ of $236$ comparisons with $3.267\times$ mean gain when masking is beneficial. Finally, the compact masked RLIM-SBRNN is competitive with channel-state-aware MLSE despite using no channel knowledge.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates whether run-length-limited ISI-mitigation (RLIM) codes maintain their advantage over uncoded transmission when evaluated with neural sequence detectors (SBRNNs) instead of threshold detectors, finding that RLIM-SBRNN receivers win in 46/59 cases with 10.36× mean gain, and proposes a compact RLIM-tailored training mask that improves performance further while remaining competitive with MLSE without channel knowledge.
AI.motivation: A central question for MC channel coding: does a code whose advantage was established under threshold detection retain it when both coded and uncoded transmission are evaluated with neural detection?
AI.method: Evaluate RLIM codes with SBRNN neural sequence detectors across 59 operating points, and propose a RLIM-tailored training mask for compact SBRNN detectors tested across 236 comparisons.
AI.result: Best RLIM-SBRNN beats best uncoded receiver in 46/59 cases with 10.36× mean gain; proposed mask improves unmasked RLIM-SBRNN in 227/236 comparisons with 3.267× mean gain; compact masked RLIM-SBRNN is competitive with channel-state-aware MLSE without channel knowledge.
AI.conclusion: RLIM codes retain their advantage under neural detection, and the proposed masking technique further enhances compact SBRNN performance, eliminating the need for channel knowledge.

title: The Capacity Region for Classes of Sum-Broadcast Channels
comment: A conference version will be presented at the 2026 IEEE Symposium on Information Theory
summary: We compute the capacity region of a sum of broadcast channels whose components are degraded, less-noisy, more-capable, deterministic, or semi-deterministic. We achieve this by showing that an auxiliary-receiver outer bound, previously introduced by some of the authors, matches Marton's inner bound. This result generalizes a previously known result for the sum of two reversely degraded broadcast channels due to El Gamal (1980). Moreover, we define a class of primary broadcast channels and show an analogous result for the sum of primary broadcast channels.

AI.tldr: This paper computes the capacity region for sums of broadcast channels with specific structures (degraded, less-noisy, more-capable, deterministic, semi-deterministic, or primary) by proving that an auxiliary-receiver outer bound matches Marton's inner bound, generalizing prior work by El Gamal.
AI.motivation: To extend the known result for the sum of two reversely degraded broadcast channels to more general classes of broadcast channels.
AI.method: The authors show that an auxiliary-receiver outer bound (previously introduced by some of the authors) matches Marton's inner bound for sums of broadcast channels where components are degraded, less-noisy, more-capable, deterministic, semi-deterministic, or primary.
AI.result: The capacity region is computed for sums of broadcast channels with the specified structures, generalizing El Gamal's 1980 result.
AI.conclusion: The auxiliary-receiver outer bound is tight for these channel classes, and a new class of "primary" broadcast channels is defined with an analogous capacity result.

title: JSCGC: Joint Source-Channel-Generation Coding for Wireless Generative Communications
summary: Conventional communication systems, including both separation-based coding and learning-based joint source-channel coding (JSCC), are typically designed under Shannon's rate-distortion theory. However, relying on generic distortion metrics fails to capture complex human visual perception, often resulting in blurred or unrealistic reconstructions. In this paper, we propose Joint Source-Channel-Generation Coding (JSCGC), a generative communication paradigm that replaces the conventional decoder with a generative model at the receiver. The received signal is treated as a condition that controls the sampling process into the learned conditional distribution, reformulating communication from deterministic reconstruction for distortion minimization to controlled generation for mutual information maximization under perceptual constraints. Based on this formulation, we develop a unified joint training and efficient stochastic sampling framework, and provide theoretical analysis of its effectiveness in both learning and inference stages. Extensive experiments on latent-space image transmission demonstrate that the JSCGC consistently improves feature-based, semantic-level, and distributional quality across diverse channel conditions, while exhibiting a distinct error behavior characterized by semantic inconsistency rather than distortion.

AI.tldr: Proposes JSCGC, a generative communication paradigm replacing conventional decoders with generative models to maximize mutual information under perceptual constraints, improving semantic and distributional quality over traditional JSCC.
AI.motivation: Conventional communication systems rely on generic distortion metrics that fail to capture human visual perception, leading to blurred or unrealistic reconstructions.
AI.method: Replaces conventional decoder with a generative model at the receiver, treating received signal as a condition for controlled sampling into learned conditional distribution, reformulating communication from distortion minimization to mutual information maximization under perceptual constraints, with unified joint training and stochastic sampling.
AI.result: JSCGC consistently improves feature-based, semantic-level, and distributional quality across diverse channel conditions, with error behavior characterized by semantic inconsistency rather than distortion.
AI.conclusion: Generative communication paradigm (JSCGC) outperforms conventional JSCC in perceptual quality, suggesting a shift from distortion minimization to controlled generation for communication systems.

title: Pinching-Antenna Enabled Multicell Wireless Systems
summary: Pinching antenna (PA) systems have recently emerged as a promising flexible-antenna technology, which can reconstruct the wireless propagation environment by dynamically adjusting the positions of pinching elements along dielectric waveguides, thereby providing new spatial degrees of freedom (DoFs) for enhancing wireless system performance. This paper investigates a multi-waveguide PA-based multi-cell communication system, focusing on the joint optimization of precoding matrices, waveguide power allocation, and antenna placement to maximize the weighted sum rate (WSR). In multi-cell scenarios, inter-cell interference typically leads to a highly coupled and nonconvex WSR maximization problem. To address this challenge, an efficient alternating optimization framework is adopted to optimize each variable in an iterative way. Specifically, fractional programming is first employed to reformulate the original problem by introducing auxiliary variables that decouple the signal and interference terms. Based on this reformulation, block coordinate descent is then applied to optimize the precoding matrices and power allocation, leading to closed-form or semi-closed-form updates. For the high-dimensional and nonconvex PA placement problem, particle swarm optimization (PSO) is utilized to perform an efficient search and improve scalability. Numerical results demonstrate that, under various system configurations, the proposed scheme significantly outperforms baseline methods, including average power allocation, fixed antenna placement, conventional multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO), and massive MIMO. These results highlight the strong potential of PA systems for large-scale multi-cell wireless communications.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an alternating optimization framework for multi-waveguide pinching antenna (PA) systems in multi-cell communications, jointly optimizing precoding matrices, waveguide power allocation, and antenna placement to maximize weighted sum rate (WSR), using fractional programming, block coordinate descent, and particle swarm optimization (PSO). Numerical results show significant outperformance over baselines like massive MIMO.
AI.motivation: Pinching antenna (PA) systems offer new spatial degrees of freedom by dynamically adjusting antenna positions along dielectric waveguides, but multi-cell interference makes WSR maximization highly coupled and nonconvex. The paper aims to address this optimization challenge to unlock PA potential in large-scale multi-cell communications.
AI.method: An alternating optimization framework is used: (1) fractional programming introduces auxiliary variables to decouple signal and interference; (2) block coordinate descent optimizes precoding matrices and power allocation with closed-form/semi-closed-form updates; (3) particle swarm optimization (PSO) handles the high-dimensional, nonconvex PA placement problem.
AI.result: Under various system configurations, the proposed scheme significantly outperforms baseline methods: average power allocation, fixed antenna placement, conventional MIMO, and massive MIMO.
AI.conclusion: PA systems demonstrate strong potential for large-scale multi-cell wireless communications, with the proposed optimization framework providing substantial performance gains.

title: Chirp Parameter Optimization and Distributed Detection for Cooperative RSMA-AFDM Systems
comment: This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: Affine frequency division multiplexing (AFDM) exhibits excellent Doppler robustness and the ability to characterize doubly selective channels. However, its signal dispersion characteristics make it challenging to directly adopt traditional time-frequency multiple access schemes. To address this issue, we introduce cooperative rate splitting multiple access (RSMA) for AFDM systems. The flexible configuration of AFDM chirp parameters can reduce the correlation between users' equivalent channels, which decreases the interference from RSMA private streams. We conduct a theoretical analysis of the cooperative RSMA-AFDM system and demonstrate that minimizing the overlap in the channel column spaces among users can effectively enhance the system performance. Guided by this analysis, we design a chirp parameter optimization scheme that reduces multi-user interference and maximizes diversity gain. To fully exploit the diversity gain brought by the proposed chirp parameter optimization, two expectation propagation (EP)-based distributed cooperative detection schemes are proposed. First, a decision-fusion-based method is developed, where local information and cooperative information are fused by maximum ratio combining, achieving a globally consistent estimate of the common stream. Second, we develop a belief-consensus EP-based detection scheme. In each iteration, user nodes exchange and fuse the first- and second-order statistics of the common stream, and the resulting beliefs gradually converge to a consistent global decision, which significantly improves the overall reliability.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes cooperative rate splitting multiple access (RSMA) for AFDM systems, including chirp parameter optimization and two EP-based distributed detection schemes to reduce multi-user interference and improve reliability.
AI.motivation: AFDM's signal dispersion characteristics make it difficult to directly adopt traditional time-frequency multiple access schemes, motivating the need for a new multiple access approach.
AI.method: The authors introduce cooperative RSMA for AFDM, theoretically analyze that minimizing channel column space overlap improves performance, design a chirp parameter optimization scheme, and propose two EP-based distributed cooperative detection methods: decision-fusion-based and belief-consensus EP-based detection.
AI.result: Minimizing overlap in channel column spaces among users effectively enhances system performance; the chirp parameter optimization reduces multi-user interference and maximizes diversity gain; both EP-based detection schemes achieve globally consistent estimates of the common stream.
AI.conclusion: Cooperative RSMA-AFDM with optimized chirp parameters and EP-based detection significantly improves overall reliability in doubly selective channels.

title: Acquisition state behaves as a structured, measurable variable governing lung-nodule AI: kernel-driven measurement instability and noise-driven detection fragility, invisible to DICOM metadata
summary: AI governance for medical imaging is formalizing: the 2026 ACR-SIIM Practice Parameter recommends local acceptance testing and ongoing drift monitoring, and the ACR Assess-AI registry monitors AI outputs using DICOM metadata for context. We argue that a necessary, currently unmonitored layer sits beneath output metrics: whether incoming studies remain within the acquisition envelope a model was validated on. Using a LUNA16-trained MONAI RetinaNet lung-nodule detector, we test whether acquisition state behaves as a structured, measurable variable. On real paired CT differing only in reconstruction kernel (NLST B30f vs B80f), kernel alone shifted AI-measured diameter and flipped a Fleischner size category in 5.2% (8 of 155) of nodules at fixed patient and acquisition, while detection confidence was unchanged (Wilcoxon p=0.22). Under controlled LIDC-IDRI perturbations the effects dissociated by axis: the noise axis degraded detection confidence (p=5.9e-32, concentrated in nodules under 6 mm) but not measurement, while the frequency/kernel axis corrupted measurement (p=8.6e-13) but not detection. A 4-feature pixel fingerprint recovered reconstruction identity (patient-level AUC about 0.95 on real CT, 0.995 on a QIBA phantom) where the ConvolutionKernel DICOM tag was uninformative (identical labels across reconstructions). The kernel axis transported across four manufacturers (leave-one-vendor-out AUC 0.94-0.98, matching the within-vendor ceiling). Acquisition state thus maps to distinct AI failure modes, frequency content to measurement reliability and noise to detection sensitivity, and is not recoverable from metadata. Acquisition-aware, input-side validation is the missing layer for the acceptance-testing and drift-monitoring requirements now entering imaging-AI accreditation.

AI.tldr: This paper argues that current AI governance for medical imaging overlooks a critical layer: whether incoming studies match the acquisition conditions (e.g., reconstruction kernel, noise) under which an AI model was validated. Using a lung-nodule detector, the authors show that acquisition state measurably affects AI outputs (e.g., kernel shifts can flip clinical size categories), can be inferred from pixel fingerprints even when DICOM metadata is uninformative, and maps to distinct failure modes (noise affects detection, frequency content affects measurement). They propose acquisition-aware input-side validation as a necessary addition to acceptance testing and drift monitoring.
AI.motivation: Current AI governance frameworks (e.g., ACR-SIIM Practice Parameter, ACR Assess-AI registry) focus on monitoring output metrics and metadata, but do not check whether incoming imaging studies fall within the acquisition envelope the AI model was validated on. This gap could lead to silent failures when acquisition parameters differ from training conditions.
AI.method: Using a LUNA16-trained MONAI RetinaNet lung-nodule detector, the authors test acquisition state as a structured, measurable variable via: (1) real paired CT scans differing only in reconstruction kernel (B30f vs B80f), (2) controlled perturbations on LIDC-IDRI data along noise and frequency/kernel axes, and (3) pixel-fingerprint-based recovery of reconstruction identity across four manufacturers.
AI.result: Kernel shift alone flipped a Fleischner size category in 5.2% of nodules without changing detection confidence. Controlled perturbations showed dissociation: noise degraded detection (especially for small nodules) but not measurement; frequency/kernel corrupted measurement but not detection. A 4-feature pixel fingerprint recovered reconstruction identity (AUC ~0.95 on real CT, 0.995 on phantom) even when DICOM ConvolutionKernel tags were identical. Kernel axis generalized across four manufacturers (leave-one-vendor-out AUC 0.94-0.98).
AI.conclusion: Acquisition state is a measurable variable that maps to distinct AI failure modes and cannot be recovered from metadata alone. Input-side, acquisition-aware validation is a necessary missing layer for acceptance testing and drift monitoring in imaging-AI accreditation.

title: Learning to Assist: Collaborative VLAs for Implicit Human-Robot Collaboration
summary: Human-robot collaboration (HRC) combines the complementary strengths of humans and robots to improve task efficiency. However, many existing collaborative systems rely on hand-engineered pipelines, limiting their scalability and flexibility for new tasks. In this work, we show that models trained end-to-end with imitation learning, specifically vision-language-action (VLA) models, can support collaborative manipulation, and characterize the key factors affecting their real-world performance. We evaluate two state-of-the-art models and identify a failure mode of action-chunking policies in implicit HRC, where demonstration action leakage (i.e., action chunks crossing latent task transitions) can cause premature assistive behavior. We find that this issue increases with longer execution horizons and occurs in real-world collaborative VLA systems, such as when a robot attempts to hand over a tool before the person is ready. We propose an inference-time steering method to mitigate these erroneous assistive actions while preserving policy performance. Finally, through a 16-participant user study on a long-horizon collaborative assembly task, we show that steering enables a longer execution horizon while mitigating premature assistance, leading to faster collaboration and fewer failures compared to a shorter-horizon policy.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that VLA models trained with imitation learning can enable human-robot collaboration, identifies "demonstration action leakage" as a key failure mode in action-chunking policies (premature assistive behavior), and proposes an inference-time steering method to mitigate it. A 16-participant user study on long-horizon assembly tasks demonstrates that steering enables longer execution horizons with faster collaboration and fewer failures.
AI.motivation: Existing HRC systems rely on hand-engineered pipelines that limit scalability and flexibility. The authors aim to show that end-to-end learned VLA models can support collaborative manipulation and identify key factors affecting real-world performance.
AI.method: Evaluated two state-of-the-art VLA models with imitation learning; identified "demonstration action leakage" (action chunks crossing latent task transitions) causing premature assistive behavior; proposed an inference-time steering method to mitigate erroneous actions; conducted a 16-participant user study on a long-horizon collaborative assembly task.
AI.result: Action leakage increases with longer execution horizons and causes premature assistive behavior (e.g., handing over a tool before the person is ready). The proposed steering method mitigates these errors while preserving policy performance. The user study showed steering enables longer horizons, faster collaboration, and fewer failures compared to shorter-horizon policies.
AI.conclusion: End-to-end VLA models can support HRC, but action-chunking policies have a failure mode in implicit collaboration. The proposed inference-time steering effectively addresses premature assistance, enabling more practical long-horizon human-robot collaboration.

title: ToolSense: A Diagnostic Framework for Auditing Parametric Tool Knowledge in LLMs
summary: Large language models deployed as agents over large tool catalogs face a critical tool-retrieval bottleneck. As embedding-based retrieval approaches rely on compact encoders that may under-capture specialized tool semantics, parametric tool retrieval addresses this by encoding each tool as a virtual token appended to the LLM vocabulary, fine-tuned in two stages (memorization then retrieval SFT) to use the LLM as a retriever, achieving strong performance on standard ToolBench retrieval benchmarks. Yet these benchmarks use verbose, fully-specified queries, and their evaluation applies constrained decoding that restricts outputs to valid token paths, neither reveals whether the model actually understands its tools. We introduce \textbf{ToolSense}, an open-source LLM-powered diagnostic framework that takes any tool catalog as input and automatically generates three benchmarks: a Realistic Retrieval Benchmark (RRB) with queries at three ambiguity tiers, an MCQ probing benchmark, and a QA probing benchmark. Applying ToolSense to ToolBench (~47k tools) and evaluating five parametric model training configurations reveals a knowledge-retrieval dissociation: on RRB queries, several configurations collapse by ~50-64 percentage points compared to fully-specified ToolBench benchmarks, falling below the embedding-model baseline. Additionally, despite strong retrieval performance, some models score near-random on factual probes, suggesting a knowledge-retrieval dissociation. We open-source the ToolSense framework and the ToolBench diagnostic benchmarks at https://github.com/SAP/toolsense.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ToolSense, a diagnostic framework revealing that parametric tool retrieval models suffer a knowledge-retrieval dissociation: they perform well on standard benchmarks but collapse (~50-64% drop) on realistic queries and score near-random on factual probes, indicating they don't truly understand the tools they retrieve.
AI.motivation: Current tool retrieval benchmarks use verbose, fully-specified queries with constrained decoding, which fails to reveal whether models actually understand tool semantics. The authors aim to expose this gap with a more diagnostic evaluation framework.
AI.method: The authors develop ToolSense, an LLM-powered framework that automatically generates three benchmarks from any tool catalog: (1) Realistic Retrieval Benchmark (RRB) with three ambiguity tiers, (2) MCQ probing benchmark, and (3) QA probing benchmark. They evaluate five parametric model training configurations on ToolBench (~47k tools).
AI.result: Parametric models collapse by 50-64 percentage points on RRB queries compared to standard benchmarks, falling below embedding-based baselines. Some models score near-random on factual probes despite strong retrieval performance, revealing a knowledge-retrieval dissociation.
AI.conclusion: Standard benchmarks overestimate parametric tool retrieval models' capabilities. The knowledge-retrieval dissociation suggests these models memorize retrieval patterns without truly understanding tool semantics, highlighting the need for more rigorous evaluation frameworks like ToolSense.

title: Stereo Vision-Based Fall Prediction and Detection using Human Pose Estimation on the AMD Kria K26 SOM
summary:
Background and Objective: Falls among elderly people can cause serious injury and reduce quality of life. Timely prediction and detection are essential to prevent harm and support well-being. We propose a portable, low-power, battery-operated, vision-based fall prediction and detection system using HPE on an AMD Kria K26 System-on-Module (SOM). The objective is a non-intrusive, privacy-preserving system for real-time fall detection.
  Methods: The system uses an Intel RealSense D455 range-sensing camera connected to the K26 SOM by USB. It captures synchronized RGB and depth frames, 640 x 480 x 3 and 640 x 480 pixels, at 60 FPS. The SOM runs a three-stage pipeline with quantized YOLOX, Anchor-to-Joint (A2J), and fall-detection models. YOLOX identifies human bounding boxes from RGB frames, then discards the RGB frames to preserve privacy. A2J uses depth frames to estimate 15 joint keypoints per person. A CNN uses selected joint coordinates (x, y, z) to classify fall activity. YOLOX was trained on CrowdHuman; A2J on ITOP, MP-3DHP, UR Fall Detection, and a custom SDSU PSG dataset; and the CNN on UR Fall Detection and SDSU PSG. The design used a single-core DPU with a serial pipeline and a dual-core DPU running YOLOX and A2J with multiple threads.
  Results: Quantized accuracy was evaluated using IoU >= 50% for YOLOX, mAP with a 10-cm rule for A2J, and classification accuracy, (TP + TN)/(TP + TN + FP + FN), for the CNN. Accuracies were 74%, 84.13%, and 75.85%. Throughput improved from 2.5 FPS for the single-threaded pipeline to 4.5 FPS for the multi-threaded version.
  Conclusion: Results demonstrate the feasibility of privacy-preserving fall detection on an AMD Kria K26 edge device. On-device HPE and fall classification runs without cloud dependency, supporting elderly monitoring and assistive healthcare. Future work will improve model accuracy and speed.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a portable, privacy-preserving fall detection system for elderly people using an AMD Kria K26 SOM with a RealSense D455 camera. It runs a quantized pipeline (YOLOX, A2J, CNN) achieving 4.5 FPS throughput and ~75-84% accuracy on various datasets.
AI.motivation: Falls among elderly people cause serious injury and reduce quality of life. Timely prediction and detection are essential to prevent harm and support well-being. The authors aim to create a non-intrusive, privacy-preserving system that runs on low-power edge devices without cloud dependency.
AI.method: The system uses an Intel RealSense D455 camera connected via USB to an AMD Kria K26 SOM. A three-stage pipeline runs on the SOM: (1) quantized YOLOX detects human bounding boxes from RGB frames (then RGB frames are discarded for privacy), (2) Anchor-to-Joint (A2J) estimates 15 joint keypoints from depth frames, (3) a CNN classifies fall activity using joint coordinates. Single-core and dual-core DPU implementations were compared.
AI.result: YOLOX achieved 74% accuracy (IoU>=50%), A2J achieved 84.13% mAP, and the fall CNN achieved 75.85% classification accuracy. Throughput improved from 2.5 FPS (single-threaded) to 4.5 FPS (multi-threaded dual-core DPU).
AI.conclusion: The results demonstrate feasible privacy-preserving fall detection on an AMD Kria K26 edge device. On-device HPE and fall classification operate without cloud dependency, supporting elderly monitoring and assistive healthcare. Future work will focus on improving model accuracy and speed.

title: Quickest Detection of Hallucination Onset: Delay Bounds and Learned CUSUM Statistics
summary: Token-level hallucination detectors are evaluated as classifiers, by AUC over all tokens, yet a streaming monitor is judged by its reaction time: the number of tokens that pass between the onset of a hallucination and the alarm. We formulate hallucination onset detection as a quickest change detection problem. A first-order Markov model of the latent faithful/hallucinated state, validated on RAGTruth, places the task inside classical change-point theory and yields Lorden's lower bound on detection delay: about 1.3 tokens at a false-alarm rate of 0.01. We then show that a causal recurrent labeler acts as a CUSUM with a learned increment; at a matched false-alarm rate it detects in 11-13 tokens, against 31 for a linear per-token baseline, and a controlled decomposition attributes most of this advantage to a better per-token score rather than to temporal accumulation. An information-rate optimality theorem of Donsker-Varadhan type explains the remaining order-of-magnitude gap: the learned score realizes only 1/4.5 of the divergence the features carry, a deficit that recalibration cannot remove, with the remainder a finite-horizon effect. Classification metrics conceal this delay structure; sequential analysis makes it measurable

AI.tldr: This paper reframes token-level hallucination detection as a quickest change detection problem, showing that causal recurrent detectors act as learned CUSUM systems, achieving 11-13 token detection delays vs 31 for baselines, with a theoretical information-rate analysis explaining remaining gaps via divergence deficits and finite-horizon effects.
AI.motivation: Standard AUC-based evaluation of hallucination detectors ignores reaction time—the crucial delay between hallucination onset and alarm. The authors aim to properly formalize this as a change-point detection problem to enable principled evaluation and improvement of streaming monitors.
AI.method: Formulates hallucination onset detection as quickest change detection using a first-order Markov model validated on RAGTruth. Shows causal recurrent labeler acts as CUSUM with learned increment. Uses Lorden's lower bound, controlled decomposition, and Donsker-Varadhan type optimality theorem for analysis.
AI.result: Lorden's lower bound gives ~1.3 token delay at 0.01 false-alarm rate. Causal recurrent detector achieves 11-13 tokens vs 31 for linear baseline. Most advantage comes from better per-token score, not temporal accumulation. Learned score realizes only 1/4.5 of available divergence.
AI.conclusion: Classification metrics (AUC) conceal important delay structure; sequential analysis makes it measurable. There remains an order-of-magnitude gap from theoretical optimum due to divergence deficit and finite-horizon effects that recalibration cannot fix.

title: Bounds and Constructions of Maximum Toroidal Distance Codes
summary: In lattice-based cryptographic schemes, both encoded messages and accumulated decryption noise are represented in a modulo $q$ space. Therefore, it is natural to study toroidal distances and maximum toroidal distance (MTD) codes. In this paper, we derive some upper bounds for minimum toroidal distance of a code, including a Plotkin-type bound, a local ball--Plotkin bound, and a Delsarte linear programming bound. We also exhibit examples showing that these bounds are sharp in some cases. Moreover, we present several code constructions with good minimum distance, some of which are MTD codes. For $\ell=2$, we obtain a family of four-point MTD codes in $\mathbb Z_q^2$. For $\ell=4$, we propose a general code construction and exhibit several explicit instances for specific values of $q$, some of which are proven to be MTD codes. For $\ell=8$, using the $E_8$ lattice, we construct codes $C=2mE_8\cap \mathbb Z_q^8$, where $q=4m$ and show that they are MTD codes. These results give explicit optimal constructions of MTD codes for $\ell=2,4,8$. In the case $\ell=16$, we construct a code with minimum toroidal distance $3$ for $q=4$, while the known upper bound in this case is $2\sqrt{3}$. Our main tools are geometric and linear programming methods.

AI.tldr: This paper derives upper bounds (Plotkin-type, local ball-Plotkin, Delsarte LP bound) for minimum toroidal distance codes and constructs optimal MTD codes for dimensions ℓ=2,4,8 using geometric and LP methods, with partial results for ℓ=16.
AI.motivation: In lattice-based cryptography, encoded messages and decryption noise live in modulo q space, motivating the study of toroidal distances and maximum toroidal distance (MTD) codes to better understand error correction in this setting.
AI.method: The authors derive upper bounds via geometric and linear programming methods (Plotkin-type, local ball-Plotkin, Delsarte LP bound). They construct codes using explicit constructions for ℓ=2, the E8 lattice for ℓ=8, and general constructions for ℓ=4 and ℓ=16.
AI.result: For ℓ=2: family of four-point MTD codes in Z_q^2. For ℓ=4: general construction with explicit MTD instances. For ℓ=8: C=2mE8∩Z_q^8 with q=4m are MTD codes. For ℓ=16: code with minimum toroidal distance 3 for q=4 (improving over known upper bound 2√3).
AI.conclusion: The bounds are sharp in some cases, and explicit optimal MTD code constructions are achieved for ℓ=2,4,8, demonstrating the power of geometric and LP methods for toroidal distance codes.

title: Sub-array Selection Optimization for Joint Self-Interference and Multi-User Interference Suppression in FD mMIMO
summary: This paper proposes a beamforming optimization scheme with joint antenna sub-array selection (SAS) and angular perturbation-based nulling (APN) for full-duplex (FD) massive multiple-input multiple-output (mMIMO) systems, to simultaneously suppress self-interference (SI) and multi-user interference (MUI). A comprehensive over-the-air SI channel measurement campaign, conducted with an 8x8Tx-8x8Rx FD array prototype, reveals significant variations across sub-arrays at different spatial locations, as well as reconfigurable characteristics of the SI channel under diverse Tx and Rx sub-array configurations. To exploit the selective SI channels, a particle swarm optimization (PSO)-based algorithm is developed to jointly determine optimal sub-array indices and perturbed steering angles, thereby effectively nullifying potential interference. Selecting sub-arrays with inherently lower SI channels notably enhances the beam-level isolation, while the added selection flexibility among comparable SI channels ensures more uniform SI suppression across diverse DL/UL locations and significantly improves worst-case isolation. Experimental evaluation based on the measured SI channel demonstrates that the proposed SAS technique achieves residual Tx-Rx beam-level SI suppression improvements of 29.2 dB and 26.6 dB for the sample 1x2 and 1x4 sub-arrays, respectively. A worst-case improvement greater than 30.7 dB is observed. Overall, the joint SAS and APN optimization scheme achieves average beam-level isolation of 85.2 dB and 83.3 dB with the 1x2 and 1x4 sub-arrays, respectively. With the application of a baseband precoder, all tested sub-array configurations achieve average MUI suppression better than -181.3 dB. These results confirm the potential of the proposed optimization algorithm to successfully reduce interference to the noise floor, thereby guaranteeing reliable FD mMIMO operation.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a joint sub-array selection and angular nulling optimization for full-duplex massive MIMO, using measured SI channels and PSO to achieve significant self-interference and multi-user interference suppression.
AI.motivation: To simultaneously suppress self-interference (SI) and multi-user interference (MUI) in full-duplex massive MIMO systems, leveraging the observation that SI channels vary significantly across different sub-array configurations.
AI.method: A particle swarm optimization (PSO)-based algorithm jointly selects optimal Tx/Rx sub-array indices and perturbed steering angles (angular perturbation-based nulling, APN), exploiting measured SI channel characteristics from an 8x8Tx-8x8Rx FD array prototype.
AI.result: Residual SI suppression improved by 29.2 dB (1x2) and 26.6 dB (1x4); worst-case improvement >30.7 dB; average beam-level isolation of 85.2 dB (1x2) and 83.3 dB (1x4); MUI suppression better than -181.3 dB with baseband precoder.
AI.conclusion: The joint SAS and APN scheme can reduce interference to the noise floor, guaranteeing reliable FD mMIMO operation.

title: JOMP: Jointly-Optimized Mixed-Precision Quantization Across Neural Video Coding Frameworks and Buffering Strategies
summary: Variational autoencoder-based neural video coding has demonstrated impressive rate-distortion performance. However, its adoption in real-world applications remains hindered by challenges, such as prohibitively high computational complexity and limited cross-platform interoperability. These issues are often overlooked, as most neural video codecs rely on floating-point arithmetic to fully explore their rate-distortion potential. Practical deployment, however, requires integer-based implementations. Converting floating-point implementations into integer-based networks is non-trivial, since it involves quantizing inter-dependent coding components, whose sensitivity to precision may vary across codec designs. This paper introduces a Jointly-Optimized Mixed-Precision (JOMP) framework, in which both quantization parameters and bit widths are treated as learnable variables during training. This enables different codec modules to operate at varying precision levels, thereby jointly optimizing the rate-distortion-complexity trade-off. To the best of our knowledge, JOMP is the first mixed-precision quantization framework for neural video codecs. Its effectiveness is validated through a systematic investigation of quantization across different coding frameworks and temporal buffering strategies. Our study marks the first attempt to a unified understanding of the combined effects of modern coding frameworks and temporal buffering strategies, with the aim of informing future development of neural video codecs from a practicality perspective. In addition, we develop a complete integerization pipeline to achieve deterministic decoding. Overall, when applied to our best-performing model, JOMP enables end-to-end mixed-precision learning for integer neural video codecs, achieving rate-distortion performance comparable to that of the state-of-the-art DCVC-FM while reducing bit operations by 87.6%.

AI.tldr: JOMP introduces a mixed-precision quantization framework for neural video codecs that treats both quantization parameters and bit widths as learnable variables, enabling different modules to operate at varying precision levels to jointly optimize the rate-distortion-complexity trade-off. It achieves performance comparable to DCVC-FM while reducing bit operations by 87.6%.
AI.motivation: Neural video codecs show impressive performance but face adoption challenges due to high computational complexity and limited cross-platform interoperability. Practical deployment requires integer-based implementations, but converting floating-point networks is non-trivial due to inter-dependent coding components with varying sensitivity to precision.
AI.method: The JOMP framework treats both quantization parameters and bit widths as learnable variables during training, enabling different codec modules to operate at varying precision levels. It includes a systematic investigation across different coding frameworks and temporal buffering strategies, along with a complete integerization pipeline for deterministic decoding.
AI.result: When applied to the best-performing model, JOMP achieves rate-distortion performance comparable to the state-of-the-art DCVC-FM while reducing bit operations by 87.6%. It also provides the first unified understanding of the combined effects of modern coding frameworks and temporal buffering strategies.
AI.conclusion: JOMP is the first mixed-precision quantization framework for neural video codecs, enabling end-to-end mixed-precision learning for integer neural video codecs. It demonstrates that practical, interoperable neural video coding can be achieved without sacrificing rate-distortion performance, informing future development from a practicality perspective.

title: Action-Effect Memory Pretraining for Robot Manipulation
summary: We present AEM, an Action-Effect Memory pretraining framework for robot manipulation that learns compact temporal representations from vision-action history. Unlike prior robot representation pretraining methods that mainly focus on single-frame visual encoding, AEM targets the temporal nature of manipulation, where the current observation alone is often insufficient under partial observability. AEM models manipulation as an action-driven interaction process by interleaving visual and action features and applying masked modeling to recover missing content from incomplete histories, thereby learning action-conditioned state evolution. The Mamba-encoded output of the final vision token is used as a compact history representation, serving as the global context for decoding and downstream control. This design preserves a single-vector temporal bottleneck while keeping inference efficient. We evaluate AEM with Diffusion Policy and Flow Policy. AEM consistently improves manipulation performance in both simulation and real-world settings, outperforming baselines across clean scenes, cluttered and random scenes, and non-Markovian tasks. Ablation studies further show that history-aware pretraining surpasses single-frame pretraining and direct frame stacking, while reducing inference latency and computational cost.

AI.tldr: AEM is a pretraining framework that learns compact temporal representations from vision-action history for robot manipulation, outperforming single-frame methods in simulation and real-world settings.
AI.motivation: Prior robot representation pretraining methods focus on single-frame visual encoding, which is insufficient for manipulation tasks under partial observability where temporal context is needed.
AI.method: AEM interleaves visual and action features, applies masked modeling to recover missing content from incomplete histories, and uses Mamba-encoded final vision token as a compact history representation bottleneck.
AI.result: AEM consistently improves manipulation performance with Diffusion Policy and Flow Policy in both simulation and real-world settings, outperforming baselines across clean, cluttered, random, and non-Markovian tasks.
AI.conclusion: History-aware pretraining surpasses single-frame pretraining and direct frame stacking, reducing inference latency and computational cost while improving performance.

title: Arbor: Tree Search as a Cognition Layer for Autonomous Agents
summary:
Arbor is a multi-agent framework that introduces structured tree search as a cognition layer for autonomous agents operating in large, stateful action spaces. Prior autonomous optimization systems operate on isolated targets with stateless evaluation. Arbor instead maintains an explicit search tree of scored hypotheses that serves as the shared working memory across agents, evolving with every measurement, treating failures as diagnostic signal that reshapes subsequent exploration, and expanding as prior successes shift the bottleneck distribution.
  We validate Arbor on full-stack LLM inference optimization, a domain where achieving peak performance has historically required coordinated effort from engineering teams across the application, framework, compiler, kernel, and hardware stack. Arbor pairs an Orchestrator agent, which drives optimization by delegating to Domain Specialists across the inference stack, with a Critic agent that safeguards stability through root-cause analysis, introspection, and measurement validation -- a checks-and-balances architecture where neither agent can unilaterally drive the system. Agent capabilities are decomposed into hard skills (domain expertise) and soft skills (coordination protocols that determine how contributions compose), enabling fully autonomous multi-day campaigns. Arbor achieves up to 193% inference throughput-latency Pareto improvement over vendor-optimized baselines, while a single agent without the harness plateaus at +33% throughput improvement and crashes irrecoverably within hours. Arbor generalizes to multiple generations of hardware platform, and run-to-run variance is within 2 percentage points demonstrating that the method is hardware-agnostic and reproducible.

AI.tldr: Arbor is a multi-agent framework using structured tree search for autonomous optimization of large, stateful systems. Validated on LLM inference, it achieves up to 193% Pareto improvement over vendor baselines, far outperforming single-agent approaches that plateau and crash.
AI.motivation: Prior autonomous optimization systems work on isolated targets with stateless evaluation, but real-world systems (like full-stack LLM inference) require coordinated effort across multiple interdependent layers (application, compiler, kernel, hardware). No existing system can autonomously optimize such complex, stateful action spaces.
AI.method: Arbor uses structured tree search as a shared cognition layer, maintaining an explicit search tree of scored hypotheses that serves as working memory across agents. It uses a checks-and-balances architecture with an Orchestrator (drives optimization via delegation to Domain Specialists) and a Critic (safeguards stability via root-cause analysis and measurement validation). Agent capabilities are decomposed into hard skills (domain expertise) and soft skills (coordination protocols).
AI.result: Up to 193% inference throughput-latency Pareto improvement over vendor-optimized baselines. Single-agent baseline plateaus at +33% throughput and crashes irrecoverably within hours. Generalizes across multiple hardware generations with run-to-run variance within 2 percentage points.
AI.conclusion: Arbor demonstrates that treating failures as diagnostic signal, using structured tree search as shared memory, and implementing agent checks-and-balances enables fully autonomous, multi-day optimization campaigns in complex, stateful environments. The method is hardware-agnostic and reproducible.

title: HairPort: In-context 3D-aware Hair Import and Transfer for Images
comment: Accepted to SIGGRAPH 2026 (Conference Papers Track). 23 pages, 15 figures, 10 tables, including supplementary material as appendices. Project page: https://deepmancer.github.io/HairPort/
summary: Transferring hairstyles between images is an important but challenging task in computer graphics, computer vision, and visual effects. It enables users to explore new looks without physically altering their hair, with applications in virtual try-on systems, augmented reality, and entertainment. Most prior works operate best under small pose gaps, and they fall short under large viewpoint and scale differences, where missing hair content must be synthesized rather than transferred. We propose HairPort, a 3D-aware hairstyle transfer framework that attempts to solve these issues by explicitly separating hair removal from transfer and enforcing geometric consistency before synthesis. We introduce a Bald Converter, which produces realistic bald versions of faces through LoRA-based in-context adaptation of FLUX.1 Kontext. To train our Bald Converter, we introduce a new dataset, Baldy, containing 6,000 paired bald and original images across diverse identities and conditions. We also use a 3D-Aware Transfer Pipeline that reconstructs and re-renders the reference hairstyle from the target viewpoint before compositing it onto the source image. Being 3D aware, our method supports large pose and scale discrepancies between the source and target. Finally, a conditional flow-matching generator synthesizes the transferred result from the bald source and geometry-aligned reference guidance. Together, our method enables accurate, pose-consistent, and identity-preserving hairstyle transfer, outperforming existing methods both qualitatively and quantitatively.

AI.tldr: HairPort is a 3D-aware framework for hairstyle transfer that handles large pose/viewpoint differences by separating hair removal (via a Bald Converter) from transfer (via a 3D-Aware Transfer Pipeline and conditional flow-matching generator), outperforming prior methods.
AI.motivation: Existing hairstyle transfer methods struggle under large pose and viewpoint gaps because missing hair content must be synthesized rather than transferred, limiting their applicability in virtual try-on, AR, and entertainment.
AI.method: 1) Bald Converter: produces realistic bald faces via LoRA-based in-context adaptation of FLUX.1 Kontext, trained on a new Baldy dataset (6,000 paired bald/original images). 2) 3D-Aware Transfer Pipeline: reconstructs and re-renders reference hairstyle from target viewpoint. 3) Conditional flow-matching generator: synthesizes final result from bald source and geometry-aligned guidance.
AI.result: HairPort outperforms existing methods both qualitatively and quantitatively, enabling accurate, pose-consistent, and identity-preserving hairstyle transfer across large pose/scale discrepancies.
AI.conclusion: The explicit separation of hair removal from transfer, combined with 3D-aware geometric consistency, is effective for robust hairstyle transfer under challenging conditions.

title: Boltzmann Attention: Learnable Ising Couplings for Cooperative Attention
summary: Attention mechanisms are central to modern sequence models, yet standard attention computes relevance primarily through individual query--key similarities. Although softmax normalization introduces competition among positions, a standard attention layer does not explicitly parameterize learnable interactions between attention decisions. This limits its ability to directly model cooperative or antagonistic co-attention structure within the attention mechanism itself. We propose Boltzmann attention, an energy-based generalization in which attention patterns are governed by an interacting Ising model. The method augments the usual data-dependent local fields with learnable pairwise couplings, allowing the model to represent inter-position correlations beyond those captured by softmax or sigmoid attention. Experiments on character-level language modeling and synthetic bracket matching show that Boltzmann attention consistently improves over standard softmax attention within a standard Transformer architecture, with the advantage becoming more pronounced as sequence length increases. A four-way ablation confirms that the improvement arises from the learnable pairwise couplings. These results suggest that explicit inter-position interactions provide a principled enhancement for attention-based sequence modeling. Moreover, the Ising formulation opens a natural path toward quantum-computing-based sampling strategies: we demonstrate that diabatic quantum annealing provides a practical training method while maintaining competitive performance with exact Boltzmann computation.

AI.tldr: Boltzmann attention replaces standard softmax attention with an Ising model that includes learnable pairwise couplings between positions, improving performance on language modeling and bracket matching, especially for longer sequences, and can be trained via quantum annealing.
AI.motivation: Standard attention lacks explicit parameterization of interactions between attention decisions, limiting its ability to model cooperative or antagonistic co-attention structures within the mechanism itself.
AI.method: Proposes Boltzmann attention, an energy-based model using an interacting Ising model that augments data-dependent local fields with learnable pairwise couplings to capture inter-position correlations.
AI.result: Boltzmann attention consistently outperforms standard softmax attention in character-level language modeling and synthetic bracket matching, with greater advantage at longer sequences; a four-way ablation confirms the improvement stems from learnable pairwise couplings.
AI.conclusion: Explicit inter-position interactions provide a principled enhancement for attention-based sequence modeling, and the Ising formulation enables quantum-computing-based sampling via diabatic quantum annealing.

title: Technical Supplement Report on Full-Duplex FBMC/QAM MIMO Systems: Transceiver Design and Optimization
summary: This technical report presents the design and analysis of filter bank multicarrier (FBMC)/QAM multi-user MISO systems. We describe the complete uplink and downlink signal processing chains and characterize the end-to-end effective channel, including inter-carrier interference, inter-symbol interference, intrinsic interference, and residual self-interference. We compare FBMC/QAM with CP-OFDM and FBMC/OQAM through the lens of the Balian-Low theorem, and analyze prototype filter choices (PHYDYAS, Type-I, and Type-II), including the interference power breakdown under MRT and ZF precoding. Furthermore, we present an online stochastic successive convex approximation framework for ergodic sum-rate maximization with closed-form power updates, and contrast it with offline Monte Carlo-based approaches. Simulation results demonstrate the BER and network spectral efficiency advantages of FBMC/QAM over CP-OFDM under residual carrier frequency offset.

AI.tldr: This report analyzes FBMC/QAM multi-user MISO systems, comparing them with CP-OFDM and FBMC/OQAM under the Balian-Low theorem, and proposes an online stochastic optimization framework for sum-rate maximization.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitations of CP-OFDM (e.g., sensitivity to carrier frequency offset) and FBMC/OQAM (e.g., intrinsic interference), by designing and analyzing FBMC/QAM as a more robust multi-carrier modulation for multi-user MISO systems.
AI.method: Complete uplink/downlink signal processing chains are described, end-to-end effective channel characterization including various interference types is provided. An online stochastic successive convex approximation framework with closed-form power updates is proposed for ergodic sum-rate maximization.
AI.result: FBMC/QAM achieves BER and network spectral efficiency advantages over CP-OFDM under residual carrier frequency offset.
AI.conclusion: FBMC/QAM is a promising candidate for multi-user MISO systems, offering robustness to CFO while maintaining good spectral efficiency, supported by both theoretical analysis (Balian-Low theorem) and simulation results.

title: Active Perception for Radio Map Reconstruction in Uncharted 3D Air-Ground Environments
summary: Radio maps provide the essential foundation for low altitude networking systems. Unlike terrestrial radio maps that are typically generated via drive test measurements, mapping the air-ground environment requires the deployment of unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs). This shift introduces two formidable challenges in uncharted 3D scenarios. First, sparse radio measurements and incomplete geometric observations hinder accurate reconstruction. Second, the large 3D action space and strict power constraints from high spectrum scanner energy consumption make informative exploration difficult. To address these issues, this paper proposes 3D uncertainty aware radio active mapping (3D-URAM), a closed loop active perception framework that decouples the mapping process into two offline trained stages. In Stage I, a Bayesian UNet is developed to recover radio maps from sparse measurements and partial geometry while providing calibrated predictive uncertainty. In Stage II, a dynamic probabilistic roadmap and a transformer based waypoint selection policy trained via proximal policy optimization maximize long horizon uncertainty reduction under travel budgets. Experimental results demonstrate that 3D-URAM reduces reconstruction error by over 50% compared to representative baselines. Real-world field tests within a 300mx200mx100m space also validate the potential of active radio map reconstruction.

AI.tldr: 3D-URAM is a closed-loop active perception framework for air-ground radio map reconstruction, using Bayesian UNet for uncertainty-aware sparse-to-dense mapping and a transformer-based PPO policy for informative UAV path planning, achieving over 50% reduction in reconstruction error.
AI.motivation: Air-ground radio mapping requires UAVs instead of drive tests, introducing challenges of sparse measurements with incomplete geometry and large 3D action spaces with strict power constraints, which existing methods cannot handle well.
AI.method: A two-stage closed-loop framework: Stage I uses Bayesian UNet to recover radio maps from sparse measurements and partial geometry with calibrated predictive uncertainty; Stage II uses a dynamic probabilistic roadmap with transformer-based waypoint selection trained via PPO to maximize long-horizon uncertainty reduction under travel budgets.
AI.result: Reduces reconstruction error by over 50% compared to baselines; validated in real-world field tests within a 300mx200mx100m space.
AI.conclusion: The proposed 3D-URAM effectively addresses sparse measurement and exploration challenges in 3D air-ground radio mapping, demonstrating significant improvement and real-world feasibility.

title: Fed-FBD: Federated Functional Block Diversification for Isolation, Privacy, and Surgical Unlearning
summary: Federated learning (FL) enables collaborative model training without sharing raw patient data, but standard approaches such as FedAvg treat each client as a black box and provide no mechanism for isolating an adversarial contributor, auditing per-client influence, or honoring a departed participant's right to be forgotten. We present Fed-FBD (Federated Functional Block Diversification), a modular federated architecture that decomposes a ResNet backbone into six functional blocks (the stem, four residual groups, and the classification head) and maintains a warehouse of N color variants, each assembled from independently tracked and contributor-stamped blocks. Fed-FBD provides three capabilities absent in FedAvg: (i) architecturally guaranteed block-level isolation, so that an adversarial or mislabelled client cannot contaminate the clean colous; (ii) privacy-by-design, where membership inference advantage is already indistinguishable from chance before any privacy mechanism is applied; and (iii) surgical machine unlearning of a departed participant's contribution at sub-second cost and without retraining. Experiments on six MedMNIST-2D datasets, PathMNIST at 224x224, and CIFAR-10 show that Fed-FBD trades a modest 0.3%-3.1% IID accuracy gap on the adequately sized datasets for these guarantees, remains within 0.8%-4.0% of FedAvg at Dirichlet alpha=1.0 on three of four datasets, and confines all six adversarial attacks we study to the poisoned client's own blocks with at most +/-0.01 AUC drift on the clean colors.

AI.tldr: Fed-FBD is a modular federated learning architecture that decomposes ResNet into six functional blocks with contributor-stamped tracking, enabling block-level adversarial isolation, privacy-by-design (membership inference indistinguishable from chance), and sub-second surgical machine unlearning, with only 0.3%-3.1% accuracy loss on IID data and effective containment of adversarial attacks.
AI.motivation: Standard FL (e.g., FedAvg) treats clients as black boxes, lacking mechanisms for isolating adversarial contributors, auditing per-client influence, or supporting the right to be forgotten.
AI.method: Decomposes ResNet backbone into six functional blocks (stem, four residual groups, classification head), maintains a warehouse of N color variants assembled from independently tracked and contributor-stamped blocks.
AI.result: - 0.3%-3.1% IID accuracy gap vs FedAvg on adequate datasets; within 0.8%-4.0% of FedAvg at Dirichlet alpha=1.0 on 3/4 datasets; all adversarial attacks confined to poisoned blocks with ≤ ±0.01 AUC drift on clean colors; sub-second unlearning.
AI.conclusion: Fed-FBD provides architectural guarantees for security, privacy, and unlearning in FL with minimal accuracy trade-offs, addressing critical limitations of standard approaches.

title: Foresight: Iterative Reasoning About Clues that Matter for Navigation
summary: Open-world mapless navigation from sparse language instructions requires resolving underspecified goals and inferring which environmental cues are relevant for reaching the goal. For instance, reaching an out-of-view destination may require interpreting ramps, signs, or detours that reveal where to go or which route to take. Prior works are limited by their reliance on known navigation factors and closed-set factor categories, or identify cues before motion planning and miss plan-dependent cues. We argue that pretrained Vision-Language Models (VLMs) can discover novel instruction-relevant cues, but require adaptation to focus on which cues matter and how they should influence motion planning. We realize these ideas in Foresight, a test-time framework in which a finetuned VLM alternates between proposing image-space motion plans and critiquing them using the language goal and visual context. Subsequent plans are conditioned on prior critiques, enabling iterative motion refinement before execution. To align plan critiques and refinements with open-set behavior preferences, we learn a reward model from human feedback and use it to post-train the VLM with reinforcement learning in the plan-critique loop. In offline evaluations and 6 real-world environments, Foresight improves average task success by 37% and reduces interventions per mission by 52% relative to state-of-the-art test-time reasoning and foundation-model baselines, while running in real-time on a Jetson AGX Orin. We will release code, data, and training details to support future work on test-time reasoning for robot motion refinement. Additional videos at: https://amrl.cs.utexas.edu/foresight

AI.tldr: Foresight is a test-time framework using finetuned VLMs that iteratively propose and critique motion plans from language instructions, improving robot navigation by 37% task success and reducing interventions by 52% over baselines in real-world environments.
AI.motivation: Prior works are limited by reliance on known navigation factors, closed-set categories, or identifying cues before motion planning (missing plan-dependent cues). Open-world mapless navigation from sparse language instructions requires resolving underspecified goals and inferring relevant environmental cues.
AI.method: A finetuned VLM alternates between proposing image-space motion plans and critiquing them using language goals and visual context. A reward model is learned from human feedback and used to post-train the VLM with reinforcement learning in the plan-critique loop for iterative motion refinement before execution.
AI.result: Foresight improves average task success by 37% and reduces interventions per mission by 52% relative to state-of-the-art test-time reasoning and foundation-model baselines, running in real-time on a Jetson AGX Orin.
AI.conclusion: VLMs can discover novel instruction-relevant cues but require adaptation to focus on cues and their influence on motion planning. The iterative plan-critique framework effectively aligns robot behavior with open-set preferences for real-world mapless navigation.

title: Strategic Decision Support for AI Agents
summary: Traditionally, decision support studies how humans use machine learning models to make better decisions. In modern agentic systems, this division of roles is increasingly reversed: AI agents act on behalf of users, while humans and tools becomes support mechanisms around them. This role reversal brings reliability concerns to the forefront, since agentic errors can be consequential and agent behavior must remain aligned with human goals and constraints. Departing from the classical view of decision support, we revisit its two basic principles, the cost--value tradeoff of seeking support and the role of uncertainty quantification, in a setting where AI agents are the central actors. We propose a framework for strategic decision support for AI agents through an optimization problem that minimizes support usage subject to controlling a counterfactual missed-support error: the probability that the agent acts alone on instances where support would have materially improved its output. At the population level, we show that the optimal policy is a threshold rule on the value of support. Building on this structure, we develop an online algorithm that adaptively thresholds such a score and uses randomized exploration to control missed-support error without distributional assumptions. We further introduce a calibration-on-the-fly method that reduces unnecessary support calls online. We instantiate this framework across diverse scenarios, including information gathering, human--AI collaboration, and tool use, showing how each can be modeled through the same strategic decision-support lens. Experiments across these settings show that our method reliably controls the target error while substantially reducing support usage in practice.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a framework for strategic decision support in AI agent systems, reversing the traditional human-AI role: AI agents act on behalf of users while humans/tools provide support. The authors formulate an optimization problem minimizing support usage while controlling counterfactual missed-support error, develop an online adaptive thresholding algorithm with randomized exploration, and introduce a calibration-on-the-fly method. Experiments across information gathering, human-AI collaboration, and tool use show reliable error control with reduced support usage.
AI.motivation: Traditional decision support studies how humans use ML models. In modern agentic systems, the roles are reversed: AI agents act for users, with humans/tools as support. This reversal raises reliability concerns about agentic errors and goal alignment. The paper revisits cost-value tradeoffs and uncertainty quantification under this new paradigm.
AI.method: The framework minimizes support usage subject to controlling counterfactual missed-support error (probability the agent acts alone when support would improve output). The optimal policy at population level is a threshold rule on support value. The authors develop an online algorithm that adaptively thresholds this score with randomized exploration, plus a calibration-on-the-fly method to reduce unnecessary support calls.
AI.result: The method reliably controls the target error while substantially reducing support usage in practice across diverse scenarios: information gathering, human-AI collaboration, and tool use.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully demonstrates that the classical decision support framework can be inverted and formalized for modern AI agent systems, offering principled optimization-based support allocation with theoretical guarantees and practical effectiveness.

title: High-Fidelity Two-Step Image Generation via Teacher-Aligned End-to-End Distillation
summary: Few-step diffusion distillation has become increasingly mature for 4-8-step generation, yet pushing further to 2 steps remains challenging. In this work, we introduce Z-Image Turbo++, a high-quality 2-step image generation model distilled from the 8-step Z-Image Turbo teacher. Our method addresses the central bottlenecks of increased task difficulty and limited model capacity in 2-step generation through three simple but effective design choices tailored to this regime. First, we propose Distribution-Aligned Adversarial Learning, which uses teacher-generated images rather than external real images as real samples for GAN training, providing a more attainable and informative adversarial target. Second, we adopt Step-Decoupled Parameterization, assigning independent model parameters to the two denoising steps to better match their distinct capacity demands. Third, we perform End-to-End Training with Iterative Regularization, allowing the first step to receive gradients from final image quality while preserving a meaningful intermediate generation through an explicit step-1 loss. Together, these designs substantially narrow the quality gap between 2-step and 8-step generation in both qualitative and quantitative evaluations, highlighting the potential of carefully tailored distillation strategies for improving the quality-efficiency trade-off in few-step generation.

AI.tldr: Z-Image Turbo++ is a 2-step image generation model distilled from an 8-step teacher, achieving near-8-step quality via three innovations: Distribution-Aligned Adversarial Learning, Step-Decoupled Parameterization, and End-to-End Training with Iterative Regularization.
AI.motivation: While few-step diffusion distillation works well for 4-8 steps, pushing to 2 steps remains challenging due to increased task difficulty and limited model capacity. The authors aim to close this quality gap.
AI.method: (1) Distribution-Aligned Adversarial Learning: uses teacher-generated images (not real images) as GAN real samples; (2) Step-Decoupled Parameterization: assigns separate model parameters for each of the two denoising steps; (3) End-to-End Training with Iterative Regularization: enables first-step gradients from final image quality while preserving meaningful intermediate generation via explicit step-1 loss.
AI.result: The method substantially narrows the quality gap between 2-step and 8-step generation in both qualitative and quantitative evaluations.
AI.conclusion: Carefully tailored distillation strategies can significantly improve the quality-efficiency trade-off in few-step generation, highlighting the potential of 2-step synthesis.

title: ReCal: Reward Calibration for RL-based LLM Routing
summary: Large language model (LLM) routing has emerged as an effective paradigm for leveraging the complementary strengths of multiple LLMs through dynamic model and reasoning-strategy selection. Recent reinforcement learning (RL)-based routing methods further improve routing quality by optimizing routing policies from interaction feedback. However, they still struggle to provide informative and comparable learning signals under heterogeneous tasks with varying difficulty. In practice, multiple objectives (e.g., correctness, format behavior) are aggregated into a single scalar reward, leading to ambiguous credit assignment and conflicting optimization signals. Moreover, reward signals exhibit significant variability across instances, where some instances produce higher or more variable rewards, introducing optimization bias that favors trivial samples over informative ones. To address these issues, we propose \textbf{ReCal}, a \textbf{\underline{Re}}ward \textbf{\underline{Cal}}ibration framework for RL-based LLM routing. We first introduce a hierarchical reward decomposition mechanism with component-wise advantage estimation. We further propose a distribution-aware optimization strategy that calibrates optimization variability through variance-aware reweighting and per-dataset normalization. Experiments on seven datasets demonstrate that ReCal consistently improves routing performance, and training stability over baselines. Code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/ReCal.

AI.tldr: ReCal is a reward calibration framework for RL-based LLM routing that decomposes rewards hierarchically and uses distribution-aware optimization to improve routing performance and training stability.
AI.motivation: Existing RL-based routing methods struggle with heterogeneous tasks because they aggregate multiple objectives into a single scalar reward (causing ambiguous credit assignment) and suffer from high reward variability across instances (introducing optimization bias toward trivial samples).
AI.method: ReCal introduces: (1) hierarchical reward decomposition with component-wise advantage estimation for clearer credit assignment, and (2) a distribution-aware optimization strategy with variance-aware reweighting and per-dataset normalization to calibrate optimization variability.
AI.result: Experiments on seven datasets show ReCal consistently improves routing performance and training stability over baselines.
AI.conclusion: ReCal effectively addresses the reward ambiguity and variability issues in RL-based LLM routing, leading to better and more stable routing outcomes.

title: Active Sensing-assisted UAV Communications with Jittering: Framework and Performance Analysis
summary: Providing reliable communication for unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) via existing cellular networks is crucial for enabling the rapid growth of the low-altitude economy. However, UAV jittering significantly degrades communication quality due to induced beam misalignment. Inspired by recent advances in integrated sensing and communication, we propose a novel two-stage active sensing-assisted communication framework tailored for ground-to-UAV links with jittering. Specifically, two schemes are conceived to leverage sensing for enhancing communication performance, namely the communication-oriented scheme and the sensing-oriented scheme. For the sensing-oriented scheme, deterministic signals are employed in the first stage to facilitate angle-of-arrival (AoA) acquisition at the UAV side, followed by pure communication service in the second stage by using the estimated AoA. In contrast, the communication-oriented scheme employs Gaussian information-bearing signals throughout both stages, with AoA estimation relying on Gaussian random signals. For both schemes, we provide maximum likelihood estimators for AoA, along with analytical results characterizing the Cramér-Rao bound. To capture the performance limit, closed-form expressions for the achievable rates of the two schemes are derived, unveiling a fundamental tradeoff between sensing and communication quality across the two stages by tuning the time allocated to the first stage. The optimal time allocation that maximizes the overall rate is obtained in semi-closed-form. Based on these results, we unveil a sufficient condition under which the communication-oriented scheme outperforms the sensing-oriented scheme, which admits an interesting threshold-based structure. Asymptotic analysis demonstrates that the performance loss of the proposed schemes relative to the jitter-free upper bound approaches zero in the high transmit power regime.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes two active sensing-assisted communication schemes (communication-oriented and sensing-oriented) for ground-to-UAV links with jittering, analyzes their Cramér-Rao bounds and achievable rates, derives optimal time allocation between sensing and communication stages, and establishes a threshold-based condition for scheme selection, showing near-jitter-free performance at high transmit power.
AI.motivation: UAV jittering causes beam misalignment that degrades communication quality in cellular-connected UAVs. The paper aims to leverage integrated sensing and communication to mitigate this problem for reliable ground-to-UAV links.
AI.method: A two-stage framework is proposed: (1) Sensing-oriented scheme uses deterministic signals in stage 1 for AoA estimation, then pure communication in stage 2; (2) Communication-oriented scheme uses Gaussian signals throughout both stages for both AoA estimation and data transmission. Maximum likelihood estimators and Cramér-Rao bounds are derived for both schemes, along with achievable rate expressions and optimal time allocation.
AI.result: Closed-form achievable rate expressions are derived, revealing a fundamental sensing-communication tradeoff via time allocation. Optimal time allocation is obtained in semi-closed-form. A threshold-based sufficient condition determines when the communication-oriented scheme outperforms the sensing-oriented scheme. Performance loss approaches zero at high transmit power relative to jitter-free bounds.
AI.conclusion: The proposed active sensing-assisted framework effectively mitigates UAV jittering effects on communication, and the two schemes offer different tradeoffs suitable for different scenarios, with near-optimal performance achievable at high transmit power.

title: Rotatable Antenna-Enabled Near-Field Integrated Sensing and Communication
summary: In this paper, we propose leveraging rotatable antennas (RAs) to enhance near-field communication and sensing by exploiting a new orientation-domain spatial degree-of-freedom (DoF) provided by element-wise antenna rotation. Specifically, we investigate an RA-enabled near-field integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) system with sub-connected hybrid beamforming, where each transmit RA can independently adjust its boresight direction under a practical rotation constraint. A spherical-wave channel model incorporating orientation-dependent antenna gains is established to characterize multi-user communication and target sensing in the presence of clutters. Based on this model, a weighted communication-sensing utility maximization problem is formulated by jointly optimizing the receive beamformer, digital beamformer, analog beamformer, and RA boresight directions. To solve the resulting non-convex problem, an alternating optimization algorithm is developed by combining fractional programming, Riemannian optimization, and a spherical-cap Frank--Wolfe-based boresight update. To further understand the impact of RA rotation on near-field sensing, we derive a closed-form root Cramer--Rao bound (RCRB) expression. Simulation results demonstrate the convergence and effectiveness of the proposed algorithm. It is shown that the RA-enabled hybrid design can match or even outperform the fully-digital FPA benchmark in some regimes, indicating that the orientation-domain DoF introduced by element-wise rotation can compensate for limited RF chains. The RCRB and beampattern results further show that RA rotation improves off-broadside sensing accuracy, enhances range-domain focusing, and suppresses same-angle clutters in the near field.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes using rotatable antennas (RAs) in near-field ISAC systems to exploit orientation-domain spatial degrees-of-freedom. An alternating optimization algorithm jointly optimizes beamformers and RA boresight directions, showing that RA-enabled hybrid beamforming can match or outperform fully-digital fixed-position antenna benchmarks, while improving off-broadside sensing accuracy, range-domain focusing, and clutter suppression in the near field.
AI.motivation: To enhance near-field communication and sensing performance by exploiting a new orientation-domain spatial degree-of-freedom provided by element-wise antenna rotation, particularly to overcome limitations of fixed-position antennas and compensate for limited RF chains in hybrid beamforming architectures.
AI.method: The authors establish a spherical-wave channel model with orientation-dependent antenna gains, formulate a weighted communication-sensing utility maximization problem, and develop an alternating optimization algorithm combining fractional programming, Riemannian optimization, and a spherical-cap Frank-Wolfe-based boresight update. A closed-form root Cramér-Rao bound (RCRB) expression is derived for sensing analysis.
AI.result: The RA-enabled hybrid design matches or outperforms fully-digital FPA benchmarks in some regimes. RA rotation improves off-broadside sensing accuracy, enhances range-domain focusing, and suppresses same-angle clutters in the near field.
AI.conclusion: The orientation-domain DoF introduced by element-wise rotation can compensate for limited RF chains, making RA-enabled hybrid beamforming a viable alternative to fully-digital systems for near-field ISAC.
