Once the human's cognitive state returns to a lower cognitive load baseline, the primary agent releases the queued agent message. Our preliminary results demonstrate the feasibility of leveraging real-time signal processing, Large Language Models (LLMs), and physical robotic embodiments to create cognitively-aware, non-intrusive multi-agent systems.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a BCI-driven framework that uses EEG signals to detect human cognitive overload in real-time, causing robotic agents to defer communications until the user's mental workload decreases, enabling more natural and non-intrusive multi-agent interactions.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-agent systems often interrupt humans during high cognitive load, causing decreased task performance. There is a need for systems that can sense and respect the user's internal cognitive state.
AI.method: A closed-loop architecture using consumer-grade BCI to monitor EEG spectral bands. When high engagement is detected, an HTTP-based signaling mechanism places the primary agent in a holding state, allowing secondary agents to process tasks in the background. Once cognitive load decreases, queued messages are released.
AI.result: Preliminary results demonstrate feasibility of integrating real-time EEG signal processing, LLMs, and physical robots to create cognitively-aware, non-intrusive multi-agent systems.
AI.conclusion: Cognitively-aligned, engagement-driven deferral of agent communications is feasible using consumer-grade BCI, enabling more natural human-robot interaction without interrupting users during high mental workload.

title: DailyReport: An Open-ended Benchmark for Evaluating Search Agents on Daily Search Tasks
summary: Search Agents (SAs) typically leverage large language models (LLMs) to support complex information-seeking tasks by autonomously exploring web sources and synthesizing information into comprehensive responses. For SAs evaluation, prior benchmarks mainly focus on specialized tasks that are unlikely to arise in real-world user scenarios. Moreover, their reliance on coarse task-level rubrics often limits evaluation interpretability. To bridge this gap, we introduce DailyReport, an open-ended benchmark to evaluate SA capabilities on daily search tasks. It contains 150 open-ended tasks with 3,546 associated rubrics, capturing widely discussed and timely information demands of real-world users. Each task is decomposed into subtasks and evaluated with cascade rubrics across disentangled dimensions. Through cascade performance attribution and user-centric aggregation, we derive highly interpretable scores for each dimension, along with a user preference score. Our results on 17 agentic systems show that current systems still fall short of users' expectations. To facilitate future research, our dataset and code are made publicly available at https://github.com/AGI-Eval-Official/DailyReport.

AI.tldr: DailyReport is a new benchmark with 150 open-ended daily search tasks and 3,546 cascade rubrics for evaluating Search Agents, revealing current systems fall short of user expectations.
AI.motivation: Existing SA benchmarks focus on specialized, unrealistic tasks with coarse rubrics that limit interpretability, failing to capture real-world user information needs.
AI.method: Constructs 150 open-ended daily search tasks with 3,546 cascade rubrics, decomposed into subtasks with disentangled evaluation dimensions, using cascade performance attribution and user-centric aggregation.
AI.result: Evaluation of 17 agentic systems shows current Search Agents still fall short of user expectations.
AI.conclusion: DailyReport provides a more realistic, interpretable evaluation framework for SAs; dataset and code are publicly released to facilitate future research.

title: A Multi-Modal Framework with Cross-Subject Pseudo-Labeling and Semantic Alignment for Micro-Gesture Recognition
summary: Micro-gestures (MGs) are spontaneous and subtle body movements that frequently convey hidden human emotions. Recognizing MGs in untrimmed videos remains highly challenging due to their extremely low signal-to-noise ratio, severe long-tailed class distribution, and the inherent domain shift encountered in cross-subject evaluation scenarios. In this paper, we propose a comprehensive multi-modal framework for Track 1 of the 4th MiGA-IJCAI Challenge. To capture fine-grained representations, we design a saliency-guided multi-modal extraction pipeline integrating 68-keypoint skeleton joint coordinates, 3D heatmap volumes, and high-resolution RGB visual features. We introduce a gentle square-root smoothed weighting mechanism paired with an Orthogonal Semantic Embedding Loss to protect tail classes without compromising overall recognition capabilities. More importantly, to bridge the cross-subject generalization gap, we propose a Cross-Modal Pseudo-Labeling (CMPL) strategy for unsupervised domain adaptation, which significantly boosts single-modal robustness. A temperature-scaled soft-voting mechanism is finally utilized to alleviate overconfidence during late fusion. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our framework achieves a competitive F1-score of 68.13\%, securing the 4th place.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a multi-modal framework for micro-gesture recognition in untrimmed videos, achieving 4th place in the MiGA-IJCAI Challenge with 68.13% F1-score by using saliency-guided feature extraction, gentle square-root smoothing, Orthogonal Semantic Embedding Loss, and Cross-Modal Pseudo-Labeling for domain adaptation.
AI.motivation: Micro-gestures are subtle body movements that convey hidden emotions, but recognizing them in untrimmed videos is challenging due to low signal-to-noise ratio, long-tailed class distribution, and domain shift in cross-subject evaluation.
AI.method: (1) Saliency-guided multi-modal extraction: 68-keypoint skeleton, 3D heatmap volumes, high-resolution RGB features. (2) Gentle square-root smoothed weighting with Orthogonal Semantic Embedding Loss to protect tail classes. (3) Cross-Modal Pseudo-Labeling (CMPL) for unsupervised domain adaptation. (4) Temperature-scaled soft-voting for late fusion.
AI.result: Achieved a competitive F1-score of 68.13%, securing 4th place in Track 1 of the 4th MiGA-IJCAI Challenge.
AI.conclusion: The proposed multi-modal framework effectively addresses micro-gesture recognition challenges (noise, class imbalance, domain shift) and demonstrates strong performance through the CMPL strategy and tailored loss functions.

title: Individual Control Barrier Functions-Guided Diffusion Model for Safe Offline Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
comment: Accepted to the 23rd IFAC World Congress, 2026
summary: Offline reinforcement learning allows control policies to be learned directly from data without online interaction, making it suitable for safety-critical tasks. Recent studies have applied diffusion models to offline reinforcement learning to leverage their strong capacity for modeling complex data distributions. However, existing approaches primarily focus on single-agent settings, leaving the safety challenges in multi-agent environments largely unexplored. In this work, we propose a safe offline multi-agent reinforcement learning algorithm that embeds neural individual control barrier functions into the diffusion model to enhance safety during trajectory generation, with control policies recovered through inverse dynamics. We evaluate our algorithm across diverse benchmarks, demonstrating substantial safety improvements while maintaining competitive rewards.

AI.tldr: A safe offline multi-agent RL algorithm that integrates neural individual control barrier functions into diffusion models to enhance safety in trajectory generation, demonstrating improved safety across benchmarks while maintaining competitive rewards.
AI.motivation: Existing offline RL diffusion models focus on single-agent settings, leaving safety challenges in multi-agent environments unexplored.
AI.method: Embeds neural individual control barrier functions into the diffusion model for safe trajectory generation, with policies recovered via inverse dynamics.
AI.result: Substantial safety improvements with competitive rewards across diverse multi-agent benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach effectively addresses safety in offline multi-agent RL by combining diffusion models with control barrier functions.

title: Embedding ISO 10218 Safety Compliance in Robots via Control Barrier Functions for Human-Robot Collaboration
summary: Human-Robot Collaboration (HRC) requires strict adherence to safety standards, such as ISO 10218, to prevent harmful interactions. Standard Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM) filters calculate safe robotic speeds based on conservative assumptions, such as constant human velocity, which prevents accurate predictions of minimum separation distances and causes unnecessary operational halts. This paper proposes a Control Barrier Function (CBF) that explicitly incorporates human acceleration data to analytically forward-predict the minimum human-robot separation distance during a worst-case robotic stopping trajectory. To guarantee safety at the control level, this predictive CBF is integrated as an inequality constraint within a Sequential Quadratic Programming (SQP) framework. Specifically, two methods are proposed: Method I, a CBF-constrained PD safety filter; and Method II, a task-scaling SQP controller that enforces a spatial tube constraint. Simulated and real-world experiments on a UR10e robot evaluate the two proposed methods against a standard industrial SSM module baseline. Results demonstrate that Method II dynamically modulates execution speed and confines spatial deviations. Compared to Method I, Method II achieves a 63\% reduction in mean trajectory error and avoids excessive evasive manoeuvres, ensuring high task throughput while complying with ISO 10218 SSM guidelines.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a Control Barrier Function (CBF) that uses human acceleration data to predict minimum separation distances in Human-Robot Collaboration, integrated into an SQP framework. Two methods are evaluated: a CBF-constrained PD safety filter and a task-scaling SQP controller with spatial tube constraints. Method II achieves 63% lower trajectory error while maintaining ISO 10218 compliance.
AI.motivation: Standard Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM) filters rely on conservative assumptions like constant human velocity, which prevent accurate prediction of separation distances and cause unnecessary operational halts in HRC.
AI.method: Proposes a CBF incorporating human acceleration data to forward-predict minimum separation distance, integrated as an inequality constraint in SQP. Two methods: (I) CBF-constrained PD safety filter; (II) task-scaling SQP controller with spatial tube constraint.
AI.result: Method II achieves 63% reduction in mean trajectory error compared to Method I, avoids excessive evasive manoeuvres, maintains high task throughput, and complies with ISO 10218 SSM guidelines.
AI.conclusion: The proposed CBF-based approach with human acceleration prediction enables safer and more efficient HRC by reducing unnecessary halts and trajectory errors while adhering to safety standards.

title: HarnessBridge: Learnable Bidirectional Controller for LLM Agent Harness
summary: Large language models are increasingly deployed as agents for long-horizon tasks, yet their performance is shaped not only by model capability and environment design, but also by the harness that mediates agent--environment interaction. Existing harnesses are largely manually engineered, making them difficult to scale as trajectories grow longer and interactions become more complex. In this work, we ask whether harness can be generated by a learnable plug-in module that can be trained in an end-to-end fashion. We introduce HarnessBridge, a lightweight learnable harness controller that parameterizes the agent--environment interface as a bidirectional projection. HarnessBridge learns two bidirectional projections: observation projection, which distills raw trajectories into compact, decision-relevant states, and action projection, which converts proposed actions into executable transitions or trajectory-grounded rejections. We train HarnessBridge on a harness supervision dataset via unified instruction tuning. On Terminal-Bench~2.0 and SWE-bench Verified, HarnessBridge matches or surpasses strong specialized harnesses while substantially reducing token usage and trajectory length, and generalizes from smaller generators to larger commercial models.

AI.tldr: HarnessBridge is a learnable, lightweight plug-in module that replaces manually engineered harnesses for LLM agents. It learns bidirectional projections for observation and action to mediate agent-environment interaction, reducing token usage and trajectory length while matching or surpassing specialized harnesses on Terminal-Bench 2.0 and SWE-bench Verified.
AI.motivation: Existing harnesses that mediate agent-environment interaction are manually engineered, making them difficult to scale as trajectories grow longer and interactions become more complex. The authors ask whether such harnesses can be generated by a learnable plug-in module trained end-to-end.
AI.method: HarnessBridge learns two bidirectional projections: (1) observation projection, which distills raw trajectories into compact, decision-relevant states, and (2) action projection, which converts proposed actions into executable transitions or trajectory-grounded rejections. It is trained via unified instruction tuning on a harness supervision dataset.
AI.result: On Terminal-Bench 2.0 and SWE-bench Verified, HarnessBridge matches or surpasses strong specialized harnesses while substantially reducing token usage and trajectory length. It also generalizes from smaller generators to larger commercial models.
AI.conclusion: Learnable harness controllers like HarnessBridge can effectively replace manually engineered harnesses, improving efficiency and scalability for long-horizon agent tasks.

title: GeoCFNet: Geometry-Aware Confidence Field Network for Robot-Assisted Endoscopic Submucosal Dissection
summary: Advanced surgical robotics has made robot-assisted endoscopic submucosal dissection (ESD) a promising approach for the en-bloc resection of large lesions, with the potential to reduce recurrence and improve long-term outcomes. However, the technical complexity and risk of complications in ESD demand stable and precise visual guidance to maintain an accurate dissection corridor and a safe tissue margin. Dense confidence fields provide an effective representation for this purpose by describing both the preferred dissection region and its spatial transition to surrounding tissue. However, reliable confidence field estimation remains challenging in dynamic endoscopic scenes due to smoke, specular highlights, tissue deformation, weak texture, and the thin geometric structure of the target region. To address these challenges, we formulate dissection guidance as a geometry-aware confidence field estimation problem and propose GeoCFNet, a geometry-aware confidence field network built on a pretrained DINOv3 backbone. GeoCFNet integrates a Token-Differentiated Fusion module to aggregate class-token context with dense patch representations, a SegFormer decoder for confidence regression, and Geometry-Aware Spatial Regularization (GASR) to preserve spatial coherence and local geometric transitions. Experimental results show that GeoCFNet achieves RMSE 0.0480, PSNR 27.1995, SSIM 0.3397, and CC 0.2466, indicating accurate and geometrically stable confidence field estimation for robot-assisted ESD guidance.

AI.tldr: GeoCFNet is a geometry-aware confidence field network for robot-assisted endoscopic submucosal dissection (ESD) guidance. It uses a DINOv3 backbone with Token-Differentiated Fusion, SegFormer decoder, and Geometry-Aware Spatial Regularization to estimate dense confidence fields in challenging dynamic endoscopic scenes, achieving RMSE 0.0480, PSNR 27.1995, SSIM 0.3397, and CC 0.2466.
AI.motivation: Robot-assisted ESD requires stable, precise visual guidance for accurate dissection and safe tissue margins, but dense confidence field estimation is challenging in dynamic endoscopic scenes due to smoke, specular highlights, tissue deformation, weak texture, and thin geometric structures.
AI.method: Proposes GeoCFNet built on a pretrained DINOv3 backbone, including a Token-Differentiated Fusion module (aggregates class-token context with dense patch representations), a SegFormer decoder for confidence regression, and Geometry-Aware Spatial Regularization (GASR) for spatial coherence and local geometric transitions.
AI.result: GeoCFNet achieves RMSE 0.0480, PSNR 27.1995, SSIM 0.3397, and CC 0.2466, demonstrating accurate and geometrically stable confidence field estimation.
AI.conclusion: The geometry-aware formulation effectively addresses dynamic endoscopic challenges, providing reliable dissection guidance for robot-assisted ESD.

title: TEDD: Robust Detection of Unstable Temporal Features
summary: When working with real-world temporal data, it is common to encounter features whose distribution is changing over time. The naive employment of Machine Learning models on this unstable data might lead to rapidly degrading performance, especially if the new distribution is much different from what was previously seen during training. In order to cope with this problem, it is critical to automatically identify features that are changing over time. With these features detected, data scientists and other practitioners will be able to mitigate the issue (for instance, by applying data transformations), deploying more robust models that retain high performance for longer periods of time. In this paper, we describe which temporal changes a feature should not suffer from, and propose TEDD, a technique to a) identify when a dataset might lead to an unstable Machine Learning model and b) automatically detect which features cause such lack of robustness. In order to achieve it, we leverage a regression model to highlight which features contribute to a good prediction of an instance's timestamp. We compare our approach to other methods in real and synthetic data, testing their detection capability on all simple change patterns. We show that our method: detects all types of basic changes, both for numerical and categorical features; can detect multivariate drifts; returns a comparable value measuring the amount of change of each feature; requires no parameter tuning; and is scalable both on number of features and instances of the dataset.

AI.tldr: TEDD is a technique that automatically detects which features are changing over time in temporal data, helping prevent Machine Learning model degradation by identifying unstable features without parameter tuning.
AI.motivation: Real-world temporal data often has features whose distributions change over time, causing ML models to degrade in performance. Automatically identifying these changing features is critical for practitioners to apply mitigations and build more robust models.
AI.method: TEDD uses a regression model to predict an instance's timestamp and analyzes which features contribute most to that prediction, thereby identifying features that exhibit temporal changes (drifts). It works for both numerical and categorical features, detects multivariate drifts, and requires no parameter tuning.
AI.result: TEDD detects all types of basic change patterns for both numerical and categorical features, handles multivariate drifts, returns comparable change magnitude values, and scales well with both feature count and instance count.
AI.conclusion: TEDD is a scalable, parameter-free approach for detecting temporal feature changes in datasets, enabling practitioners to build more robust ML models that maintain high performance over time.

title: Proprioceptive-visual correspondence enables self-other distinction in humanoid robots
summary: Distinguishing self from others is a prerequisite for social intelligence, yet humanoid robots that increasingly share workspaces with humans still lack this ability. Here we show that a humanoid robot can learn self-other distinction from proprioceptive-visual correspondence, without any identity labels or kinematic models. Once established, this distinction bootstraps a predictive self-model that maps joint configurations to three-dimensional body occupancy, capturing how the robot's body changes with action. In multi-agent scenes involving humans or morphologically identical robots, the system reliably identifies itself, learns a 3D self-model, and supports downstream tasks including target reaching, collision-aware motion planning, and human-to-robot motion retargeting. Together, these results outline a route toward bodily self-representation in robots that act and coordinate alongside others in shared physical environments. Project page: https://euron-zc.github.io/humanoid-self-model/.

AI.tldr: A humanoid robot learns to distinguish itself from others using proprioceptive-visual correspondence, builds a 3D self-model, and uses it for downstream tasks like reaching, collision avoidance, and motion retargeting.
AI.motivation: Humanoid robots lack self-other distinction ability, which is essential for social intelligence when sharing workspaces with humans.
AI.method: The robot learns self-other distinction from proprioceptive-visual correspondence without identity labels or kinematic models, then bootstraps a predictive self-model that maps joint configurations to 3D body occupancy.
AI.result: The system reliably identifies itself in multi-agent scenes, learns a 3D self-model, and supports target reaching, collision-aware motion planning, and human-to-robot motion retargeting.
AI.conclusion: This work outlines a route toward bodily self-representation in robots that coordinate alongside others in shared physical environments.

title: The Hidden Power of Scaling Factor in LoRA Optimization
summary: In Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA), the scaling factor $α$ is often treated as a mere complement to the learning rate, yet its role in optimization remains poorly understood. In this paper, we reveal that the scaling factor $α$ and the learning rate function differently, with $α$ emerging as the dominant driver of effective optimization, delivering gains that cannot be replicated by learning rate scaling alone. Through the synergy of extensive empirical analysis and a theoretical Signal-Drift framework, we uncover three findings into LoRA's scaling mechanism: First, LoRA's spectral suppression smooths the optimization landscape, rendering standard hyperparameters overly conservative and creating an optimization gap. Second, when leveraging this smoothness to accelerate convergence, $α$ outperforms the learning rate by amplifying the task signal without increasing the drift ratio. Third, the optimal scaling factor follows a sublinear relationship with the rank, well characterized by a square-root law with an unexpectedly large coefficient, revealing the insufficient scaling of existing rank-tied heuristics. Based on these insights, we propose LoRA-$α$, a minimalist framework that restores $α$ to its principled regime, making LoRA compatible with standard small learning rates. Extensive evaluations across diverse tasks demonstrate that LoRA-$α$ consistently improves performance while streamlining hyperparameter search, unleashing the learning potential of LoRA.

AI.tldr: This paper reveals that LoRA's scaling factor α plays a fundamentally different and more dominant role than the learning rate in optimization, proposing a Signal-Drift framework and a new LoRA-α method that improves performance with simplified hyperparameter tuning.
AI.motivation: The scaling factor α in LoRA is poorly understood and often treated merely as a complement to the learning rate; the authors aim to clarify its distinct optimization role.
AI.method: Empirical analysis combined with a theoretical Signal-Drift framework to study LoRA's spectral suppression, convergence acceleration, and rank-scaling relationship.
AI.result: α dominates optimization over learning rate; spectral suppression creates an optimization gap; α amplifies task signals without increasing drift; optimal α follows a square-root law with rank but with a larger coefficient than previously assumed.
AI.conclusion: Proposes LoRA-α, a minimalist framework that restores α to its principled regime, enabling standard small learning rates and improving performance while simplifying hyperparameter search.

title: SAM-Deep-EIoU: Selective Mask Propagation for Multi-Object Tracking
summary: Multi-object tracking has a heavy-tailed difficulty distribution: most frames are easy for a lightweight base tracker, while a small fraction are intrinsically hard. Video object segmentation (VOS) models can often preserve identity through the hard frames where the base tracker fails, but they are much more expensive in compute and memory. We propose selective mask propagation, a tracking algorithm that dispatches from a base tracker to a VOS model only on windows where an assignment-uncertainty signal fires. The base tracker's output is modified only when the VOS model makes a confident prediction that contradicts the base tracker's identity assignment; weak or inconclusive predictions preserve the base output. The method is training-free, treats both the base tracker and the VOS model as black boxes, and can benefit from replacing the VOS component with a more capable model. On DanceTrack, selective mask propagation improves three different base trackers. On SportsMOT, where identity preservation is central to sports analytics, SAM3-Deep-EIoU with global track association achieves state-of-the-art performance on the benchmark with 86.8 HOTA.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes selective mask propagation, a method that uses a lightweight base tracker for most frames and only activates an expensive VOS model on difficult frames where identity assignment is uncertain, improving multi-object tracking efficiency and accuracy.
AI.motivation: Multi-object tracking has a heavy-tailed difficulty distribution where most frames are easy but a few are hard. Lightweight trackers fail on hard frames, while expensive VOS models preserve identity but are computationally costly.
AI.method: A selective mask propagation algorithm that dispatches a VOS model only when an assignment-uncertainty signal fires. Base tracker output is modified only when VOS makes a confident contradictory prediction; weak predictions preserve the base output. The method is training-free and treats both models as black boxes.
AI.result: On DanceTrack, the method improves three different base trackers. On SportsMOT, SAM3-Deep-EIoU with global track association achieves state-of-the-art performance with 86.8 HOTA.
AI.conclusion: Selective mask propagation effectively combines lightweight and heavy models for efficient tracking, achieving SOTA results on SportsMOT while remaining training-free and model-agnostic.

title: Physics-Aware Auxiliary Losses Improve Out-of-Distribution Generalization of a GNN Synthesizability Filter
summary:
Machine-learning drug-discovery pipelines increasingly rely on generative models that propose molecules far from the data used to train downstream synthesizability filters. Existing
  filters (SAScore, SCScore, RAscore, DeepSA) are purely statistical and degrade in exactly this out-of-distribution (OOD) regime. We ask whether cheap, closed-form physical priors, used
  as auxiliary supervision on a graph neural network (GNN), improve OOD generalization. We add two auxiliary losses to a GINE backbone: a topological complexity regression supervised by
  the Bertz index, and a strain-energy soft penalty supervised by MMFF94 force-field energy. On a 65,177-molecule corpus (HIV, Tox21, COCONUT) labeled by SAScore thresholds we reproduce
  a strong in-distribution baseline, then evaluate a 4-way ablation (baseline / +complexity / +strain / +both) on a single-source OOD split (train on drug-like HIV+Tox21, test on
  COCONUT natural products), repeated over 5 seeds with paired bootstrap confidence intervals. All three physics-aware variants give a small but statistically significant OOD improvement
  over the baseline (mean OOD AUC 0.9774): +complexity Delta = +0.0060 (95% CI [+0.0023, +0.0102]), +strain Delta = +0.0032 ([+0.0008, +0.0052]), +both Delta = +0.0066 ([+0.0038,
  +0.0093]); every interval excludes zero, and the combination is best. The variants are indistinguishable in-distribution, so the effect is visible only under OOD evaluation. We are
  explicit that the effects are modest, and we report a cautionary methodological finding: a single-seed version of this experiment produced a qualitatively different (non-monotone)
  story that did not survive multi-seed evaluation.

AI.tldr: Adding cheap physical priors (Bertz complexity + MMFF94 strain energy) as auxiliary losses to a GNN-based synthesizability classifier yields small but statistically significant OOD generalization improvements, but effects are modest and single-seed evaluations can be misleading.
AI.motivation: Existing synthesizability filters (SAScore, SCScore, RAscore, DeepSA) are purely statistical and degrade under out-of-distribution (OOD) conditions where generative models propose novel molecules far from training data.
AI.method: Added two auxiliary losses to a GINE backbone: (1) topological complexity regression via Bertz index, (2) strain-energy soft penalty via MMFF94 force field. Evaluated 4-way ablation on OOD split (train: drug-like HIV+Tox21, test: COCONUT natural products) over 5 seeds with paired bootstrap confidence intervals.
AI.result: All physics-aware variants show small but significant OOD AUC improvements over baseline (0.9774): +complexity +0.0060, +strain +0.0032, +both +0.0066. Effects are indistinguishable in-distribution. Single-seed evaluation produced a qualitatively different story.
AI.conclusion: Physical priors modestly improve OOD generalization for synthesizability prediction; multi-seed evaluation is critical for reliable conclusions.

title: WT-UMI: Tactile-based Whole-Body Manipulation via Force-Supervised Contact-Aware Planning
summary: Whole-body humanoid manipulation of bulky, deformable, and shared-load objects requires distributed contact sensing and explicit force regulation, yet most imitation policies treat contact force only implicitly. On the other hand, different demonstration sources provide complementary modalities with inherent trade-offs: human demonstrations capture natural contact forces but not robot-executable actions, while teleoperation directly records robot actions but with less natural force regulation. This paper presents \textbf{WT-UMI}, a wearable whole-body tactile interface worn by human operators or mounted on humanoids, providing accurate observations of tactile images, contact forces, and end-effector poses across both human demonstration and humanoid teleoperation modes. We introduce a force-conditioned target-pose correction module that converts measured human poses into contact-aware robot targets by learning corrections from teleoperation data. To leverage the natural force interaction in human data, we propose a force-supervised planner that predicts end-effector pose chunks and contact-force trajectories. The predicted contact force serves as the reference for a tactile-based admittance controller. Across five contact-rich tasks spanning deformable objects, bulky rigid objects, and human--humanoid collaboration, WT-UMI improves success rate and reduces contact-position tracking error over four policy baselines. Our project page is available at https://wt-umi.github.io/WTUMI/.

AI.tldr: WT-UMI is a wearable whole-body tactile interface that enables both human demonstration and humanoid teleoperation for contact-rich manipulation tasks, using force-conditioned pose correction and force-supervised planning with tactile-based admittance control to outperform baselines on deformable, bulky, and collaborative tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing imitation policies treat contact force only implicitly, and different demonstration sources (human vs. teleoperation) have complementary but incomplete modalities—human demos capture natural forces but not robot-executable actions, while teleoperation records actions but lacks natural force regulation.
AI.method: WT-UMI uses a wearable whole-body tactile interface for both human demonstration and humanoid teleoperation; a force-conditioned target-pose correction module converts human poses to contact-aware robot targets; a force-supervised planner predicts pose chunks and contact-force trajectories; and a tactile-based admittance controller uses predicted force as reference.
AI.result: Across five contact-rich tasks (deformable objects, bulky rigid objects, human–humanoid collaboration), WT-UMI improves success rate and reduces contact-position tracking error over four policy baselines.
AI.conclusion: Explicit force regulation via tactile sensing and force-supervised planning is crucial for whole-body humanoid manipulation of bulky, deformable, and shared-load objects, and WT-UMI effectively bridges human demonstration and robot teleoperation modalities.

title: Zero-source LLM Hallucination Detection with Human-like Criteria Probing
summary: Large language models (LLMs) often hallucinate by generating factually incorrect or unfaithful content, posing significant risks to their safe use. Detecting such hallucinations is particularly challenging under the zero-source constraint, where no model internals or external references are available, and detection must rely solely on the textual query-answer pair. In this paper, we propose Human-like Criteria Probing for Hallucination Detection (HCPD), a paradigm that emulates the multi-faceted reasoning of human evaluators. Its core is a Human-like Criteria Probing (HCP) mechanism, in which a LLM agent adaptively decomposes its judgment into a weighted set of interpretable criteria and aggregates criterion-specific scores into a final truthfulness measure. To achieve this adaptive capability, we introduce a reward-based alignment scheme using only weak supervision from semantic consistency. At inference, we employ a multi-sampling aggregation strategy to ensure robust decisions while preserving full interpretability. We further provide theoretical analysis supporting the reliability of our approach. Extensive experiments show that HCPD consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, offering an effective and explainable solution for zero-source hallucination detection. Code is available at https://github.com/TRISKEL10N/HCPD.

AI.tldr: HCPD is a zero-source hallucination detection method for LLMs that emulates human evaluators by adaptively decomposing judgments into interpretable criteria with weighted scores, using reward-based alignment and multi-sampling aggregation for robust, explainable detection.
AI.motivation: LLMs frequently hallucinate (producing factually incorrect/unfaithful content), which poses safety risks. Detecting hallucinations under the zero-source constraint (no model internals or external references, only text query-answer pair) is particularly challenging.
AI.method: Proposes Human-like Criteria Probing (HCP) mechanism where an LLM agent adaptively decomposes judgments into weighted interpretable criteria, uses reward-based alignment with weak supervision from semantic consistency, and employs multi-sampling aggregation at inference for robust decisions with full interpretability.
AI.result: HCPD consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in zero-source hallucination detection, with theoretical analysis supporting its reliability.
AI.conclusion: HCPD offers an effective and explainable solution for zero-source hallucination detection, outperforming existing methods while maintaining interpretability.

title: TetherCache: Stabilizing Autoregressive Long-Form Video Generation with Gated Recall and Trusted Alignment
summary: Autoregressive video diffusion models provide a natural formulation for streaming and variable-length video generation by conditioning newly generated frames on previously generated content. However, extending these models to minute-level generation remains challenging: the limited KV-cache budget prevents the model from retaining the full history, while repeatedly conditioning on self-generated frames induces a context distribution shift that accumulates over time, leading to visual artifacts, quality degradation, and temporal drift. In this paper, we propose TetherCache, a training-free and plug-and-play cache management strategy for drift-resistant long video generation. TetherCache organizes the cache into sink, memory, and recent regions, and introduces two complementary mechanisms. First, GRAB (Gated Recall with Attention-Diversity Balancing) selects long-range memory frames using a gated score that combines attention-based relevance with temporal diversity, preserving informative yet diverse historical context under a fixed cache budget. Second, TAME (Trusted Alignment via Memory Editing) lightly edits newly recalled memory tokens by aligning their statistics to a trusted context distribution, reducing the pollution caused by drifted historical features. Built on Self-Forcing, TetherCache consistently improves long-video generation quality on VBench-Long across 30s, 60s, and 240s settings. In particular, for 240s generation, it substantially improves overall and semantic scores while reducing quality drift from 7.84 to 1.33, demonstrating its effectiveness for stable long-horizon autoregressive video diffusion.

AI.tldr: TetherCache is a training-free cache management strategy for autoregressive video diffusion models that mitigates drift in long video generation by organizing KV-cache into sink/memory/recent regions and using two mechanisms: GRAB (gated recall with attention-diversity balancing) for selecting diverse long-range memories, and TAME (trusted alignment via memory editing) to align recalled tokens to a trusted distribution, achieving significant improvements on VBench-Long up to 240s generation.
AI.motivation: Autoregressive video diffusion models struggle with minute-level generation due to limited KV-cache budgets (cannot retain full history) and context distribution shift from conditioning on self-generated frames, which causes artifacts, quality degradation, and temporal drift over time.
AI.method: TetherCache organizes cache into three regions (sink, memory, recent) and introduces GRAB, which selects long-range memory frames via a gated score combining attention-based relevance with temporal diversity, and TAME, which edits recalled memory tokens by aligning their statistics to a trusted context distribution to reduce pollution from drifted features.
AI.result: Consistent improvements on VBench-Long at 30s, 60s, and 240s settings; for 240s generation, overall and semantic scores substantially improve while quality drift decreases from 7.84 to 1.33.
AI.conclusion: TetherCache is an effective, training-free plug-and-play solution for stable long-horizon autoregressive video generation, successfully addressing drift and quality degradation in extended sequences.

title: Physics-Informed Neural Networks for Chemotherapy Pharmacokinetics: Benchmarking the Clinical Estimator and Exposing Parameter Identifiability
summary:
Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) are an attractive tool for partial-observation problems in biology, where the governing dynamics are known but some compartments cannot be measured. Chemotherapy pharmacokinetics (PK) is a clean instance: drug concentration in plasma is routinely measured, but concentration in tissue -- which determines tumour kill and off-target toxicity -- is not. We benchmark a PINN against the standard clinical baseline (nonlinear least-squares on the analytical biexponential plasma solution, hereafter NLS) and a physics-agnostic neural baseline (a data-only MLP) on two PK problems. On the linear two-compartment problem, NLS is near-optimal; the PINN matches it to within a small constant factor while also producing the tissue curve in a single training pass, whereas the data-only MLP fails on tissue by roughly 10x. On a Michaelis-Menten extension (saturable elimination), the biexponential closed form no longer exists, so NLS is mis-specified and silently returns meaningless rate constants. The PINN instead exposes a deeper fact: the Michaelis-Menten two-compartment model is non-identifiable from plasma alone, and the PINN reports this honestly by converging to a basin with k12 -> 0. Adding two sparse tissue observations largely resolves identifiability: across five seeds the PINN recovers k21 to within 1% of truth and Vmax, Km to within one standard-deviation bar, while k12 moves in the correct direction (0.02 -> 0.82) but remains ~2 sigma below truth -- a recovery the closed-form NLS estimator cannot attempt at all, because its biexponential ansatz describes only plasma. Our claim is not that PINNs beat NLS. It is that PINNs offer a uniform recipe that ties the textbook estimator on the textbook problem, exposes structural identifiability that
  the textbook estimator hides, and absorbs heterogeneous measurements within a single loss.

AI.tldr: PINNs match the standard clinical method on linear two-compartment pharmacokinetics and outperform it on nonlinear (Michaelis-Menten) models by exposing non-identifiability issues and recovering hidden tissue dynamics, whereas the standard method fails when its closed-form assumptions are violated.
AI.motivation: To address the problem that tissue drug concentration — which determines both tumor kill and off-target toxicity — cannot be directly measured in clinical settings, motivating the need for methods that can infer unobserved compartments from plasma measurements alone.
AI.method: Benchmarks PINNs against nonlinear least-squares (NLS) on the analytical biexponential solution and a physics-agnostic MLP on two pharmacokinetics problems: a linear two-compartment model and a Michaelis-Menten extension with saturable elimination.
AI.result: On linear problems, PINN matches near-optimal NLS within a small constant factor while also producing the tissue curve; on the Michaelis-Menten problem, PINN correctly exposes structural non-identifiability from plasma alone and recovers parameters well with sparse tissue observations, while NLS silently returns meaningless results.
AI.conclusion: PINNs offer a uniform recipe that ties the textbook estimator on textbook problems, exposes identifiability issues that standard methods hide, and can absorb heterogeneous measurements within a single loss function.

title: Humor Style Drives Laughter, Topic Shapes Acceptability: Evaluating Bilingual Personal and Political Robot-Delivered AI Jokes
comment: Accepted in the 35th IEEE International Conference on Robot and Human Interactive Communication (RO-MAN 2026), Kitakyushu, Fukuoka, Japan
summary: Humor plays a central role in human social relationships, and recent advances in computational humor create new opportunities for integrating humor into human-robot interaction (HRI). While large language models (LLMs) can generate diverse forms of humor, it remains unclear how humor style, joke content, and language preference shape perceptions of robot-delivered humor in group settings. In this exploratory study, we employed a mixed factorial design in which participants evaluated AI-generated jokes delivered by a robot in a university classroom. We examined the effects of humor type (Affiliative, Self-Enhancing, Aggressive, Self-Defeating) and joke content (person-related vs. political) on perceived funniness and appropriateness, as well as preferred language. Results show that humor type significantly influences funniness, with Aggressive and Affiliative humor rated higher, while joke content primarily affects appropriateness, with person-related jokes preferred over political ones. Language preference was shaped by both joke content and participants' self-reported fluency and humor practices.

AI.tldr: This study explores how humor style, joke content, and language preference affect perceptions of robot-delivered humor in group settings. Using AI-generated jokes delivered by a robot in a classroom, they found Aggressive and Affiliative humor funnier, person-related jokes more appropriate, and language preference influenced by fluency and humor practices.
AI.motivation: Humor is central to human social relationships, and while computational humor has advanced with LLMs, it remains unclear how humor style, joke content, and language preference shape perceptions of robot-delivered humor in group settings.
AI.method: Mixed factorial design experiment in a university classroom where participants evaluated AI-generated jokes delivered by a robot. Examined humor type (Affiliative, Self-Enhancing, Aggressive, Self-Defeating), joke content (person-related vs. political), and language preference.
AI.result: Humor type significantly influences perceived funniness (Aggressive and Affiliative rated highest). Joke content primarily affects appropriateness (person-related jokes preferred over political). Language preference shaped by both joke content and participants' fluency/humor practices.
AI.conclusion: Different humor dimensions (type and content) affect distinct perceptions (funniness vs. appropriateness), and individual language factors matter for robot-delivered humor in groups.

title: MDForge: Agentic Molecular Dynamics Pipeline Design under Sparse Simulator Feedback
summary: Molecular dynamics (MD) is the canonical in-silico method for atomistic molecular science, simulating molecular behavior from first-principle physics. Designing an MD pipeline for a new system requires substantial expert knowledge: running it on even one molecule is expensive, ruling out trial-and-error. We automate this expert pipeline-design process with an LLM agent. Unlike existing MD agents that orchestrate a predefined tool set, we treat pipeline design as open-ended code generation in which the agent's behavior is reshaped online by verbal reward. Specifically, we build MDForge, an LLM agent whose in-context update rule densifies the sparse reward via a multi-agent debate among physics experts. On three SAMPL host-guest binding free-energy benchmarks, MDForge automatically designs MD pipelines competitive with human experts. Deployed on a library of unseen candidate guests, its CB[7] pipeline discovers a novel binder that wet-lab competition NMR confirms is a high-affinity, picomolar CB[7] binder. Our data and code are available at https://github.com/Zehong-Wang/MDForge.

AI.tldr: MDForge is an LLM agent that automates molecular dynamics pipeline design through open-ended code generation guided by multi-agent debate rewards, achieving expert-competitive results on binding free-energy benchmarks and discovering a novel picomolar binder.
AI.motivation: Designing MD pipelines requires substantial expert knowledge and is expensive, making trial-and-error infeasible. The goal is to automate this pipeline design process to reduce human effort and enable broader application of MD simulations.
AI.method: MDForge uses an LLM agent that treats pipeline design as open-ended code generation. Its in-context update rule densifies sparse reward via a multi-agent debate among physics experts, allowing online reshaping of agent behavior through verbal reward without a predefined tool set.
AI.result: On three SAMPL host-guest binding free-energy benchmarks, MDForge achieves pipelines competitive with human experts. Its CB[7] pipeline discovered a novel high-affinity, picomolar CB[7] binder confirmed by wet-lab competition NMR.
AI.conclusion: Automating MD pipeline design with LLM agents using open-ended code generation and multi-agent debate reward is a viable approach that can discover novel molecular binders without human expert intervention.

title: SeamEdit: A Black-Box VLM-Agnostic Pipeline for Large-Image Semantic Editing
summary: Semantic region editing for large images must satisfy two requirements at the same time: high generative quality and natural integration with surrounding content. Some related methods rely on white-box models and leave the strong generation capability of closed-source models underexplored. Directly applying closed-source models to tiled editing, however, introduces several failure modes: semantic deformation, canvas-level alignment drift, and visible seam artifacts. This paper presents SeamEdit, a training-free and model-agnostic pipeline that treats any VLM with inpainting capability as a black-box oracle. SeamEdit mitigates these issues through a five-stage post-hoc pipeline: overlay-based tile decomposition, black-box VLM inpainting, geometric and color-consistency correction, seam-risk-based multi-candidate ranking, and dynamic-programming curved seam fusion. The pipeline reduces seam visibility and supports semantic modification of arbitrary tile regions.

AI.tldr: SeamEdit is a training-free, model-agnostic pipeline that uses any VLM inpainting oracle for semantic region editing in large images, addressing failures like deformation, drift, and seams via a five-stage post-hoc process.
AI.motivation: Existing methods rely on white-box models with limited generative power, while closed-source models (VLMs) struggle with tiled editing due to semantic deformation, alignment drift, and seam artifacts.
AI.method: A five-stage pipeline: (1) overlay-based tile decomposition, (2) black-box VLM inpainting, (3) geometric and color-consistency correction, (4) seam-risk-based multi-candidate ranking, and (5) dynamic-programming curved seam fusion.
AI.result: The pipeline reduces seam visibility and enables semantic modification of arbitrary tile regions without retraining.
AI.conclusion: SeamEdit offers a practical, training-free solution to leverage powerful closed-source VLMs for large-image semantic editing while mitigating key tiling failure modes.

title: A Zero-shot Generalized Graph Anomaly Detection Framework via Node Reconstruction
summary: Cross-domain graph anomaly detection (GAD) aims to identify abnormal nodes in unseen target graphs, showing strong potential in real-world applications with heterogeneous graph data. However, existing methods often depend on dataset-specific feature semantics and structural patterns, which limits their ability to generalize across different domains. To address this challenge, we propose AlignGAD, a zero-shot generalized graph anomaly detection framework. Our framework is built upon three key components: a Global Unification Module that aligns heterogeneous node features and normalizes graph signals in the spectral domain; a Clustering Module that constructs cluster-aware graph views to capture group-level abnormal patterns; and a Node Discrepancy Scoring Module that measures reconstruction discrepancy and aggregates anomaly evidence from different graph views. Experiments on multiple real-world datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of AlignGAD under the zero-shot GAD setting.

AI.tldr: AlignGAD is a zero-shot framework for cross-domain graph anomaly detection that aligns heterogeneous features, captures group-level abnormal patterns via clustering, and aggregates anomaly evidence across graph views.
AI.motivation: Existing GAD methods struggle to generalize across different domains because they rely on dataset-specific feature semantics and structural patterns.
AI.method: Three modules: (1) Global Unification Module for feature alignment and spectral normalization, (2) Clustering Module for cluster-aware graph views, (3) Node Discrepancy Scoring Module for measuring reconstruction discrepancy and aggregating anomaly evidence.
AI.result: Effectiveness demonstrated on multiple real-world datasets under zero-shot GAD setting.
AI.conclusion: AlignGAD enables zero-shot generalization across domains for graph anomaly detection.

title: See Selectively, Act Adaptively: Dual-Level Structural Decomposition for Bimanual Robot Manipulation
summary: In bimanual robotic manipulation, task-relevant visual information varies with the task stage and context, while the interaction of the two arms shifts between independent and coordinated modes, making policy learning challenging. However, existing monolithic Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies process diverse visual inputs and interaction patterns through a single shared representation and action generation pathway, often failing to separately account for visual relevance and bimanual interaction structure. To address this issue, we propose a bimanual manipulation VLA framework based on Dual-Level Structural Decomposition. The View-Selective Visual Router dynamically adjusts wrist-view contributions to emphasize relevant visual cues, while the Interaction-Aware Action Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) decomposes action generation into coordinated and arm-wise pathways to adapt to varying bimanual interaction modes. We evaluate the proposed method on six simulated bimanual manipulation tasks in RoboTwin 2.0 and three long-horizon real-world tasks. Our model improves the overall average success rate over a monolithic baseline by 27.7% in simulation and 43.3% in real-world evaluation, while consistently outperforming single-module variants across both settings. These results demonstrate that jointly considering selective visual processing and explicit decomposition of bimanual interaction structures provides an effective inductive bias for robust bimanual manipulation.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a bimanual manipulation VLA framework with dual-level structural decomposition, featuring a View-Selective Visual Router for dynamic visual emphasis and an Interaction-Aware Action Mixture-of-Experts for decoupled action generation, achieving 27.7% (sim) and 43.3% (real) success rate improvements over monolithic baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing monolithic VLA policies fail to separately account for varying visual relevance and shifting bimanual interaction patterns (independent vs. coordinated), as they process diverse visual inputs and interaction modes through a single shared representation and action generation pathway.
AI.method: Dual-Level Structural Decomposition: (1) View-Selective Visual Router dynamically adjusts wrist-view contributions to emphasize task-relevant visual cues per stage; (2) Interaction-Aware Action Mixture-of-Experts decomposes action generation into coordinated and arm-wise pathways to handle varying bimanual interaction modes.
AI.result: Average success rate improved by 27.7% over monolithic baseline in simulation (6 tasks in RoboTwin 2.0) and 43.3% in real-world evaluation (3 long-horizon tasks), consistently outperforming single-module variants.
AI.conclusion: Jointly considering selective visual processing and explicit decomposition of bimanual interaction structures provides an effective inductive bias for robust bimanual manipulation.

title: Iterating Toward Better Search: A Two-Agent Simulation Framework for Evaluating Agentic Search Architectures in E-Commerce
summary: We present a modular two-agent simulation framework for evaluating conversational shopping assistant architectures. An independent buyer agent, configured with personas, missions, and patience levels, is paired with an interchangeable responder that integrates with a real e-commerce search API. Holding the buyer constant across experiments enables controlled comparison of responder designs on identical scenarios. Using 2011 conversations across 14 persona buckets, we establish four empirical findings. First, rolling-window memory outperforms intent-extraction memory on all quality metrics while being 35% faster per query. Second, illustrating rapid evidence-driven iteration, a systematic failure analysis of a responder version enables targeted fixes that reduce failure and near-failure rates by 62% across the full dataset. Third, swapping the responder LLM backbone from Gemini~2.5 to Llama~3.3~70B costs 0.16--0.45 points despite identical architecture. Finally, we document systematic philosophical disagreement between frontier LLM judges: Gemini rewards process correctness while Claude demands concrete outcomes, despite using the same evaluation prompt.

AI.tldr: A modular two-agent simulation framework for evaluating conversational shopping assistants is presented, with four key findings: rolling-window memory outperforms intent-extraction memory; failure analysis reduced failures by 62%; LLM backbone swaps cause performance drops; and different LLM judges (Gemini vs. Claude) disagree on evaluation criteria.
AI.motivation: To enable controlled, reproducible evaluation of conversational shopping assistant architectures using a modular two-agent simulation framework with independent buyer agents and interchangeable responders.
AI.method: A modular two-agent simulation framework with a buyer agent (configured with personas, missions, patience levels) and an interchangeable responder integrated with real e-commerce search API, tested on 2011 conversations across 14 persona buckets.
AI.result: Four findings: (1) Rolling-window memory beats intent-extraction memory on quality metrics and is 35% faster; (2) Failure analysis reduced failure/near-failure rates by 62%; (3) Swapping LLM backbone from Gemini 2.5 to Llama 3.3 70B costs 0.16-0.45 points; (4) Gemini and Claude judges systematically disagree on evaluation despite same prompt.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables empirical evaluation of shopping assistants, revealing important trade-offs in memory design, iteration strategies, backbone choices, and judge biases that must be accounted for in system development.

title: LaME: Learning to Think in Latent Space for Multimodal Embedding via Information Bottleneck
summary: Reasoning-driven universal multimodal embedding has advanced rapidly by introducing Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning into the embedding pipeline. Despite the strong performance across both general and complex tasks, this paradigm suffers from two core limitations: (i) autoregressive CoT reasoning incurs high computational cost, making it impractical for low-latency retrieval; and (ii) embedding performance is heavily coupled with CoT annotation quality, making large-scale training unreliable. These raise fundamental questions: Is textual CoT the optimal form of reasoning for embedding, and can effective embedding reasoning be accomplished in latent space? To this end, we propose LaME (Latent Reasoning Multimodal Embedding), which formulates embedding-oriented latent reasoning as a weakly supervised information bottleneck. LaME employs K learnable reason tokens as a fixed-capacity bottleneck, completing all reasoning within a single forward pass. The two weak supervision signals structurally decouple contrastive from autoregressive objectives and eliminate dependence on CoT annotations, while a two-stage training pipeline ensures stable convergence. Experiments on MMEB-v2 and MRMR show that LaME achieves competitive performance, surpassing some explicit CoT-based models, while delivering 60x faster inference than explicit CoT methods and 2x faster than latent baselines with throughput comparable to discriminative embedding models. Code will be released.

AI.tldr: LaME proposes a latent reasoning multimodal embedding method that uses learnable reason tokens as a fixed-capacity bottleneck, eliminating expensive CoT reasoning while achieving competitive performance with 60x faster inference than explicit CoT methods.
AI.motivation: Current CoT-based multimodal embeddings suffer from high computational cost and unreliable performance due to coupling with CoT annotation quality, raising the question of whether textual CoT is the optimal reasoning form for embedding tasks.
AI.method: LaME formulates embedding-oriented latent reasoning as a weakly supervised information bottleneck using K learnable reason tokens, completing all reasoning in a single forward pass. It uses two weak supervision signals to decouple contrastive and autoregressive objectives, eliminating CoT annotation dependency, with a two-stage training pipeline for stable convergence.
AI.result: LaME achieves competitive performance on MMEB-v2 and MRMR, surpassing some explicit CoT-based models. It delivers 60x faster inference than explicit CoT methods, 2x faster than latent baselines, with throughput comparable to discriminative embedding models.
AI.conclusion: Latent space reasoning can effectively replace textual CoT reasoning for multimodal embeddings, achieving both efficiency and performance without expensive autoregressive inference or reliance on CoT annotations.

title: EMG-Based Adaptation of Anisotropic Virtual Fixtures for Robot-Assisted Surgical Resection and Dissection
summary: In this paper, we address the development of an adaptive assistance system for robot-assisted laparoscopic surgery, specifically for delicate tasks such as Resection and Dissection. Even if Virtual Fixtures offer significant advantages for guiding a surgeon's movements, conventional Virtual Fixtures are often defined by fixed geometries, lacking the flexibility to adapt to the surgical workflow or the surgeon's immediate intent. To address these limitations, we propose a novel framework for an adaptive and anisotropic virtual fixture. In addition, we introduce an intuitive control interface that modulates the fixture's geometry in real-time based on the surgeon's intent, inferred from EMG signals. This approach allows the surgeon to dynamically expand or disengage the constraint by contracting their forearm muscles, enabling seamless transitions between precise guided motion and free repositioning of the tool. Experimental results from a pilot user study, based on a standardized surgical training task, demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method. The system showed significant improvements in task accuracy and movement consistency, alongside a reduction in perceived cognitive load, effort, and frustration.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an adaptive, anisotropic virtual fixture system for robot-assisted laparoscopic surgery that uses EMG signals from the surgeon's forearm to dynamically adjust constraint geometry, improving accuracy and reducing cognitive load.
AI.motivation: Conventional Virtual Fixtures used in robot-assisted surgery are limited by fixed geometries that cannot adapt to surgical workflow or surgeon's intent, making them insufficient for delicate tasks like Resection and Dissection.
AI.method: The authors develop an adaptive and anisotropic virtual fixture framework with an EMG-based control interface that infers surgeon intent from forearm muscle contractions, allowing real-time expansion or disengagement of geometric constraints.
AI.result: A pilot user study on a standardized surgical training task showed significant improvements in task accuracy and movement consistency, along with reduced perceived cognitive load, effort, and frustration.
AI.conclusion: The proposed adaptive virtual fixture system effectively addresses the inflexibility of conventional fixtures, enhancing surgical precision and user experience through intuitive muscle-based control.

title: MARS: Margin-Adversarial Risk-controlled Stopping for Parallel LLM Test-time Scaling
summary: Parallel test-time scaling samples many reasoning traces and majority-votes their answers, improving LLM accuracy but requiring traces to run to completion, incurring substantial computational overhead. We observe that probing partial traces at intermediate checkpoints can extract current answers without disrupting generation, revealing an evolving aggregate vote. Based on this observation, we introduce MARS, a margin-adversarial stopping rule that estimates which active traces are likely to change their answers and stops once the leader remains safe under a conservative bound on future vote movement. The rule separates two sources of uncertainty. It learns the trace-level switch probabilities that determine how much of the current margin is likely to be retained, while handling the harder question of where switching traces land through an adversarial bound calibrated from warmup traces. With true switch probabilities, MARS guarantees with high probability that the early-stopped answer matches the full-budget vote. In practice, a five-feature logistic model closely matches oracle switching behavior. Across three reasoning models and three competition-math benchmarks, MARS saves 25-47% of self-consistency tokens and 14-29% on top of DeepConf Online, a strong confidence-weighted baseline that already filters and truncates weak traces, while matching the accuracy of the corresponding full-budget baselines.

AI.tldr: MARS is a margin-adversarial stopping rule for parallel test-time scaling that early-stops majority voting on partial reasoning traces, saving 25-47% tokens while matching full-budget accuracy across reasoning models and math benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Standard parallel test-time scaling requires all reasoning traces to run to completion before majority voting, incurring substantial computational overhead. The authors observe that probing partial traces at intermediate checkpoints can reveal evolving aggregate votes, motivating a method to stop early without sacrificing accuracy.
AI.method: MARS uses a margin-adversarial stopping rule that separates two uncertainty sources: (1) trace-level switch probabilities (learned via a five-feature logistic model) estimating how likely active traces are to change their answer, and (2) an adversarial bound (calibrated from warmup traces) handling where switching traces land. Stopping occurs when the leader's margin remains safe under a conservative bound on future vote movement.
AI.result: Across three reasoning models and three competition-math benchmarks, MARS saves 25-47% of self-consistency tokens and 14-29% on top of DeepConf Online (a strong confidence-weighted baseline), while matching the accuracy of full-budget baselines.
AI.conclusion: With true switch probabilities, MARS guarantees high probability that the early-stopped answer matches the full-budget vote. The practical logistic model closely matches oracle switching behavior, enabling substantial computational savings without accuracy loss.

title: Unified MRI Brain Image Translation via Hierarchical Tumor Structure Comparison
summary:
Multi-modal MRI brain image translation via available modalities holds significant practical importance in modern medicine, providing robust support for early diagnosis, treatment planning, and outcome assessment of diseases. For this purpose, it is important to ensure the fidelity of the tumor regions after translation. However, existing brain image translation methods ignore the structure information of different tumor regions, which could assist translation models in enhancing the quality and clinical applicability of the translated images.
  In this work, we propose a novel translation model called HTSCGAN, which is a unified multi-modal brain image translation generative adversarial model integrating the structural information within tumor regions with the aim of improving the quality of brain image translation. Specifically, the generator employs three Patch Contrast Module (PCM) with different patch sizes to capture the hierarchical structural information of the tumor regions. In addition, a pretrained Patch Classifier (PC) and a pretrained Structure-Aware Encoder (SAE) are employed to derive the generated image containing the same tumor region structure as the ground truth image via patch classification loss and tumor perceptual loss, respectively. The experiments on BraTS2020 and BraTS2021 demonstrate strong performance of our model in both translation tasks and down stream segmentation tasks, highlighting its effectiveness in enhancing the quality and clinical relevance of the translated brain images. Our code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/HTSCGAN.

AI.tldr: HTSCGAN is a multi-modal MRI brain image translation model that uses hierarchical patch contrast modules and structure-aware encoders to preserve tumor region fidelity, outperforming existing methods on BraTS datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing brain image translation methods ignore the structural information of different tumor regions, which is critical for ensuring fidelity and clinical applicability of translated images.
AI.method: HTSCGAN uses a generator with three Patch Contrast Modules (PCM) of different patch sizes to capture hierarchical tumor structure, plus a pretrained Patch Classifier (PC) and Structure-Aware Encoder (SAE) to enforce tumor region consistency via patch classification loss and tumor perceptual loss.
AI.result: Experiments on BraTS2020 and BraTS2021 show strong performance in both translation tasks and downstream segmentation tasks, demonstrating enhanced quality and clinical relevance.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating hierarchical tumor structure information improves multi-modal brain image translation quality and clinical utility, as validated by quantitative and downstream task evaluations.

title: How Useful is Causal Invariance for Domain Adaptation in Finite-Sample Settings?
summary:
Machine learning models often degrade when they are deployed on a target distribution that differs from the source distributions they were trained on. Recent work in causality-based domain generalization has shown how shared causal structure between domains can induce invariant predictors, e.g., models on a subset of features which have stable risk across structured domain shifts. However, the extent to which such population-level causal invariances can lead to gains in finite-sample settings remains underexplored. In particular, in practice we often have access to a few labeled target samples, a setting called supervised domain adaptation (sDA). In this paper, we explore when (full or partial) causal knowledge can provably improve supervised domain adaptation.
  As a first step, we study linear regression, where full or partial causal knowledge specifies a collection of invariant or possibly invariant feature subsets, each yielding a source-trained candidate predictor. We derive matching upper and lower bounds showing that finite-sample gains are governed by the target-risk margins separating the candidates, together with the finite-source estimation error. When these margins are sufficiently large relative to $n_Q$, an adaptive aggregation procedure can match the best candidate predictor while avoiding negative transfer relative to target-only learning. On the other hand, when the margins are too small, no algorithm can reliably exploit the candidate collection to obtain faster finite-sample rates. We further connect these margins to structural shift magnitude in linear SCMs and validate the theory on real-world causal benchmarks.

AI.tldr: This paper studies when causal knowledge (invariant feature subsets) can provably improve supervised domain adaptation in linear regression, deriving matching upper and lower bounds that show gains depend on target-risk margins and finite-source estimation error.
AI.motivation: To understand the finite-sample benefits of causal invariance for domain adaptation, beyond the well-known population-level guarantees.
AI.method: The authors study linear regression with causal knowledge specifying invariant/possibly invariant feature subsets. They derive matching upper and lower bounds on finite-sample gains, analyze margins relative to target-only learning, and connect margins to structural shift magnitude in linear SCMs.
AI.result: Finite-sample gains are governed by target-risk margins separating candidate predictors and finite-source estimation error. When margins are large relative to n_Q, adaptive aggregation matches the best candidate and avoids negative transfer; when margins are too small, no algorithm can reliably exploit causal knowledge for faster rates.
AI.conclusion: Causal invariances can provably improve supervised domain adaptation, but only when target-risk margins are sufficiently large; the theory is validated on real-world causal benchmarks.

title: Low cost, easily manufactured, highly flexible strain and touch sensitive fiber for robotics applications
summary: Existing stretch and touch sensors for robots are generally expensive with respect to at least one of material costs, required manufacturing equipment, or manufacturing time. We present and experimentally characterize a conductive fiber made using only inexpensive commercial off-the-shelf parts (conductive thread at $0.07/ft, silicone tubing at $0.94/ft) and tools (loop-style needle threader at $2), which can be manufactured quickly (20 cm length in 2 minutes.) We demonstrate its use as a resistive strain sensor with three applications: Triggering a grasp in a pneumatically actuated assistive finger, sensing the pose of a pneumatically actuated robotic strap, and estimating the pose of a flexible solid. We also demonstrate that it can be used as a capacitive sensor with two applications: First, as a touch sensor which triggers a commercial robot arm to move, and second, as a near-field sensor enabling the robot arm to follow a moving hand. The capacitive sensors are knitted, showcasing the high flexibility of the fiber. We discuss methods for improving manufacturing scalability and their cost trade-offs. Finally, we demonstrate a method for repairing a cut fiber.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a low-cost, fast-manufacturable conductive fiber made from commercial off-the-shelf materials ($0.07/ft thread, $0.94/ft tubing) that functions as both a resistive strain sensor and capacitive touch/near-field sensor, demonstrated in robotic applications including grasp triggering, pose sensing, and hand-following.
AI.motivation: Existing stretch and touch sensors for robots are too expensive in terms of materials, equipment, or manufacturing time.
AI.method: A conductive fiber is fabricated using conductive thread, silicone tubing, and a loop-style needle threader, serving as both resistive strain sensors and capacitive touch/near-field sensors.
AI.result: Successful demonstrations: grasp triggering in assistive finger, pose sensing in robotic strap and flexible solid (resistive); touch-triggered robot arm movement and hand-following (capacitive). Knitted capacitive sensors show high flexibility, and a repair method for cut fibers is demonstrated.
AI.conclusion: The proposed fiber is inexpensive, quick to manufacture, and versatile (dual-mode sensing), with discussion of scalability improvements and cost trade-offs.

title: PRISMR: Overcoming Parse Collapse in Multimodal Listwise Ranking via Parameterized Representation Internalization
summary:
Generative listwise ranking with Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) aims to capture global list context in a single forward pass, but
  its effectiveness degrades in long-context multimodal scenarios. We identify a recurring failure mode, parse collapse, where the
  autoregressive decoder produces fluent yet incomplete rankings by silently omitting candidates and terminating early. This
  failure stems from limited context utilization rather than simple formatting mistakes, making prompt engineering and constrained
  decoding insufficient. We propose PRISMR (Parameterized Representation Internalization for Semantic Multimodal Ranking), a
  framework that replaces transient in-context list processing with parametric structural conditioning. PRISMR uses a lightweight
  hypernetwork to encode multimodal candidates in parallel and generate item-specific LoRA weights, which are synthesized into an
  instance-specific adapter for a LMM. This paradigm enables more robust internalization of list structure while preserving the
  base model. We further introduce a large-scale multimodal review-ranking benchmark for evaluation. Experiments demonstrate that
  PRISMR substantially reduces parse collapse, improves listwise ranking performance, and transfers effectively across domains and
  instruction-tuned backbones.

AI.tldr: PRISMR addresses "parse collapse" in generative listwise ranking with LMMs by replacing in-context list processing with parametric structural conditioning via a hypernetwork that generates item-specific LoRA weights, reducing omissions and improving ranking performance.
AI.motivation: Existing generative listwise ranking with LMMs suffers from "parse collapse"—autoregressive decoders produce fluent but incomplete rankings by silently omitting candidates and terminating early, especially in long-context multimodal scenarios. This stems from limited context utilization, not simple formatting errors.
AI.method: PRISMR (Parameterized Representation Internalization for Semantic Multimodal Ranking) uses a lightweight hypernetwork to encode multimodal candidates in parallel and generate item-specific LoRA weights, synthesized into an instance-specific adapter for the LMM, replacing transient in-context list processing with parametric structural conditioning.
AI.result: PRISMR substantially reduces parse collapse, improves listwise ranking performance, and transfers effectively across domains and instruction-tuned backbones.
AI.conclusion: PRISMR's parametric approach to encoding list structure is more robust than in-context processing, and the framework generalizes well across different domains and backbone models.

title: PP-OCRv6: From 1.5M to 34.5M Parameters, Surpassing Billion-Scale VLMs on OCR Tasks
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have achieved impressive results on general vision-language tasks, yet they suffer from hallucination, imprecise localization, and prohibitive computational cost when applied to dedicated OCR scenarios. This paper presents PP-OCRv6, a lightweight OCR system that combines architectural innovation with data-centric optimization. PP-OCRv6 redesigns the backbone, detection neck, and recognition neck around a unified MetaFormer-style building block with structural reparameterization, decoupling spatial token mixing from channel mixing and supporting both tasks through task-specific stride configurations. Three model tiers (medium, small, tiny) share the same block primitives, covering deployment scenarios from server to edge. On our in-house benchmarks, PP-OCRv6_medium achieves 83.2% recognition accuracy and 86.2% detection Hmean, outperforming PP-OCRv5_server by +5.1% and +4.6% respectively while surpassing Qwen3-VL-235B, GPT-5.5, and Gemini-3.1-Pro with orders of magnitude fewer parameters. The tiny tier achieves 3.9$\times$ faster inference than PP-OCRv5_mobile on Intel Xeon CPU while maintaining comparable accuracy.

AI.tldr: PP-OCRv6 is a lightweight OCR system using MetaFormer-style blocks with structural reparameterization, achieving superior accuracy over large VLMs (Qwen3-VL-235B, GPT-5.5, Gemini-3.1-Pro) at a fraction of the parameters, and offering three model tiers for flexible deployment.
AI.motivation: Existing VLMs suffer from hallucination, imprecise localization, and high computational cost in OCR scenarios, motivating the need for a dedicated, lightweight OCR system.
AI.method: PP-OCRv6 redesigns backbone, detection neck, and recognition neck using a unified MetaFormer-style block with structural reparameterization, decoupling spatial token mixing from channel mixing with task-specific stride configurations across three tiers (medium, small, tiny).
