To address the sparse and delayed supervision provided by final veracity labels, ProFact introduces process-aware rewards that provide stage-level learning signals throughout the verification process. Empirical evaluation shows that ProFact consistently outperforms strong baselines in both verification performance and inference efficiency. These results highlight the effectiveness of process-aware trajectory optimization for multi-stage fact verification.

AI.tldr: ProFact is an agentic reinforcement learning framework that uses process-aware rewards to optimize multi-stage fact verification pipelines end-to-end, outperforming strong baselines in both accuracy and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-stage fact verification systems optimize individual stages in isolation or rely on fixed heuristics, lacking adaptive coordination among stages (claim decomposition, evidence gathering, answer generation, verdict prediction), leading to suboptimal outcomes.
AI.method: ProFact employs an agentic reinforcement learning framework that trains a unified policy to coordinate all verification stages end-to-end, and introduces process-aware rewards to provide stage-level learning signals, addressing sparse and delayed supervision from final veracity labels.
AI.result: ProFact consistently outperforms strong baselines in both verification performance and inference efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Process-aware trajectory optimization is effective for multi-stage fact verification, highlighting the benefit of end-to-end adaptive coordination and stage-level reward signals.

title: Budget-Constrained Step-Level Diffusion Caching
summary: Step-level caching accelerates diffusion models by exploiting temporal redundancy across denoising steps. Existing methods make per-step cache decisions using threshold-based heuristics, without directly optimizing for final output quality. As a result, their inference latency varies across inputs and is difficult to control at deployment. In this work, we propose BudCache, which inverts this formulation: rather than letting per-step error thresholds dictate the runtime cost, we fix the compute budget in advance and search for the cache policy that best preserves the final output. To tackle the combinatorial complexity of step selection, we combine Simulated Annealing with deterministic Hill Climbing. This offline search identifies high-quality cache policies within minutes and introduces no online search or thresholding overhead during inference. When the compute budget is very tight, we further introduce cache-aware schedule alignment, which adapts the time discretization to the selected cache policy to reduce cache-induced trajectory mismatch. Experiments on FLUX.1-dev and Wan2.1 show that BudCache achieves better generation quality than heuristic caching baselines under the same inference budgets. Code is available at https://github.com/Westlake-AGI-Lab/BudCache

AI.tldr: BudCache proposes a compute-budget-aware caching method for diffusion models that pre-optimizes cache policies via simulated annealing and hill climbing, achieving better quality than heuristic methods under fixed inference budgets.
AI.motivation: Existing step-level caching methods use threshold-based heuristics to make per-step cache decisions, which do not directly optimize for final output quality and result in unpredictable inference latency that is difficult to control during deployment.
AI.method: BudCache inverts the typical formulation: it fixes the compute budget in advance and searches for the optimal cache policy via Simulated Annealing combined with deterministic Hill Climbing. For tight budgets, it introduces cache-aware schedule alignment to adapt time discretization to the selected cache policy, reducing trajectory mismatch.
AI.result: Experiments on FLUX.1-dev and Wan2.1 show that BudCache achieves better generation quality than heuristic caching baselines under the same inference budgets.
AI.conclusion: By pre-optimizing cache policies offline with no online search overhead, BudCache provides controllable, budget-aware inference acceleration while preserving output quality better than heuristic approaches.

title: Is Spurious Correlation Removal Always Learnable?
summary: Invariant learning can fail even when the invariant structure is statistically identifiable. We show a conditional computational barrier: under a black-box samplable supervised sparse recovery primitive motivated by average-case sparse-recovery reductions, there exist \emph{samplable} multi-environment instances with a one-dimensional predictive invariant subspace ($k=1$) that are learnable with polynomial samples by exhaustive search, while any polynomial-time constant-accuracy recovery algorithm would contradict the primitive. We further quantify environment diversity by a separation parameter $γ$, which controls identifiability and the curvature of invariance objectives. Under sufficient diversity and local Gaussian regularity, the minimax risk is $\mathbb{E}[\dist(\hat{V},V_{\mathrm{inv}})^2]=Θ(k(d-k)/(n|\mathcal{E}|))$, and under label-induced shifts a phase transition occurs at $n^*\propto k(d-k)/(|\mathcal{E}|γ^2)$ with refined estimation error scaling proportional to $1/γ^2$. Synthetic and real datasets illustrate the predicted gaps and transitions and motivate simple diversity diagnostics.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies a computational barrier for invariant learning (even when statistically identifiable), shows minimax risk bounds for invariant subspace estimation under sufficient diversity, and demonstrates a phase transition in label-shift settings—validated on synthetic and real data.
AI.motivation: Invariant learning can fail computationally even when the invariant structure is statistically identifiable. The paper aims to characterize fundamental computational and statistical limits under black-box sparse recovery primitives and environment diversity.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis: (1) Conditional hardness result via reductions from average-case sparse recovery; (2) Minimax risk analysis under separation parameter γ; (3) Phase transition analysis under label-induced shifts. Experiments on synthetic and real datasets validate predictions.
AI.result: Under a sparse-recovery primitive, polynomial-time algorithms fail to recover a 1-D invariant subspace. Minimax risk: Θ(k(d-k)/(n|ℰ|)). Phase transition at n* ∝ k(d-k)/(|ℰ|γ²) with 1/γ² scaling.
AI.conclusion: Computational barriers exist for invariant learning despite statistical identifiability; environment diversity (γ) critically governs both identifiability and estimation accuracy; simple diversity diagnostics are motivated.

title: ERTS: Adversarial Robustness Testing of Ethical AI via Semantic Perturbation in a Bounded Consequence Space
summary: As AI systems are deployed in high-stakes ethical contexts such as healthcare triage, autonomous vehicle control, and employment screening, formal methods for evaluating their robustness against adversarial manipulation of ethical reasoning remain underdeveloped. This paper introduces the Ethical Robustness Testing System (ERTS), a closed-pipeline framework that: (1) encodes ethical dilemmas into a 22-dimensional Ethical Consequence Space (ECS) grounded in established ethical theory; (2) applies 17 semantic perturbation functions subject to 6 validity constraint classes including a novel semantic coherence constraint; (3) measures decision deviation via a 4-component Ethical Instability Index (EII); and (4) produces domain-adaptive pre-deployment robustness assessment verdicts. We evaluate 4 structured baseline models and 2 production LLMs (Gemini 2.0 Flash and Llama 3.2) across 50 ethical scenarios spanning 8 deployment domains, generating 1,500 adversarial test cases. Results demonstrate that only 33% of models achieve assessment clearance, with the local Llama-3.2 model proving particularly vulnerable to fairness corruption and information degradation attacks (ERS = 0.737). To the best of our knowledge, no existing framework combines a bounded ethical consequence space, semantic coherence constraints, and domain-adaptive assessment in a single adversarial testing pipeline.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces ERTS, a framework for testing ethical robustness of AI systems using a 22-dimensional ethical space, 17 perturbation functions, and a 4-component instability index. Testing on 6 models across 50 scenarios with 1,500 adversarial cases shows only 33% pass assessment, with Llama-3.2 being especially vulnerable.
AI.motivation: AI systems are deployed in high-stakes ethical contexts (healthcare, autonomous vehicles, employment screening), yet formal methods for evaluating their robustness against adversarial manipulation of ethical reasoning remain underdeveloped.
AI.method: ERTS framework: (1) encodes ethical dilemmas into a 22-dimensional Ethical Consequence Space (ECS); (2) applies 17 semantic perturbation functions under 6 validity constraints including semantic coherence; (3) measures decision deviation via a 4-component Ethical Instability Index (EII); (4) produces domain-adaptive pre-deployment robustness assessment verdicts. Tested on 4 structured baselines + 2 LLMs (Gemini 2.0 Flash, Llama 3.2) across 50 scenarios and 8 domains with 1,500 adversarial test cases.
AI.result: Only 33% of models achieved assessment clearance. Llama-3.2 was particularly vulnerable to fairness corruption and information degradation attacks (ERS = 0.737).
AI.conclusion: No existing framework combines a bounded ethical consequence space, semantic coherence constraints, and domain-adaptive assessment in a single adversarial testing pipeline, highlighting the novelty of ERTS.

title: Circuit Synchronization Precedes Generalization: Causal Evidence from Fourier Structure in Grokking Transformers
summary: Grokking -- where a transformer on modular arithmetic suddenly transitions from near-chance to near-perfect validation accuracy -- is attributed to a Fourier circuit, but its timing, causal structure, and controllability remain poorly understood. We introduce the Frequency Synchronization Degree (FSD), a normalised, permutation-tested metric for Fourier circuit synchronisation requiring no prior circuit knowledge. Across nine modular addition configurations (primes p in {53, 71, 97, 113, 131}, three seeds), FSD synchronises 500-3,000 steps before grokking (mean lead +1,722 steps; all nine positive, sign-test p~0.004), and precedes a restricted-logit loss baseline (Nanda et al.'s excluded loss) in all nine cases, making it the earliest available predictor. We provide direct causal evidence that the inter-phase gap is a regularisation phenomenon: forking training at the FSD-ceiling step and varying weight decay lambda produces strictly monotone earlier grokking, with Delta_t proportional to 1/lambda. This law replicates across three primes (p in {53,97,131}; R^2=1.00 and R^2=0.99 for two clean cases), captured as Delta_t ~ C/lambda, consistent with (1/lambda)*log(||W_mem||/tau). Architecture ablations show an attention-only model groks with a strong FSD precursor; an MLP-only model never groks; a single-layer model's FSD lags, confirming the precursor is a multi-block circuit property.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Frequency Synchronization Degree (FSD), a metric that predicts grokking in transformers up to 3,000 steps before it occurs, and demonstrates that grokking timing is causally controlled by weight decay following an inverse proportional law (Δt ~ C/λ).
AI.motivation: While grokking (sudden generalization in modular arithmetic transformers) is known to involve Fourier circuits, the precise timing, causal mechanisms, and controllability of this transition remain poorly understood. The authors aim to develop an early predictor and uncover the causal relationship between regularization and grokking timing.
AI.method: The authors propose Frequency Synchronization Degree (FSD), a normalized permutation-tested metric for Fourier circuit synchronization. They test across nine modular addition configurations (primes p ∈ {53,71,97,113,131}, three seeds), perform forking training at FSD-ceiling steps with varying weight decay λ, and conduct architecture ablations (attention-only, MLP-only, single-layer).
AI.result: FSD synchronizes 500-3,000 steps before grokking (mean lead: +1,722 steps; all nine positive, p~0.004), outperforming the excluded loss baseline. Forking experiments reveal a strictly monotonic inverse relationship: Δt ∝ 1/λ (R²=1.00 and 0.99 for clean cases). Architecture ablations show attention-only models grok with strong FSD precursor, MLP-only models never grok, and single-layer models show lagging FSD.
AI.conclusion: FSD is the earliest known predictor of grokking, and grokking timing is causally controlled by weight decay through a 1/λ scaling law, confirming it as a regularization-driven phenomenon. The precursor is a multi-block circuit property requiring both attention and MLP components.

title: Physics-Guided Spatiotemporal Learning for Coastal Wave Peak Period Estimation from Video
summary: Wave parameters in the nearshore are crucial for coastal engineering, shoreline protection, marine hazard assessment, and coastal management for climate resilience. Traditional monitoring systems like buoys and radar platforms offer accurate monitoring but can have high installation and maintenance expenses and limited spatial coverage. Passive ocean monitoring using video has been achieved by leveraging deep learning, however, many methods are not physically interpretable, feasible, and validated for oceanography. In thiswork, a Physics-Guided Deep Spatiotemporal Learning Framework for direct estimation of nearshore wave peak periods from passive coastal video stream is proposed. The framework combines automated temporal-variance based region-of-interest detection, multi-stage Sim-to-Real transfer learning, and physics-informed regularization to enhance the predictive accuracy and physical consistency. A variety of spatiotemporal architectures were assessed, such as transformer-based and recurrent-convolutional ones, alongside synthetic pretraining,silver-label adaptation, and expert fine-tuning. The results show that transformer-based architectures outperformed in terms of the accuracy of the instantaneous prediction, while lightweight recurrent-convolutional architectures achieved higher temporal stability and operational oceanographic skill. Ablation studies also demonstrated the benefits of physics-guided regularization in terms of trend-following consistency, and physically implausible predictions. Explainability auditing also helped to focus attention in hydrodynamically active surf-zone regions and showed good agreement with the physically derived wave propagation behavior. In general, the proposed framework shows the promise of physics-guided video-based deep learning systems for long-term coastal wave monitoring that are cost-efficient and operationally feasible.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a Physics-Guided Deep Spatiotemporal Learning Framework that uses passive coastal video streams to estimate nearshore wave peak periods, combining ROI detection, Sim-to-Real transfer learning, and physics-informed regularization. Transformer architectures excel at instantaneous accuracy while recurrent-convolutional models offer better temporal stability for operational use.
AI.motivation: Traditional wave monitoring systems (buoys, radar) are expensive and offer limited spatial coverage. While deep learning on video has been explored, many existing methods lack physical interpretability, feasibility, and oceanographic validation. A cost-efficient, physically consistent alternative is needed.
AI.method: A Physics-Guided Deep Spatiotemporal Learning Framework is developed, featuring: (1) automated temporal-variance based ROI detection, (2) multi-stage Sim-to-Real transfer learning (synthetic pretraining, silver-label adaptation, expert fine-tuning), and (3) physics-informed regularization. Various architectures (transformer-based, recurrent-convolutional) are evaluated.
AI.result: Transformer-based architectures achieve higher instantaneous prediction accuracy; lightweight recurrent-convolutional architectures provide better temporal stability and operational oceanographic skill. Ablation studies confirm physics-guided regularization improves trend consistency and reduces physically implausible predictions. Explainability shows attention focuses on hydrodynamically active surf-zone regions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework demonstrates that physics-guided video-based deep learning is a promising, cost-efficient, and operationally feasible approach for long-term coastal wave monitoring.

title: Measurement-Calibrated Multi-Camera Fusion for Vision-Based Indoor Localization
comment: This paper has been accepted for presentation at the IEEE 22st International Conference on Automation Science and Engineering (CASE 2026)
summary:
Indoor vision-based localization systems are affected by detection noise, occlusions, and limited camera coverage, leading to uncertainty at multiple stages of the pipeline. While multi-camera data fusion is widely used to mitigate these issues, it is typically treated as a black-box component and evaluated solely end-to-end, obscuring its mechanistic contributions. To address this gap, this work investigates whether explicitly characterizing single-camera localization errors can be leveraged to calibrate and optimize multi-camera data fusion.
  We introduce a measurement-calibrated fusion approach that integrates component-wise error quantification, specifically isolating homography calibration, human detection, and motion tracking. A component-wise evaluation is conducted to quantify error contributions from homography calibration, human detection, and motion tracking.
  Experimental results show that data fusion improves localization accuracy compared to single-camera baselines. While measurement-calibrated fusion provides only limited improvement in absolute accuracy over standard fusion, it substantially reduces trajectory variance and improves motion smoothness, which are critical for applications requiring stable and continuous motion estimates. These results highlight the value of explicit error characterization when designing data fusion strategies for vision-based indoor positioning systems.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a measurement-calibrated fusion approach for multi-camera indoor localization that explicitly characterizes single-camera errors (homography calibration, human detection, motion tracking) to optimize fusion. Results show limited absolute accuracy gains but significant improvements in trajectory smoothness and variance reduction.
AI.motivation: Indoor vision-based localization suffers from detection noise, occlusions, and limited coverage. Multi-camera fusion is typically treated as a black box, obscuring its mechanistic contributions. The authors aim to investigate whether explicitly characterizing single-camera errors can calibrate and optimize multi-camera fusion.
AI.method: A measurement-calibrated fusion approach that integrates component-wise error quantification across three sub-components: homography calibration, human detection, and motion tracking. Component-wise evaluation isolates each error contribution.
AI.result: Data fusion improves accuracy over single-camera baselines. Measurement-calibrated fusion offers limited improvement in absolute accuracy over standard fusion, but substantially reduces trajectory variance and improves motion smoothness.
AI.conclusion: Explicit error characterization is valuable when designing data fusion strategies for vision-based indoor positioning, particularly for applications needing stable and continuous motion estimates.

title: Predicting Cognitive Load from Speech and Interaction Dynamics in Dyadic Conversations
summary: Estimating cognitive load from speech has largely been studied in controlled laboratory settings, with limited understanding of its reliability in natural collaborative conversations. We investigate whether speech and interaction dynamics predict perceived cognitive load during dyadic conversations. We analyze audio from 53 dyads performing nine collaborative tasks and extract static acoustic, dynamic, and interaction features to train a two-head Gated Recurrent Unit encoder to predict cognitive load scores. Results show conversational interaction provides useful signals for predicting cognitive load related to time pressure, mental work, effort, and task performance. Temporal demand is associated with turn-taking dynamics such as overlap and speaker switch, while mental demand is linked to imbalanced participation between speakers. These findings highlight the importance of task structure and conversational interaction for modeling cognitive load in natural collaborative settings.

AI.tldr: This paper uses speech and interaction dynamics (turn-taking, speaker imbalance) to predict perceived cognitive load from dyadic conversations, showing that conversational features—especially temporal and mental demand—are reliable indicators in natural collaborative settings.
AI.motivation: Cognitive load estimation from speech has mostly been studied in controlled lab settings; the authors aim to understand its reliability in natural collaborative conversations, where real-world dynamics may differ significantly.
AI.method: 53 dyads performed 9 collaborative tasks; audio was recorded and features (static acoustic, dynamic, interaction) were extracted. A two-head Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) encoder was trained to predict perceived cognitive load scores.
AI.result: Conversational interaction signals predicted cognitive load dimensions including time pressure, mental work, effort, and task performance. Temporal demand correlated with turn-taking dynamics (overlap, speaker switch), while mental demand correlated with imbalanced participation.
AI.conclusion: Task structure and conversational interaction are crucial for modeling cognitive load in natural collaborative settings, highlighting the value of interaction features beyond static acoustic measures.

title: ReSum: Synergizing LLM Reasoning and Summarization with Reinforcement Learning
comment: 24 pages, including 13 pages of main text and 11 pages of appendix
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) is a central technique for improving long-horizon reasoning in Large Language Models (LLMs). However, existing RLVR methods often encourage unnecessarily long reasoning rollouts, which can degrade reasoning coherence and exhaust the available context budget. Existing approaches to long-context organization often depend on external mechanisms to organize rollouts, rather than enabling the model to manage its own reasoning trajectory. To address this limitation, we propose ReSum, a novel RLVR framework that enables LLMs to compress and organize their reasoning trajectories through self-summarization. Our pilot studies show that self-summarization stabilizes generation by lowering token-level entropy, and that introducing a ``summarization'' phrase can substantially mitigate errors propagated from an incorrect rollout prefix. Motivated by these findings, ReSum adopts a summarization-aware adaptive rollout mechanism that contrastively evaluates whether self-summarization benefits the ongoing reasoning process. Specifically, when the model spontaneously triggers self-summarization, ReSum masks the summarization phrase to create a contrastive branch; for non-summarization positions, it instead randomly injects the phrase to create a matched branch. We further design a summarization-aware advantage to enable finer-grained comparison between contrastive rollout trajectories. Extensive experiments show that ReSum improves performance at an average of 4\% while reducing rollout length by 18.6\%.

AI.tldr: ReSum is an RLVR framework that uses self-summarization to help LLMs compress and organize reasoning trajectories, improving performance by 4% while reducing rollout length by 18.6%.
AI.motivation: Existing RLVR methods encourage unnecessarily long reasoning rollouts, degrading coherence and exhausting context budgets; current approaches rely on external mechanisms rather than enabling models to self-organize their reasoning.
AI.method: ReSum uses a summarization-aware adaptive rollout mechanism that contrastively evaluates self-summarization benefits: masks summarization phrases for contrastive branches and randomly injects phrases for non-summarization positions, with a summarization-aware advantage for finer-grained comparison.
AI.result: Improves performance by an average of 4% while reducing rollout length by 18.6%.
AI.conclusion: Self-summarization is an effective internal mechanism for LLMs to manage reasoning trajectories, improving both efficiency and quality in RLVR.

title: EPM-JEPA: Operator-Side Experience Modulation in JEPA-Family World Models
comment: 16 pages, 5 figures, 9 tables, 5 code listings. Pre-registered experimental study with mechanism analysis
summary: JEPA-family world models use a static predictor whose weights do not adapt when test-time dynamics diverge from training. We compare two mechanisms for incorporating accumulated experience into a JEPA predictor under distribution shift: operand-side injection, where a compressed experience representation is added as a residual to the predictor's hidden state (EI-JEPA), and operator-side modulation, where the same representation generates low-rank weight deltas via LoRA applied to the predictor's weights (EPM-JEPA). On a pre-registered comparison (Moving MNIST, gravity shift), EPM-JEPA (D_shift^{n=50} = 0.7848 +/- 0.0078, three seeds) differs from EI-JEPA (0.8238) by delta = 4.74% - Outcome C: a null result - by our stated criterion, a valid outcome. As a secondary, non-pre-registered observation, EPM-JEPA improves 1.90% over a no-memory baseline (0.8000), consistently across seeds, while EI-JEPA underperforms the baseline, indicating the benefit is specific to weight-level modulation. Our primary contribution is a mechanism analysis: the D_shift^{n=50} trajectory reflects three independent dynamical processes - buffer cycling, EMA target drift, and an intrinsic LoRA settling transient of +0.021 - rather than convergence to equilibrium. These findings motivate PEM-JEPA, a physics-grounded successor addressing this dynamical-peak limitation.

AI.tldr: The paper compares two memory mechanisms for JEPA world models under distribution shift: operand-side (EI-JEPA) and operator-side (EPM-JEPA). The pre-registered comparison shows a null result (4.74% difference). However, EPM-JEPA improves 1.90% over no-memory baseline while EI-JEPA underperforms. The key contribution is a dynamical analysis showing the D_shift trajectory reflects three independent processes, motivating PEM-JEPA.
AI.motivation: Static predictors in JEPA world models cannot adapt when test-time dynamics diverge from training, motivating the need for mechanisms to incorporate accumulated experience under distribution shift.
AI.method: Two mechanisms are compared: operand-side injection (EI-JEPA) adds compressed experience as residual to hidden states; operator-side modulation (EPM-JEPA) generates low-rank weight deltas via LoRA. Tested on Moving MNIST with gravity shift.
AI.result: Pre-registered comparison shows null result (4.74% difference). Secondary analysis reveals EPM-JEPA improves 1.90% over no-memory baseline while EI-JEPA underperforms. The D_shift trajectory reflects three processes: buffer cycling, EMA target drift, and LoRA settling transient (+0.021).
AI.conclusion: Weight-level modulation (operator-side) benefits adaptation, but the dynamical-peak limitation motivates PEM-JEPA as a physics-grounded successor.

title: Can I Buy Your KV Cache?
summary: Right now, across the world, AI agents are repeating the same absurd act: to read one document, they each recompute it from scratch. Every agent re-runs prefill, the most compute-intensive step a large model takes, over identical text, only to rebuild a key-value (KV) cache identical to the one the agent before it just built. The same answer, computed a million times. We make a proposal that is almost offensively simple: compute it once. Let a publisher precompute a document's KV cache, and let every other agent buy the right to load it and skip prefill. It works, and it is token-exact: loading a precomputed KV and continuing matches prefilling from scratch (24/24 greedy tokens, and at the logits level), with no accuracy cost. On Qwen3-4B, reuse is 9-50x cheaper in compute than prefill, and the gap widens with length (prefill's attention scales with L^2), so a single reuse already pays it back. Then the part that matters: where the KV lives. Shipping it fails, because KV is nearly incompressible, so per-load egress costs more than the prefill it saves. Hosting it provider-side, exactly as production prompt-caching works, removes egress entirely. The size of the prize is set by our measured compute saving: serving one hot 3774-token document to 80M agents costs ~$1.5M to re-prefill but only ~$0.03M of reuse compute (49.7x less). The 0.1x cache-read tariff APIs charge passes a 10x discount to users while sitting inside this measured envelope, so the 10x is a floor that the measured ~50x compute saving clears, and the gap to the physical ~50x is provider margin: millions of dollars per popular document. We frame the resulting agent-native prefill CDN and leave lossless KV compression and a cross-party payment layer as the open problems.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes that instead of every AI agent re-computing the KV cache for the same document from scratch, publishers should precompute it once and let agents buy the right to load it, achieving 9-50x compute savings with no accuracy loss, though shipping the KV cache is impractical due to egress costs, so provider-side hosting (like prompt-caching) is the viable path.
AI.motivation: Current AI agents redundantly re-run prefill (the most compute-intensive step) for identical documents, rebuilding the same KV cache repeatedly, wasting massive amounts of compute and money.
AI.method: Let a publisher precompute a document's KV cache once, then let other agents load it to skip prefill entirely. The approach is token-exact—loading matches prefilling from scratch at both greedy token and logits levels with no accuracy degradation.
AI.result: On Qwen3-4B, reuse is 9-50x cheaper than prefill (gap widens with length due to L^2 attention scaling). Serving a 3774-token document to 80M agents costs ~$1.5M to re-prefill vs ~$0.03M reuse (49.7x less). However, shipping KV fails due to high egress costs; provider-side hosting removes this issue.
AI.conclusion: Proposes an agent-native prefill CDN architecture. Key open problems are lossless KV compression and a cross-party payment layer for cache access.

title: What's Old is New Again: Classical Dimensionality Reduction for Efficient Saliency-Guided Biometric Attack Detection
comment: 16 pages (8 main, 2 references, 6 appendix), 4 figures (3 main, 1 appendix), 13 tables (3 main, 10 appendix)
summary: Saliency-guided training is a paradigm in visual recognition that encourages models to focus on the most relevant image regions during learning. While its application in biometric presentation attack detection (PAD) has shown strong benefits in robustness and generalization, adoption is often limited by the high cost, domain specificity, and limited scalability of existing saliency acquisition methods, such as human annotations over a limited dataset. We present a novel, cost-efficient, and highly-scalable approach to saliency acquisition using maps inspired by classical dimensionality reduction techniques: PCA and LDA. Our proposed methods generate saliency maps directly from raw training data, requiring no human annotation nor domain knowledge. We contextualize the effectiveness of these saliency sources in three saliency-explored domains (iris PAD, synthetic face detection, fingerprint PAD) and demonstrate its scalability in two saliency-novel domains (fingerprint vein PAD and ID card PAD). Across all domains tested, models trained using dimensionality reduction-sourced saliency maps exceed baseline and sometimes SOTA saliency methods without any resource investment or domain-specific tooling. Our findings overcome an important yet unaddressed barrier to saliency-guided training for biometric attack detection and beyond.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a cost-efficient saliency acquisition method using PCA/LDA dimensionality reduction to generate saliency maps for biometric presentation attack detection (PAD). The approach requires no human annotation or domain knowledge, outperforms baselines across five biometric domains (iris, synthetic face, fingerprint, fingerprint vein, ID card PAD), addressing a key barrier to saliency-guided training adoption.
AI.motivation: Existing saliency-guided training in PAD shows strong benefits but adoption is limited by high cost, domain specificity, and limited scalability of current saliency acquisition methods (e.g., human annotations).
AI.method: The authors generate saliency maps using PCA and LDA dimensionality reduction directly from raw training data, requiring no human annotation or domain knowledge. These maps are used for saliency-guided training in biometric PAD.
AI.result: Models trained with dimensionality-reduction-based saliency maps exceed baselines and sometimes SOTA saliency methods across five domains (iris PAD, synthetic face detection, fingerprint PAD, fingerprint vein PAD, ID card PAD), demonstrating scalability and effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach overcomes an important barrier to saliency-guided training adoption by providing a cost-efficient, scalable, annotation-free saliency acquisition method, with demonstrated effectiveness across multiple biometric attack detection domains.

title: Exposure Bias as Epistemic Underidentification in Recursive Forecasting
summary: Recursive multi-step forecasting is usually framed as distribution shift: models are trained on observed histories but deployed on their own predictions. We show this framing is incomplete by proving that, under partial observability or state truncation, recursive rollout is also an epistemic underidentification problem. Even with deterministic latent dynamics, one-step Bayes supervision identifies behavior only on observed contexts and need not identify the deployed recursive predictor once rollout queries self-generated induced states whose correct local targets are not determined by numeric state alone. We formalize this with induced states $Z$ and provenance variables $P$, and derive a decomposition of induced-state error into teacher-forcing/rollout mismatch, representation--class approximation, and provenance information gaps. Empirically, we show that rollout enters a distinct induced-state regime, that fixed induced states define a distinct local corrective task, and that closed-loop gains arise not only from local adaptation but also from changing the induced states visited during rollout. Using a simple binary provenance encoding, provenance-aware correction can further improve performance, though gains are conditional rather than uniform. These results recast exposure bias as reasoning under self-induced epistemic uncertainty.

AI.tldr: This paper challenges the framing of recursive multi-step forecasting as purely a distribution shift problem, showing it is also an epistemic underidentification issue under partial observability or state truncation. The authors formalize induced states and provenance variables, decompose error sources, and empirically demonstrate that rollout enters distinct induced-state regimes where provenance-aware correction yields conditional improvements.
AI.motivation: The authors argue that the common framing of recursive multi-step forecasting as distribution shift (training on observed histories but deploying on self-generated predictions) is incomplete. They aim to show that under partial observability or state truncation, this is fundamentally an epistemic underidentification problem where one-step Bayes supervision fails to identify the correct recursive predictor.
AI.method: The paper formalizes the problem using induced states Z and provenance variables P, deriving a decomposition of induced-state error into three components: teacher-forcing/rollout mismatch, representation-class approximation, and provenance information gaps. Empirical experiments demonstrate distinct induced-state regimes during rollout and the value of provenance-aware correction.
AI.result: Empirically shows: (1) rollout enters a distinct induced-state regime different from teacher-forcing; (2) fixed induced states define a distinct local corrective task; (3) closed-loop gains arise from both local adaptation and changing induced states visited during rollout; (4) provenance-aware binary encoding yields conditional improvements rather than uniform gains.
AI.conclusion: The paper recasts exposure bias as reasoning under self-induced epistemic uncertainty, providing a more nuanced understanding of why recursive multi-step forecasting fails and suggesting that provenance information can help mitigate these issues.

title: IterCAD: An Iterative Multimodal Agent for Visually-Grounded CAD Generation and Editing
summary: Computer-Aided Design is pivotal in modern manufacturing, yet existing automated methods predominantly rely on open-loop, one-shot generation, creating a mismatch with iterative real-world practices. In this paper, we present IterCAD, a unified multimodal agent framework for closed-loop, interactive CAD generation and editing. We formulate the task as a multi-turn interaction between a multimodal agent and an executable CAD sandbox, covering three tasks: Drawing-to-Code, Text-to-Code, and Interactive Editing. To support this, we develop a data synthesis pipeline incorporating advanced industrial manufacturing features to generate standard-compliant multi-view engineering drawings, complex code-editing tasks, and high-fidelity interaction trajectories. We optimize the agent via progressive SFT followed by geometry-aware reinforcement learning with viable-prefix masking to enhance code executability and geometric fidelity. Finally, we introduce the IterCAD-Bench evaluation suite and propose the Chamfer Distance Tolerance-Recall (CD-TR) curve alongside its AUC-TR metric, establishing a survivor-bias-free standard that unifies code validity and geometric precision. Extensive experiments demonstrate that IterCAD achieves highly competitive performance across multiple benchmarks, significantly outperforming existing approaches in both code executability and geometric precision, while exhibiting superior capabilities in closed-loop iterative refinement.

AI.tldr: IterCAD is a unified multimodal agent framework for closed-loop, interactive CAD generation and editing, achieving superior performance across multiple benchmarks in code executability and geometric precision.
AI.motivation: Existing automated CAD methods rely on open-loop, one-shot generation, which mismatches the iterative nature of real-world design practices. This motivates the need for a closed-loop, interactive CAD system.
AI.method: The paper proposes IterCAD, a multimodal agent framework operating via multi-turn interaction with a CAD sandbox. It uses a data synthesis pipeline with industrial manufacturing features, progressive SFT optimization, and geometry-aware reinforcement learning with viable-prefix masking. A new evaluation metric (CD-TR curve and AUC-TR) is also introduced.
AI.result: IterCAD achieves highly competitive performance across multiple benchmarks, significantly outperforming existing approaches in both code executability and geometric precision, with superior closed-loop iterative refinement capabilities.
AI.conclusion: The closed-loop, interactive approach with geometry-aware learning and novel evaluation metrics establishes a new standard for CAD generation, effectively bridging the gap between automated methods and real-world iterative design practices.

title: Edit the Bits, Diff the Codes: Bitwise Residual Editing for Visual Autoregressive Models
summary: Text-guided image editing with visual autoregressive (VAR) generators requires controlling both what the model samples and where the sampled change is written back into the image code. Existing VAR editors mainly operate on token streams, features, or flat next-token logits, leaving two native structures of bitwise-residual VAR models underused: the per-bit Bernoulli prediction head and the additive multi-scale residual code field from which the image is assembled. We propose BitResEdit, a training-free editor for bitwise-residual VAR generators such as Infinity. BitEdit performs source-negative guidance by tilting the post-CFG per-bit log-odds along a source--target contrast computed on a shared edited prefix, then projects each update into a closed-form Bernoulli-KL trust region around the clean CFG sampler. ResEdit converts the sampled bits into per-scale continuous-code residuals, gates them with a localization mask, and re-injects them through the generator's native sum-of-scales. Together they couple decision-time bit guidance with combination-time code composition, so masked-out latent features are preserved exactly by code arithmetic while localized, scale-aware edits are applied inside the target region. On PIE-Bench with Infinity-2B, BitResEdit attains the strongest text alignment among same-backbone VAR editors, improving CLIP on the edited region by +1.07 over the strongest prior editor while keeping background preservation competitive with it. Ablations show BitEdit and ResEdit play complementary roles in target alignment and background preservation.

AI.tldr: BitResEdit is a training-free editor for bitwise-residual VAR models that combines bit-level guidance (BitEdit) and scale-aware code composition (ResEdit) to achieve strong text alignment and background preservation for text-guided image editing.
AI.motivation: Existing VAR editors underuse two native structures of bitwise-residual VAR models: the per-bit Bernoulli prediction head and the additive multi-scale residual code field.
AI.method: BitEdit performs source-negative guidance via post-CFG per-bit log-odds with Bernoulli-KL trust region; ResEdit converts bits to per-scale residuals, gates with a localization mask, and re-injects via sum-of-scales.
AI.result: On PIE-Bench with Infinity-2B, BitResEdit improves CLIP on edited region by +1.07 over the strongest prior VAR editor while keeping background preservation competitive.
AI.conclusion: BitEdit and ResEdit play complementary roles in target alignment and background preservation, enabling training-free editing with strong performance.

title: DeepJEB++: Foundation Model-Driven Large-Scale 3D Engineering Dataset via 2D Latent Space Augmentation
comment: 16 pages, 14 figures. Submitted to ASME Journal of Mechanical Design
summary: Data-driven engineering design is constrained by the lack of large-scale 3D datasets that pair geometry with physics-based performance labels. In particular, existing 3D data augmentation techniques have limitations in preserving subtle and diverse geometric variations, and it remains difficult to automate the subsequent simulation-labeling process, where boundary conditions vary depending on the generated geometry. We present DeepJEB++, a foundation-model-driven data-augmentation framework that expands a small seed set of jet engine brackets into a large, simulation-labeled 3D dataset under constrained resources. Our key idea is to augment in the data-rich 2D latent space, then transfer to 3D. In Stage 1, we fine-tune a pretrained 2D latent diffusion model on multi-view renders and synthesize novel views by latent interpolation, retaining manufacturable designs through a vision-language-model (VLM) quality filter. In Stage 2, the validated images are lifted to 3D meshes by a domain-adapted generative foundation model. In Stage 3, an automated pipeline recognizes the load and bolt interfaces on each mesh and assigns finite-element labels -- mass, stress, and displacement -- without manual intervention. We assess augmentation quality along three intrinsic axes: manufacturability, label fidelity against the SimJEB ground truth, and distributional consistency. Starting from fewer than 400 seed designs, DeepJEB++ yields 15,360 simulation-labeled 3D brackets -- a 40x expansion -- using a single GPU per stage. The dataset will be made publicly available to support reproducible engineering-AI research.

AI.tldr: DeepJEB++ is a foundation-model-driven framework that expands a small seed set of jet engine brackets (under 400 designs) into a large, simulation-labeled 3D dataset (15,360 designs, 40x expansion) by augmenting in 2D latent space, lifting to 3D, and automating finite-element labeling, using a single GPU per stage.
AI.motivation: Data-driven engineering design is limited by the lack of large-scale 3D datasets pairing geometry with physics-based performance labels. Existing 3D augmentation techniques struggle to preserve subtle geometric variations, and automating simulation labeling with varying boundary conditions remains difficult.
AI.method: Stage 1: Fine-tune a pretrained 2D latent diffusion model on multi-view renders and synthesize novel views via latent interpolation, with VLM-based manufacturability filtering. Stage 2: Lift validated 2D images to 3D meshes using a domain-adapted generative foundation model. Stage 3: Automatically recognize load/bolt interfaces on each mesh and assign finite-element labels (mass, stress, displacement).
AI.result: From fewer than 400 seed designs, DeepJEB++ produces 15,360 simulation-labeled 3D brackets (40x expansion) using a single GPU per stage. Quality is assessed via manufacturability, label fidelity against SimJEB ground truth, and distributional consistency.
AI.conclusion: DeepJEB++ demonstrates an effective data augmentation pipeline for engineering design, enabling large-scale labeled 3D dataset creation under constrained resources. The dataset will be made publicly available to support reproducible engineering-AI research.

title: A Quantitative Experimental Repeated Measures Study of Training Dynamics in a Small Llama Style Language Model Under a Compute-Aware Token Budget
summary: This study examines training dynamics in a small Llama-style language model trained under a fixed, compute-constrained token budget. Rather than evaluating efficiency solely through endpoint performance, the study uses a quantitative experimental repeated measures design to analyze how validation loss, validation perplexity, rolling volatility, backslide behavior, spike behavior, and between-seed variability change across token-based training intervals. Six independent training runs were conducted on a 4.26-million-parameter model using the TinyStories corpus, CPU-based full-precision training, and a target budget of approximately 20 million cumulative training tokens. Metrics were collected across 21 intervals, producing 126 seed-by-interval observations. Repeated measures ANOVA showed statistically significant interval effects for validation loss, validation perplexity, and rolling volatility. Descriptive trajectories revealed rapid early improvement followed by non-monotonic degradation during later training intervals. Mean validation loss decreased from 8.3552 at initialization to 2.7996 near 4 million tokens, but increased to 3.9010 by the final checkpoint. Validation perplexity followed the same pattern, falling sharply early in training before rising later. Derived telemetry further showed recurrent validation-loss backslides and no interval-summary evidence of a stable phase under the predefined criteria. These findings suggest that compute-aware language model evaluation should examine training trajectories rather than endpoint metrics alone. In constrained compute settings, additional token exposure may increase computational cost without producing proportional generalization gains, and interval-level telemetry can reveal instability, regression, and diminishing returns that final metrics may obscure.

AI.tldr: A study of a small Llama-style LM under a fixed compute budget shows that training beyond an early optimum leads to degradation in validation loss/perplexity and increased instability, arguing for evaluating training trajectories rather than just endpoint metrics.
AI.motivation: To challenge the common practice of evaluating language model efficiency solely through final performance metrics, and to demonstrate that in compute-constrained settings, additional training tokens can degrade generalization and introduce instability that endpoint metrics fail to capture.
AI.method: Used a repeated measures experimental design with a 4.26M-parameter Llama-style model trained on TinyStories for ~20M tokens. Six independent runs, 21 measurement intervals (126 observations). Metrics: validation loss, perplexity, rolling volatility, backslide/spike behavior, between-seed variability. Analyzed via repeated measures ANOVA and descriptive trajectory analysis.
AI.result: Statistically significant interval effects for loss, perplexity, and volatility. Mean validation loss improved from 8.36 to 2.80 (at ~4M tokens) but degraded to 3.90 by the end. Perplexity mirrored this U-shaped trajectory. Recurrent backslides and no stable phase were observed under predefined criteria.
AI.conclusion: Compute-aware evaluation should examine full training trajectories rather than only final metrics. Under constrained compute budgets, extra tokens may increase cost without proportional gains, and interval-level telemetry reveals instability and diminishing returns that endpoint metrics obscure.

title: Contrast-Informed Augmentation and Domain-Adversarial Training for Adult-to-Neonatal MR Reconstruction Generalization
summary: Purpose: To investigate whether contrast-informed data augmentation and domain-adversarial training improve the adult-to-neonatal generalization of the E2E-VarNet. Methods: Three training regimes were investigated: (1) adult-only training with unaugmented adult data, (2) mixed training with paired unaugmented and neonatal-informed augmented adult data, and (3) mixed training with a domain-adversarial objective. Models were trained on retrospectively undersampled multi-coil adult T2-weighted brain MR data and evaluated on neonatal and adult test data at acceleration factors $R=4$ and $R=8$ using quantitative metrics and qualitative evaluation. Feature analyses assessed whether domain-adversarial training altered the latent representations of unaugmented adult, augmented adult, and neonatal test samples. Results: Mixed training (Mixed) and mixed domain-adversarial training (Mixed-DAT) outperformed unaugmented adult-only training (Unaug-Only) when evaluated on neonatal data. At R=4, Mixed-DAT achieved the best performance (SSIM = 0.924 +/- 0.027, PSNR = 33.98 +/- 1.15 dB). At R=8, Mixed-DAT performed best when measured using SSIM (0.848 +/- 0.031 vs. 0.766 +/- 0.037 for Unaug-Only and 0.814 +/- 0.035 for Mixed) and Mixed performed best when measured using PSNR (29.56 +/- 0.83 dB vs. 26.26 +/- 0.78 dB for Unaug-Only and 29.43 +/- 0.83 dB for Mixed-DAT). Qualitative assessment of t-SNE plots suggested that Mixed-DAT increased the overlap among the latent representations of the unaugmented adult, augmented adult, and neonatal test data. Conclusion: Contrast-informed augmentation and domain-adversarial training improved adult-to-neonatal generalization of deep learning-based MR reconstruction. These findings suggest that contrast-informed data augmentation combined with adversarial training may improve robustness to domain shift in undersampled neonatal MR reconstruction.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates whether contrast-informed data augmentation and domain-adversarial training can improve the generalization of the E2E-VarNet model from adult to neonatal brain MRI reconstruction. Mixed training with augmented data and domain-adversarial objectives outperformed adult-only training, with Mixed-DAT achieving best SSIM at both R=4 and R=8, and Mixed achieving best PSNR at R=8. T-SNE analysis showed increased overlap of latent representations, indicating reduced domain shift.
AI.motivation: To address the domain shift problem in deep learning-based MR reconstruction, specifically the poor generalization of models trained on adult data to neonatal data, by leveraging contrast-informed data augmentation and domain-adversarial training techniques.
AI.method: Three training regimes for E2E-VarNet were compared: (1) adult-only training with unaugmented data, (2) mixed training with unaugmented adult + neonatal-informed augmented adult data, and (3) mixed training with a domain-adversarial objective. Models were trained on retrospectively undersampled multi-coil adult T2-weighted brain MR data and evaluated on neonatal and adult test data at R=4 and R=8.
AI.result: Mixed and Mixed-DAT outperformed Unaug-Only on neonatal data. At R=4, Mixed-DAT achieved best performance (SSIM=0.924±0.027, PSNR=33.98±1.15 dB). At R=8, Mixed-DAT had best SSIM (0.848±0.031) while Mixed had best PSNR (29.56±0.83 dB). T-SNE plots showed Mixed-DAT increased overlap among latent representations across domains.
AI.conclusion: Contrast-informed augmentation and domain-adversarial training improve adult-to-neonatal generalization of DL-based MR reconstruction. Combining contrast-informed data augmentation with adversarial training may enhance robustness to domain shift in undersampled neonatal MR reconstruction.

title: Reliability of Probabilistic Emulation of Physical Systems
summary: Two dominant approaches have emerged for generating probabilistic forecasts of physical systems: generative models, such as diffusion or flow matching; and ensembles of deterministic models with stochasticity injected, trained using the continuous ranked probability score (CRPS) loss. While both approaches have demonstrated strong predictive accuracy, the reliability of their uncertainties has not been systematically assessed. We address this gap by developing a framework to evaluate both approaches across diverse 2D spatiotemporal physical systems, under matched model size and computational budget. We assess the reliability of probabilistic emulation by inspecting the empirical coverage of predictive intervals, while also considering accuracy and computational efficiency metrics. CRPS-trained ensembles typically achieve more reliable uncertainties on both single-step prediction and autoregressive rollouts, demonstrating better coverage than the standard alternative of training generative models in a latent space. Moreover, the CRPS approach offers significantly faster inference. When generative models are trained in ambient rather than a compressed latent space, which is often infeasible for high-dimensional problems, they exhibit comparable coverage to CRPS-trained ensembles, though with substantially larger inference latency. In contrast, when CRPS-trained ensembles are trained in latent space they do not show a marked degradation in coverage with respect to ambient space. Both generative models and CRPS-trained ensembles demonstrate good predictive accuracy. To facilitate future research and application, we release AutoCast, a modular framework implementing both generative models and CRPS-trained ensembles, alongside AutoSim, a flexible dataset generation package for rapid prototyping.

AI.tldr: This paper systematically compares CRPS-trained ensembles and generative models (diffusion/flow matching) for probabilistic forecasting of 2D spatiotemporal physical systems, finding that CRPS-trained ensembles provide more reliable uncertainties with faster inference, while latent-space training degrades generative models less than ensembles.
AI.motivation: Both generative models and CRPS-trained ensembles have shown strong predictive accuracy for probabilistic forecasting, but their uncertainty reliability has never been systematically assessed under matched conditions.
AI.method: The authors develop a unified evaluation framework comparing CRPS-trained ensembles and generative models across diverse 2D spatiotemporal physical systems, controlling for model size and computational budget, and assessing reliability via empirical coverage of predictive intervals, accuracy, and computational efficiency.
AI.result: CRPS-trained ensembles yield more reliable uncertainties and faster inference; generative models in ambient space match ensemble coverage but with higher latency; latent-space training degrades coverage less for ensembles than for generative models.
AI.conclusion: CRPS-trained ensembles are preferred for reliable uncertainty and fast inference; the released AutoCast and AutoSim frameworks facilitate future research and prototyping.

title: MiniMax Sparse Attention
summary: Ultra-long-context capability is becoming indispensable for frontier LLMs: agentic workflows, repository-scale code reasoning, and persistent memory all require the model to jointly attend over hundreds of thousands to millions of tokens, yet the quadratic cost of softmax attention makes this untenable at deployment scale. We introduce MiniMax Sparse Attention (MSA), a blockwise sparse attention built upon Grouped Query Attention (GQA). A lightweight Index Branch scores key-value blocks and independently selects a Top-k subset for each GQA group, enabling group-specific sparse retrieval while maintaining efficient block-level execution; the Main Branch then performs exact block-sparse attention over only the selected blocks. Designed around a principle of simplicity and scalability, MSA is deliberately streamlined, making it straightforward to deploy efficiently across a broad range of GPUs. To translate sparsity into practical speedups, we co-design MSA with a GPU execution path that uses exp-free Top-k selection and KV-outer sparse attention to improve tensor-core utilization under block-granular access. On a 109B-parameter model with native multimodal training, MSA performs on par with GQA while reducing per-token attention compute by 28.4x at 1M context. Paired with our co-designed kernel, MSA achieves 14.2x prefill and 7.6x decoding wall-clock speedups on H800. Our inference kernel is available at: https://github.com/MiniMax-AI/MSA. A production-grade natively multimodal model powered by MSA has been publicly released at: https://huggingface.co/MiniMaxAI/MiniMax-M3.

AI.tldr: MiniMax Sparse Attention (MSA) replaces quadratic softmax attention with blockwise sparse attention for ultra-long-context LLMs. It uses a lightweight Index Branch to select Top-k key-value blocks per GQA group, then performs exact sparse attention on only those blocks. At 1M context on a 109B multimodal model, it matches GQA quality while reducing attention compute by 28.4x and achieving 14.2x prefill / 7.6x decoding speedups on H800 GPUs.
AI.motivation: Ultra-long-context capabilities are increasingly essential for frontier LLMs (agentic workflows, repository-scale code reasoning, persistent memory), but standard softmax attention's quadratic cost makes deployment at scale prohibitive.
AI.method: MSA is a blockwise sparse attention built on GQA: a lightweight Index Branch scores KV-blocks and selects a Top-k subset per GQA group, enabling group-specific sparse retrieval; then the Main Branch performs exact block-sparse attention only over selected blocks. Co-designed GPU kernels use exp-free Top-k selection and KV-outer sparse attention to improve tensor-core utilization under block-granular access.
AI.result: On a 109B-parameter natively multimodal model, MSA performs on par with GQA while reducing per-token attention compute by 28.4x at 1M context. Co-designed kernels achieve 14.2x prefill and 7.6x decoding wall-clock speedups on H800 GPUs.
AI.conclusion: MSA provides a simple, scalable, and deployment-friendly sparse attention that delivers substantial practical speedups for ultra-long-context inference, and both the inference kernel and a production-grade multimodal model powered by MSA have been publicly released.

title: EvTexture++: Event-Driven Texture Enhancement for Video Super-Resolution
comment: IEEE TPAMI 2026. Extended version of arXiv:2406.13457 (ICML 2024). Project page: https://dachunkai.github.io/evtexture-project-page/
summary: Event-based vision has drawn increasing attention owing to its distinctive properties, including ultra-high temporal resolution and extreme dynamic range. Recent works have introduced it to video super-resolution (VSR) to enhance flow estimation and temporal alignment. In contrast, this paper shifts the focus of event signals from motion refinement to texture enhancement in VSR. We propose EvTexture++, the first event-driven framework dedicated to texture enhancement in VSR. It leverages high-frequency spatiotemporal details from events to improve texture recovery. EvTexture++ incorporates a customized texture enhancement branch, along with an iterative texture enhancement module that progressively exploits high-temporal-resolution event information for texture restoration. This enables gradual refinement of texture regions across iterations, yielding more accurate and detailed high-resolution outputs. Besides intra-frame texture recovery, large motions could degrade inter-frame temporal consistency, particularly in texture regions, leading to texture flickering. To mitigate this, we further exploit the continuous-time motion cues of events to enhance temporal consistency, introducing a temporal texture alignment module that estimates event-guided texture-aware flow for precise inter-frame texture alignment. Moreover, EvTexture++ is designed as a plug-and-play tool to flexibly boost the performance of existing VSR models. Experiments on five datasets demonstrate that EvTexture++ achieves state-of-the-art performance. When integrated into recent VSR models, it yields significant improvements, with gains of up to 1.55 dB in PSNR on the texture-rich Vid4 dataset. Code: https://github.com/DachunKai/EvTexture.

AI.tldr: EvTexture++ is an event-driven texture enhancement framework for video super-resolution (VSR). It uses high-frequency event signals to improve texture recovery, introduces iterative texture enhancement and temporal texture alignment modules, and serves as a plug-and-play tool that boosts existing VSR models by up to 1.55 dB PSNR.
AI.motivation: Prior event-based VSR works used events mainly for motion refinement and flow estimation. This paper shifts the focus to leveraging events specifically for texture enhancement, addressing both intra-frame texture recovery and inter-frame temporal consistency (texture flickering) in VSR.
AI.method: EvTexture++ proposes: (1) a texture enhancement branch with an iterative texture enhancement module that progressively uses high-temporal-resolution event data to refine textures; (2) a temporal texture alignment module that leverages continuous-time event motion cues to compute event-guided texture-aware flow for precise inter-frame alignment; (3) a plug-and-play design compatible with existing VSR models.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance on five datasets. When plugged into recent VSR models, it achieves up to 1.55 dB PSNR improvement on the texture-rich Vid4 dataset.
AI.conclusion: Events are highly effective for texture enhancement in VSR beyond motion refinement. The proposed framework significantly improves texture quality and temporal consistency, and its plug-and-play nature makes it widely applicable.

title: scLLM-DSC: LLM-Knowledge Enhanced Cross-Modal Deep Structural Clustering for Single-Cell RNA Sequencing
summary: Clustering is fundamental to scRNA-seq analysis, serving as a cornerstone for identifying cell populations and resolving tissue heterogeneity. However, existing methods focus on mining numerical statistical patterns, suffering from semantic agnosticism by neglecting the intrinsic biological functions encoded by genes. While Large Language Models (LLMs) offer promising semantic capabilities, their direct adaptation to cell clustering is hindered by the structural mismatch between generative pre-training objectives and discriminative downstream tasks. To bridge this gap, we propose scLLM-DSC, a novel LLM-Knowledge Enhanced Cross-Modal Deep Structural Clustering framework. Diverging from data-driven paradigms, scLLM-DSC establishes a semantically-grounded representation by synergizing two views: a Knowledge-Driven Semantic View derived from NCBI gene priors and contextualized Cell2Sentence embeddings, and a Structure-Aware Topological View extracted via a graph-guided encoder. Crucially, we introduce a cross-modal contrastive alignment mechanism to enforce consistency between biological semantics and transcriptomic features within a unified latent space. Extensive benchmarks demonstrate that scLLM-DSC significantly outperforms eleven state-of-the-art baselines in clustering accuracy.

AI.tldr: scLLM-DSC is a clustering framework for scRNA-seq that integrates LLM-derived biological semantics with structural transcriptomic features via cross-modal contrastive alignment, outperforming 11 state-of-the-art methods.
AI.motivation: Existing clustering methods ignore biological semantics by focusing only on numerical/statistical patterns; LLMs have semantic capabilities but their generative pre-training doesn't directly suit discriminative clustering tasks.
AI.method: Proposes a dual-view framework: (1) Knowledge-Driven Semantic View using NCBI gene priors + Cell2Sentence embeddings, and (2) Structure-Aware Topological View via a graph-guided encoder, aligned via cross-modal contrastive learning.
AI.result: Significantly outperforms eleven state-of-the-art baselines in clustering accuracy.
AI.conclusion: LLM-enhanced cross-modal structural clustering bridges the gap between semantic knowledge and transcriptomic data, proving effective for cell clustering.

title: Neuro-Symbolic Agents for Regulated Process Automation: Challenges and Research Agenda
comment: Accepted as a poster in NILA Workshop @ IJCAI-ECAI 2026
summary: LLM-based agents are entering regulated industries where they automate judgment intensive quality management processes. We argue that symbolic structures already embedded in these domains, including regulations, typed process models, and compliance constraints, should be treated not merely as external monitoring mechanisms but as core architectural components that shape the agent's decision-making and behavior. We propose compliance-by-construction as a complementary paradigm to guardrail-based monitoring: a structural foundation that prevents control-flow violations, while guardrails remain essential for catching semantic errors. We identify a structured set of neuro-symbolic research challenges on foundational and capability level and show that addressing them jointly enables compliance-by-construction. We call on the neuro-symbolic community to engage with regulated process automation as a high impact research domain.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes "compliance-by-construction" as an architectural paradigm for LLM-based agents in regulated industries, integrating symbolic structures (regulations, process models, compliance constraints) directly into agent decision-making rather than using them only as external monitors.
AI.motivation: LLM-based agents are entering regulated industries requiring judgment-intensive quality management, yet existing approaches treat compliance as external monitoring rather than intrinsic to agent architecture, risking control-flow violations.
AI.method: Proposes compliance-by-construction as a neuro-symbolic paradigm where symbolic domain structures (regulations, typed process models, compliance constraints) serve as core architectural components shaping agent behavior, complemented by guardrails for semantic errors.
AI.result: Identifies a structured set of neuro-symbolic research challenges at foundational and capability levels that must be jointly addressed to enable compliance-by-construction.
AI.conclusion: Calls on the neuro-symbolic community to pursue regulated process automation as a high-impact research domain.

title: Towards Effective Waste Segmentation for Automated Waste Recycling in Cluttered Background
summary: Rapid expansion of urban areas and population growth is causing an immense increase in waste production, which demands the need for efficient and automated waste management. In this scenario, automated waste recycling (AWR) using deep learning methods can assist humans in optimal waste management. Recent deep learning approaches for AWR provide promising waste segmentation performance, however, these methods rely on large backbone networks that are inefficient for AWR systems and suffer from performance deterioration in cluttered scenes. To this end, an optimal waste segmentation network is introduced which effectively utilizes the spatial domain to capture localized structural dependencies and the spectral domain to efficiently extract global contextual relationships. This cascaded design allows the network to progressively leverage both local and global representations across complementary domains to highlight the semantic information necessary for effective segmentation of various waste objects. Furthermore, auxiliary feature enhancement module (AFEM) is introduced to enhance the target objects' boundaries and blob amplification for better segmentation in cluttered scenarios. Extensive experimentation on ZeroWaste-aug, ZeroWaste-f and SpectralWaste datasets reveals the merits of the proposed method.

AI.tldr: A novel waste segmentation network combining spatial and spectral domain features with an auxiliary feature enhancement module to improve waste segmentation performance in cluttered scenes for automated waste recycling.
AI.motivation: Existing deep learning methods for automated waste recycling rely on large backbone networks that are inefficient and suffer from performance degradation in cluttered scenes, motivating the need for a more efficient and robust solution.
AI.method: A cascaded network design that leverages spatial domain for local structural dependencies and spectral domain for global contextual relationships, plus an Auxiliary Feature Enhancement Module (AFEM) to improve boundary detection and blob amplification in cluttered scenarios.
AI.result: Evaluated on ZeroWaste-aug, ZeroWaste-f, and SpectralWaste datasets, demonstrating superior performance over existing methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed spatial-spectral cascaded network with AFEM effectively addresses inefficiency and cluttered scene challenges in automated waste segmentation.

title: CausalMoE: A Billion-Scale Multimodal Foundation Model for Granger Causal Discovery with Pattern-Routed Heterogeneous Experts
summary: Granger Causal Discovery (GCD) is fundamental for analyzing temporal dependencies in complex systems. However, existing neural GCD methods predominantly rely on a "one-size-fits-all" paradigm, struggling to capture distribution shifts and dynamic regime changes inherent in real-world time series. This often leads to entangled representations and spurious causal graphs. In this paper, we propose CausalMoE, a billion-scale multimodal Granger causal foundation model that explicitly models patch-level heterogeneity. CausalMoE introduces a Pattern-Routed Mixture of Heterogeneous Experts, which dynamically identifies latent temporal patterns and routes patches to specialized domain experts, effectively decoupling regime-specific mechanisms from shared dynamics. To ensure interpretable graph recovery, we design a Causality-Aware Self-Attention mechanism operating across variables, yielding sparse Granger causal graphs via proximal optimization. Furthermore, CausalMoE is the first to integrate LLMs and VLMs to align numerical signals with textual and visual priors, regularizing causal estimation in complex scenarios. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CausalMoE establishes a new state-of-the-art on fully supervised benchmarks, while effectively generalizing to few-shot settings where traditional methods fail.

AI.tldr: CausalMoE is a billion-scale Granger causal foundation model using Mixture of Heterogeneous Experts and multimodal alignment (LLMs+VLMs) to handle distribution shifts and regime changes in time series, achieving SOTA on supervised and few-shot causal discovery benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing neural GCD methods rely on a "one-size-fits-all" paradigm that fails to capture distribution shifts and dynamic regime changes in real-world time series, leading to entangled representations and spurious causal graphs.
AI.method: (1) Pattern-Routed Mixture of Heterogeneous Experts for identifying latent temporal patterns and decoupling regime-specific mechanisms; (2) Causality-Aware Self-Attention with proximal optimization for sparse Granger causal graph recovery; (3) Integration of LLMs and VLMs to align numerical signals with textual and visual priors.
AI.result: Establishes new SOTA on fully supervised benchmarks and generalizes effectively to few-shot settings where traditional methods fail.
AI.conclusion: CausalMoE demonstrates that explicitly modeling patch-level heterogeneity and incorporating multimodal priors significantly improves Granger causal discovery under distribution shifts and limited data scenarios.

title: Optimizing Appliance Scheduling for Solar Energy Management Using Metaheuristic Algorithms
comment: 9 pages; full results and methodology for poster paper accepted to GECCO 2026
summary: Renewable energy is essential for meeting future energy demands; however, solar energy generation, which occurs only during daylight hours often does not align with household consumption patterns. Appliances such as cookers, washing machines, and dryers are typically operated according to user preferred schedules rather than solar energy availability, creating a scheduling optimization problem. The objective is to determine optimal appliance start times to maximize renewable energy utilization while minimizing user inconvenience and adhering to system constraints. This paper presents a metaheuristic approach using Iterated Local Search (ILS) and Simulated Annealing (SA) to optimize appliance start times, while considering appliance operating durations, power consumption, inverter limit, battery state of charge constraints, and solar generation forecasts. Unlike most existing work, the scheduling is extended beyond a single day to accommodate unfinished tasks from previous days (spillover), ensuring operational continuity and enabling sequential operation across multiple days. Experimental results show that the sequential multi-day scheduling framework effectively manages system constraints while ensuring user convenience under exclusive solar generation. These findings also open opportunities for future research on multi-objective trade-offs between investment in equipment of various sizes, return on that investment, and user satisfaction.

AI.tldr: This paper uses Iterated Local Search and Simulated Annealing to optimize appliance scheduling for multi-day solar energy utilization, addressing spillover tasks and balancing renewable use with user convenience.
AI.motivation: Solar energy generation (daytime only) often mismatches household appliance usage patterns (user-preferred schedules), creating a need to optimize start times to maximize renewable utilization while minimizing inconvenience.
AI.method: The authors apply Iterated Local Search (ILS) and Simulated Annealing (SA) metaheuristics to optimize appliance start times, considering operating durations, power consumption, inverter limits, battery state of charge, and solar forecasts. The scheduling is extended beyond a single day to handle spillover tasks.
AI.result: The sequential multi-day scheduling framework effectively manages system constraints while ensuring user convenience under exclusive solar generation.
AI.conclusion: The approach opens opportunities for future research on multi-objective trade-offs between equipment investment size, return on investment, and user satisfaction.

title: Revisiting Vehicle Color Recognition in Long-Tailed Surveillance Scenarios
comment: Accepted for presentation at the 2026 International Conference on Pattern Recognition (ICPR) - V3SC Workshop
summary: Vehicle color recognition is an important cue for vehicle identification in surveillance systems, especially when license plates are illegible due to low resolution, occlusion, motion blur, or poor illumination. However, real-world vehicle color distributions are highly imbalanced, making overall accuracy insufficient to assess performance on rare but operationally relevant colors. This paper presents a comprehensive study of vehicle color recognition under severe class imbalance using UFPR-VeSV, a challenging real-world surveillance dataset. We investigate synthetic minority-class augmentation through two off-the-shelf generative strategies: text-conditioned image generation with RunDiffusion/JuggernautXL and image-conditioned color editing with Gemini 2.0 Flash. The curated synthetic data are combined with modern visual representations, loss reweighting, learning-rate scheduling, color-safe augmentation, foreground-aware preprocessing, and ensemble fusion. The bestperforming approach achieves 94.6% micro accuracy and 79.7% macro accuracy, improving macro accuracy by 8.2 percentage points over recent literature. A manual error analysis further shows that many remaining failures are visually ambiguous even for human annotators, highlighting the practical limits of color-based vehicle identification in unconstrained surveillance imagery. The generated images and source code are publicly available at https://github.com/viniciusorru/vcr-synthetic

AI.tldr: This paper tackles vehicle color recognition under severe class imbalance in surveillance systems by combining synthetic minority-class augmentation (via text-conditioned image generation and image-conditioned color editing) with modern visual representations, loss reweighting, and ensemble methods, achieving 94.6% micro accuracy and 79.7% macro accuracy (+8.2% macro improvement over prior work).
AI.motivation: Vehicle color recognition is critical for surveillance when license plates are illegible, but real-world datasets suffer from highly imbalanced color distributions, making overall accuracy misleading for rare but operationally relevant colors.
AI.method: Synthetic minority-class augmentation using RunDiffusion/JuggernautXL (text-conditioned) and Gemini 2.0 Flash (image-conditioned color editing), combined with modern visual representations, loss reweighting, learning-rate scheduling, color-safe augmentation, foreground-aware preprocessing, and ensemble fusion.
AI.result: 94.6% micro accuracy and 79.7% macro accuracy, with an 8.2 percentage point improvement in macro accuracy over recent literature.
AI.conclusion: Synthetic augmentation effectively mitigates class imbalance for vehicle color recognition, though many remaining errors are visually ambiguous even for humans, indicating inherent limits of color-based identification in unconstrained surveillance.

title: TWLA: Achieving Ternary Weights and Low-Bit Activations for LLMs via Post-Training Quantization
