summary: Large language models (LLMs) exhibit exceptional general language processing capabilities, but their memory and compute costs hinder deployment. Ternarization has emerged as a promising compression technique, offering significant reductions in model size and inference complexity. However, existing methods struggle with heavy-tailed activation distributions and therefore keep activations in high precision, fundamentally limiting end-to-end inference acceleration. To overcome this limitation, we propose TWLA, a post-training quantization (PTQ) framework that achieves 1.58-bit weight compression and 4-bit activation quantization while maintaining high accuracy. TWLA comprises three components: (1) Euclidean-to-Manifold Asymmetric Ternary Quantizer (E2M-ATQ) minimizes layer-output error under weight ternarization via a two-stage optimization from Euclidean initialization to manifold relocation; (2) Kronecker Orthogonal Tri-Modal Shaping (KOTMS) applies a Kronecker-structured orthogonal rotation to reshape weights into ternary-friendly tri-modal distributions, while the shared rotation statistically suppresses activation outliers; and (3) Inter-Layer Aware Activation Mixed Precision (ILA-AMP) explicitly introduces adjacent-layer second-order interaction costs in bit allocation and jointly optimizes for the layer-wise disparity of activation quantization gains induced by the shared orthogonal transform, preventing cascades triggered by a few weak layers. Extensive experiments demonstrate that TWLA maintains high accuracy under W1.58A4, while delivering significant inference acceleration. The code is available at <https://github.com/Kishon-zzx/TWLA>.

AI.tldr: TWLA is a post-training quantization framework that achieves 1.58-bit weight and 4-bit activation quantization for LLMs via three components: E2M-ATQ (ternary quantization), KOTMS (outlier-suppressing orthogonal rotation), and ILA-AMP (inter-layer aware bit allocation), delivering both high accuracy and inference acceleration.
AI.motivation: Existing ternarization methods struggle with heavy-tailed activation distributions, forcing activations to remain in high precision and thus limiting end-to-end inference acceleration. The paper aims to achieve both aggressive weight ternarization (1.58-bit) and low-bit activation quantization (4-bit) simultaneously while maintaining accuracy.
AI.method: Three components: (1) E2M-ATQ — a two-stage optimization (Euclidean initialization → manifold relocation) for layer-output error minimization under weight ternarization; (2) KOTMS — Kronecker-structured orthogonal rotation reshaping weights into tri-modal distributions and suppressing activation outliers; (3) ILA-AMP — adjacent-layer second-order interaction costs in bit allocation, jointly optimizing for layer-wise disparity caused by the shared orthogonal transform.
AI.result: TWLA maintains high accuracy under W1.58A4 (1.58-bit weights, 4-bit activations) while delivering significant inference acceleration compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: TWLA effectively overcomes the heavy-tailed activation distribution challenge, enabling aggressive low-bit quantization for both weights and activations, and achieves a practical speed-accuracy trade-off for LLM deployment.

title: Evaluation Sovereignty in Metadata-Driven Classification: A Multi-Track Framework for Weakly Supervised Information Systems
summary:
Evaluation in machine learning is typically treated as a neutral measurement process. However, in operational information systems, evaluation outcomes are often conditioned by the processes used to generate labels. This paper does not seek to improve classification performance. Instead, it examines the validity of performance measurement under differing label-authority regimes. This issue is particularly relevant in large-scale metadata-driven systems, where labels are often incomplete, inconsistent, or weakly supervised.
  We introduce evaluation sovereignty, defined as the degree to which performance metrics are independent of label authority and supervision regime, and propose a multi-track evaluation framework that systematically varies training and evaluation label sources. Using hierarchical multi-label classification on large-scale scientific metadata, we demonstrate that models exhibiting strong performance under operational ("silver") evaluation degrade substantially under independent ("gold") evaluation, particularly for fine-grained classification. For example, Micro-F1 decreases from approximately 0.54 to 0.03. Notably, ranking-based metrics remain above baseline, revealing a divergence between latent model signal and classification validity.
  These findings suggest that commonly reported performance metrics may reflect alignment with labeling processes rather than true predictive capability. We therefore reconceptualize evaluation validity as a system-level property shaped by label governance and provide a practical methodology for auditing intelligent systems operating under weak supervision.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the concept of "evaluation sovereignty" to examine how label authority regimes affect performance metrics in machine learning. Using hierarchical multi-label classification on scientific metadata, they show that models performing well under operational ("silver") labels degrade substantially under independent ("gold") evaluation (e.g., Micro-F1 drops from ~0.54 to 0.03), suggesting reported metrics may reflect alignment with labeling processes rather than true predictive capability.
AI.motivation: To challenge the assumption that evaluation in machine learning is neutral, and to examine how label-authority regimes (e.g., silver vs. gold labels) distort performance measurement in large-scale metadata-driven systems where labels are often incomplete, inconsistent, or weakly supervised.
AI.method: Introduces "evaluation sovereignty" as a new concept and proposes a multi-track evaluation framework that systematically varies training and evaluation label sources. Applied to hierarchical multi-label classification on large-scale scientific metadata.
AI.result: Models performing well under silver evaluation degrade substantially under gold evaluation (Micro-F1 from ~0.54 to 0.03). Ranking-based metrics remain above baseline, revealing divergence between latent model signal and classification validity.
AI.conclusion: Commonly reported performance metrics may reflect alignment with labeling processes rather than true predictive capability. Evaluation validity should be reconceptualized as a system-level property shaped by label governance. Provides a practical methodology for auditing intelligent systems under weak supervision.

title: Surflo: Consistent 3D Surface Flow Model with Global State
summary: Geometry is invariant to viewpoint, which makes any collection of images a redundant encoding of a single 3D state. Existing feed-forward reconstruction models fail to exploit this: per-view methods emit overlapping, unaligned pointmaps that grow linearly with input count, while global-latent methods commit to a fixed, low-resolution output. We introduce Surflo, which compresses a variable number of unposed RGB views into K latent tokens-one global state-and decodes oriented 3D surface points by independently transporting them from noise onto the surface via flow matching. This frees the output from any fixed grid or token budget: the same latent yields from a few thousand to a million points in a single forward pass. To suppress the local inconsistencies inherent to independent per-point decoding, an inference-time guidance term correlates nearby points by injecting a photometric gradient during ODE integration. Surflo matches or surpasses feed-forward baselines on surface metrics, runs an order of magnitude faster than optimization-based methods that require hundreds of views, and is the only feed-forward approach to combine a global latent with arbitrary-resolution decoding.

AI.tldr: Surflo compresses unposed multi-view images into K latent tokens and decodes arbitrary-resolution 3D surfaces via flow matching, using inference-time photometric guidance to ensure consistency.
AI.motivation: Existing feed-forward reconstruction models fail to exploit the geometric invariance of multi-view images: per-view methods produce unaligned pointmaps scaling with input count, while global-latent methods are limited to fixed, low-resolution outputs.
AI.method: Compresses variable unposed RGB views into K latent tokens (global state), then decodes oriented 3D surface points by transporting noise via flow matching. An inference-time guidance term injects photometric gradients during ODE integration to suppress local inconsistencies from independent per-point decoding.
AI.result: Matches or surpasses feed-forward baselines on surface metrics; runs an order of magnitude faster than optimization-based methods requiring hundreds of views; achieves arbitrary-resolution decoding (thousands to millions of points) in a single forward pass.
AI.conclusion: Surflo is the only feed-forward approach combining a global latent with arbitrary-resolution decoding, offering flexibility and efficiency for 3D reconstruction from multi-view images.

title: A green solvent screening tool for emerging materials via uncertainty aware, transformer enhanced transfer learning
summary: Accurate prediction of solubility remains a central challenge across materials science and sustainable chemistry. In particular due to emerging technologies like organic and hybrid photovoltaics, batteries, and catalysis, solvent usage is expected to increase significantly within the coming years. Therefore, substituting solvents with greener alternatives is vital. This is where machine learning can have substantial impact. However, the limited data on critical parameters of solubility significantly constraints machine learning efficacy. In this work, we transfer a pre-trained foundational model on QM9 targets to our application with minimal data requirements. Additionally, the pipeline integrates uncertainty quantification, allowing the user to gauge the confidence of the predictions. As baseline, we succeed in predicting the Hansen solubility parameters and Dielectric Constant for which extensive databases exist. Importantly, we achieve high model performance on additional targets, such as Gutmann Donor and Acceptor numbers, where the available data is extremely limited. Overall, we augment data on solubility descriptors by orders of magnitude with high quality predictions. For effective dissemination, we deploy easy-to-use, easily integrateable with high throughput labs, customizable tool for ranking and screening possible solvent substitutes. Finally, we rediscovered known green solvent alternatives and proposed new candidates proving its relevance for finding eco-friendly solvents.

AI.tldr: This paper uses transfer learning from a pre-trained QM9 model to predict solubility descriptors (Hansen parameters, dielectric constant, Gutmann donor/acceptor numbers) with limited data, integrates uncertainty quantification, and deploys a tool for green solvent screening, achieving high-quality predictions and rediscovering known eco-friendly alternatives.
AI.motivation: Accurate solubility prediction is critical for sustainable chemistry (e.g., photovoltaics, batteries, catalysis), but data scarcity on key solubility parameters limits machine learning effectiveness for green solvent discovery.
AI.method: Transfer learning from a pre-trained foundational model on QM9 targets, combined with uncertainty quantification, to predict solubility descriptors with minimal data requirements.
AI.result: High performance on Hansen solubility parameters and dielectric constant (large datasets), and strong results on Gutmann donor/acceptor numbers (extremely limited data). Augmented solubility descriptor data by orders of magnitude.
AI.conclusion: The tool successfully rediscovered known green solvent alternatives and proposed new candidates, demonstrating its relevance for eco-friendly solvent substitution.

title: Why Sampling Is Not Choosing: Intentionality, Agency, and Moral Responsibility in Large Language Models
summary: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have prompted claims that such systems exhibit agency or qualify as moral agents. This paper argues that these attributions are misguided. We maintain that moral responsibility requires commitment-bearing agency grounded in intrinsic intentionality and self-attributed action, and that such agency constitutes the form of free will relevant to responsibility. Although LLMs generate coherent and normatively evaluable outputs, their operation is fully characterized by probabilistic input-output mappings learned from data. Their apparent intentionality is derived rather than intrinsic, and their outputs are neither owned as commitments nor guided by reasons. Variability introduced by stochastic sampling does not amount to choice or authorship. We address objections from the intentional stance, functionalism, compatibilism, and the presence of moral reasoning in model outputs, arguing that none suffice to establish genuine agency.

AI.tldr: This paper argues that large language models (LLMs) are not genuine moral agents because they lack commitment-bearing agency, intrinsic intentionality, self-attributed action, and the form of free will necessary for moral responsibility.
AI.motivation: To challenge recent claims that LLMs exhibit agency or qualify as moral agents, and to clarify the conceptual requirements for genuine moral responsibility.
AI.method: Conceptual/philosophical analysis: The paper defines moral responsibility as requiring commitment-bearing agency with intrinsic intentionality and self-attributed action, then evaluates LLMs against these criteria while addressing counterarguments from the intentional stance, functionalism, compatibilism, and moral reasoning in outputs.
AI.result: LLMs fail to meet the conditions for moral agency: their intentionality is derived (not intrinsic), their outputs are not owned as commitments or guided by reasons, and stochastic sampling does not constitute genuine choice or authorship.
AI.conclusion: Attributions of moral agency to LLMs are misguided; the objections considered do not establish genuine agency.

title: World Tracing: Generative Pixel-Aligned Geometry Beyond the Visible
summary: Image-to-3D methods often trade off faithfulness and completeness: depth estimators are anchored to input pixels but stop at the visible surface, while image-to-3D models generate complete shapes that are often misaligned with the input. We introduce World Tracing, a generative pixel-aligned geometry representation that predicts 3D points aligned with observed pixels while completing geometry beyond the visible surface. For each input pixel, World Tracing predicts an ordered stack of camera-space 3D points, where the first layer represents the visible surface and subsequent layers represent front-to-back intersections with occluded surfaces. We instantiate this representation with a world-tracing diffusion transformer, WT-DiT, which treats multiple geometry layers as separate denoising tokens coupled through factorized and global attention. WT-DiT is trained with pixel-space flow matching and a mixed noise schedule that balances visible-surface reconstruction with occluded-geometry generation. World Tracing achieves strong performance on visible-surface reconstruction and complete geometry generation across object, scene, and dynamic benchmarks, outperforming both depth predictors and image-to-3D generators. It also preserves 2D-to-3D correspondence, enabling text-driven 3D scene editing, geometry-conditioned novel-view video synthesis, and training-free integration with textured-mesh generators.

AI.tldr: World Tracing introduces a generative pixel-aligned geometry representation that predicts ordered stacks of 3D points per pixel—visible surface first, then occluded layers—using a diffusion transformer (WT-DiT) to achieve both faithful visible-surface reconstruction and complete 3D geometry generation.
AI.motivation: Existing image-to-3D methods suffer from a trade-off: depth estimators are pixel-aligned but only capture visible surfaces, while generative models produce complete shapes that often misalign with input images. The authors aim to bridge faithfulness and completeness in a single representation.
AI.method: World Tracing predicts per-pixel ordered stacks of camera-space 3D points (visible surface + occluded layers). It uses a world-tracing diffusion transformer (WT-DiT) with factorized and global attention to treat multiple geometry layers as separate denoising tokens, trained with pixel-space flow matching and a mixed noise schedule.
AI.result: Achieves strong performance on visible-surface reconstruction and complete geometry generation across object, scene, and dynamic benchmarks, outperforming both depth predictors and image-to-3D generators. Also preserves 2D-to-3D correspondence for downstream tasks.
AI.conclusion: World Tracing effectively unifies faithful visible-surface alignment with complete 3D geometry generation, enabling applications like text-driven 3D scene editing, novel-view video synthesis, and integration with textured-mesh generators without additional training.

title: Limits of spectral learning under noise
summary: Learning functional relationships from noisy data is a central problem in scientific inference. Spectral methods approximate unknown functions by expanding them in a basis and estimating the corresponding coefficients from data, but the stability of these coefficients under noise remains poorly understood. Here we study supervised regression with additive label noise using sparse spectral representations across multiple bases and dimensions. We show that noise induces a predictable drift in the learned coefficient vector whose magnitude depends on the effective number of active spectral modes. After whitening the empirical feature geometry, we derive a closed-form expression for the overlap between noisy and noiseless coefficient vectors, revealing a universal degradation curve governed by a single intrinsic noise scale. Numerical experiments across Fourier, Legendre, Bessel, and Haar bases confirm the theoretical prediction. The results demonstrate that spectral learning exhibits a fundamental noise threshold beyond which coefficient estimates become unstable, placing intrinsic limits on recovering functional structure from noisy data.

AI.tldr: This paper studies spectral learning under label noise, showing that noise induces predictable coefficient drift governed by the number of active modes. A closed-form expression for coefficient overlap reveals a universal degradation curve with a critical noise threshold, validated across multiple basis families.
AI.motivation: Spectral methods are widely used for learning functional relationships from noisy data, but the stability of spectral coefficients under noise remains poorly understood. The authors aim to characterize this stability and identify fundamental limits on recovering functional structure.
AI.method: The authors study supervised regression with additive label noise using sparse spectral representations across multiple bases and dimensions. They whiten the empirical feature geometry and derive a closed-form expression for the overlap between noisy and noiseless coefficient vectors.
AI.result: Noise induces predictable coefficient drift; the magnitude depends on the effective number of active spectral modes. The overlap follows a universal degradation curve controlled by a single intrinsic noise scale. Numerical experiments across Fourier, Legendre, Bessel, and Haar bases confirm predictions.
AI.conclusion: Spectral learning has a fundamental noise threshold beyond which coefficient estimates become unstable, placing intrinsic limits on recovering functional structure from noisy data.

title: CloudCons: A Comprehensive End-to-End Benchmark for Cloud Resource Consolidation
summary: Driven by conservative over-provisioning to guarantee service reliability, resource utilization in cloud data centers remains at low levels. To mitigate this, the forecast-then-optimize paradigm has emerged to optimize consolidation by anticipating future demands. While emerging time series foundation models promise to enhance this paradigm through zero-shot generalization, existing benchmarks focus solely on prediction error metrics. The actual decision utility of these advanced models remains unverified, rendering their practical value for downstream tasks uncertain. To bridge this gap, we propose CloudCons, a comprehensive end-to-end benchmark designed to evaluate forecasting models within the specific context of cloud resource consolidation. We build high-quality datasets that cover diverse workloads from Huawei Cloud, Microsoft Azure, and Google Borg, capturing distinct service characteristics ranging from synchronized diurnal rhythms to stochastic, pulse-like bursts and high-frequency noise. We conduct an extensive evaluation of statistical, deep learning, and foundation models. Our experiments reveal a pivotal finding: while foundation models demonstrate superior zero-shot forecasting accuracy, this advantage does not inherently translate into better decision utility. Of practical significance, we systematically analyze how the selection of predictive quantiles acts as a critical lever. We provide actionable guidelines for calibrating these selections to balance the trade-off between resource efficiency and service reliability, offering vital insights for real-world deployment decisions.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces CloudCons, a benchmark evaluating forecasting models for cloud resource consolidation. Key finding: foundation models excel at zero-shot forecasting accuracy but this advantage does not directly translate to better decision utility in downstream consolidation tasks.
AI.motivation: Cloud data centers suffer from low resource utilization due to conservative over-provisioning. While forecast-then-optimize paradigms exist, current benchmarks only measure prediction error — not actual decision utility of forecasts for downstream consolidation tasks.
AI.method: Proposes CloudCons, an end-to-end benchmark with high-quality datasets from Huawei Cloud, Microsoft Azure, and Google Borg (covering diverse workload patterns), and evaluates statistical, deep learning, and foundation models on both forecasting accuracy and downstream decision utility.
AI.result: Foundation models show superior zero-shot forecasting accuracy, but this does not inherently translate into better decision utility. The selection of predictive quantiles is identified as a critical lever balancing resource efficiency vs. service reliability.
AI.conclusion: Provides actionable guidelines for calibrating predictive quantile selections in cloud consolidation, offering practical insights for real-world deployment decisions.

title: Flex4DHuman: Flexible Multi-view Video Diffusion for 4D Human Reconstruction
comment: 18 pages, 8 figures. Code, and multi-view caption dataset available
summary: We present Flex4DHuman, a multi-view video diffusion model that transforms a monocular or sparse multi-view video of a dynamic subject into synchronized dense multi-view videos using only relative camera-pose conditioning. Unlike prior human-centric methods that rely on skeletons, depth maps, normals, or rendered target-view geometry, Flex4DHuman requires no explicit geometry priors and instead conditions generation through relative camera-pose positional encoding. The generated videos can be directly ingested by downstream reconstruction pipelines to create dynamic 4D Gaussian splats. Built on the Wan 2.1 1.3B text-to-video model, Flex4DHuman preserves the backbone architecture and encodes camera and view information through a five-axis positional encoding that extends spatio-temporal RoPE with view indices and continuous SE(3) relative camera geometry. A three-stage curriculum progressively trains the model for pose following, flexible reference-to-target view generation, and temporal rollout. To support temporal rollout, we train with clean historical target-view tokens. We also add multi-view captions to enable test-time text control. Combined with an off-the-shelf 4D Gaussian Splatting stage, our framework lifts monocular static-camera videos into dynamic 4D Gaussian splats. Experiments on DNA-Rendering and ActorsHQ show that Flex4DHuman surpasses prior state-of-the-art methods, while the same formulation generalizes to animal categories after mixed human-animal training. These capabilities make Flex4DHuman a practical step toward scalable 4D content creation from casual monocular videos for simulation, gaming, AR/VR, and video re-shooting.

AI.tldr: Flex4DHuman is a multi-view video diffusion model that converts monocular or sparse multi-view videos into synchronized dense multi-view videos using only relative camera-pose conditioning, without explicit geometry priors. It enables dynamic 4D Gaussian splatting from casual videos.
AI.motivation: To enable scalable 4D content creation from casual monocular videos for applications like simulation, gaming, AR/VR, and video re-shooting, without requiring explicit geometry priors (skeletons, depth maps, normals).
AI.method: Built on Wan 2.1 text-to-video model; uses five-axis positional encoding (extending RoPE with view indices and SE(3) relative camera geometry); three-stage curriculum training (pose following, reference-to-target generation, temporal rollout); clean historical target-view tokens for rollout; multi-view captions for text control.
AI.result: Surpasses prior SOTA on DNA-Rendering and ActorsHQ; generalizes to animal categories after mixed human-animal training; generates videos directly usable by downstream 4D Gaussian Splatting reconstruction.
AI.conclusion: Flex4DHuman provides a practical approach for scalable 4D content creation from casual monocular videos without geometry priors, with strong performance and generalization across categories.

title: Emotional regulation improves deep learning-based image classification
summary: Emotion significantly influences cognition, enhancing memory and learning under certain conditions. Drawing on this principle, emotion-augmented deep learning investigates how affective states can improve neural network architectures and learning paradigms, achieving better generalization than non-emotional models. However, existing methods often rely solely on objective neurophysiological factors, neglecting the role of subjectivity in emotion. To bridge this gap, the present study introduces Emotional Regulation, a novel framework for modeling emotion in deep learning through artificial subjective experience. The method employs pre-training based on affective stimuli, balancing non-emotional and emotionally-influenced responses in downstream task optimization. Extensive experimentation was conducted in image classification, pre-training ResNet and ViT architectures on four emotional datasets, using CIFAR-10 and -100 as target benchmarks. Results reveal improvements over the aforementioned backbones, providing evidence of Emotional Regulation as a promising method for defining emotion-augmented deep learning through artificial subjective experience. Furthermore, the proposed approach overcomes the related work in image classification based on CIFAR, revealing Emotional Regulation as the new state-of-the-art in emotion-augmented deep learning for large-scale vision datasets. The study also enforces evidence of the impact of affective states in improving machine learning tasks' optimization, encouraging further investigation on emotion-inspired architectures.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Emotional Regulation, a framework that models emotion through artificial subjective experience (rather than objective neurophysiological factors) to augment deep learning, achieving state-of-the-art results in image classification on CIFAR benchmarks using pre-trained ResNet and ViT architectures on emotional datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing emotion-augmented deep learning methods rely only on objective neurophysiological factors and neglect the role of subjectivity in emotion, limiting their effectiveness.
AI.method: Emotional Regulation framework: pre-training on affective stimuli with a balance between non-emotional and emotionally-influenced responses during downstream task optimization, using artificial subjective experience to model emotion.
AI.result: The method improves over standard ResNet and ViT backbones on CIFAR-10/100, achieves state-of-the-art in emotion-augmented deep learning for large-scale vision, and demonstrates that affective states improve ML optimization.
AI.conclusion: Emotional Regulation is a promising approach for emotion-augmented deep learning through artificial subjective experience, and the findings encourage further research on emotion-inspired architectures.

title: Uncertainty-Aware Hybrid Retrieval for Long-Document RAG
summary: Retrieval augmented generation (RAG) depends critically on the quality and granularity of retrieved evidence. Large retrieval units preserve context but often introduce irrelevant content, which can dilute answer bearing evidence and worsen long context utilization. Fine-grained units are more compact, but they may be difficult to retrieve reliably because short chunks can lack semantic, lexical, or bridging cues needed to match the query. We propose Uncertainty-aware Multi-Granularity RAG (UMG-RAG), a training-free hybrid retrieval framework that treats chunk granularity as query-specific reliability estimation. Instead of training a new retriever or modifying the generator, UMG-RAG uses existing dense and sparse retrievers as complementary experts across multiple chunk granularities. For each query, it converts each expert-granularity score list into an evidence distribution, estimates reliability from distribution entropy, and fuses candidates according to query-specific semantic, lexical, and granularity confidence. We further introduce UMGP-RAG, a parent promotion variant that uses fine-grained hits to locate relevant evidence while returning broader non-redundant parent chunks for local coherence. Experiments on question answering benchmarks show that uncertainty-aware fusion and parent promotion improve generation quality while maintaining a lightweight, plug-and-play retrieval pipeline.

AI.tldr: UMG-RAG proposes a training-free hybrid retrieval framework that treats chunk granularity as query-specific reliability estimation, using uncertainty-aware fusion of multiple retrievers and granularities to improve RAG quality.
AI.motivation: RAG performance depends on retrieval quality and granularity. Large chunks cause dilution of evidence; small chunks lack retrieval cues. Existing methods require training or modifying models, which is expensive and inflexible.
AI.method: UMG-RAG uses existing dense and sparse retrievers across multiple chunk granularities without training. It converts expert-granularity scores into evidence distributions, estimates reliability via distribution entropy, and fuses candidates based on query-specific semantic, lexical, and granularity confidence. UMGP-RAG is a variant using parent promotion for local coherence.
AI.result: Experiments on QA benchmarks show that uncertainty-aware fusion and parent promotion improve generation quality while maintaining a lightweight, plug-and-play retrieval pipeline.
AI.conclusion: Treating chunk granularity as query-specific reliability estimation and fusing multi-granularity evidence via uncertainty can enhance RAG without additional training or model modifications.

title: SpatialClaw: Rethinking Action Interface for Agentic Spatial Reasoning
summary: Spatial reasoning, the ability to determine where objects are, how they relate, and how they move in 3D, remains a fundamental challenge for vision-language models (VLMs). Tool-augmented agents attempt to address this by augmenting VLMs with specialist perception modules, yet their effectiveness is bounded by the action interface through which those tools are invoked. In this work, we study how the design of this interface shapes the agent's capacity for open-ended spatial reasoning. Existing spatial agents either employ single-pass code execution, which commits to a full analysis strategy before any intermediate result is observed, or rely on a structured tool-call interface that often offers less flexibility for freely composing operations or tailoring the analysis to each task. Both designs offer limited flexibility for open-ended, complex 3D/4D spatial reasoning. We therefore propose SpatialClaw, a training-free framework for spatial reasoning that adopts code as the action interface. SpatialClaw maintains a stateful Python kernel pre-loaded with input frames and a suite of perception and geometry primitives, letting a VLM-backed agent write one executable cell per step conditioned on all prior outputs, enabling the agent to flexibly compose and manipulate perception results and adapt its analysis to both intermediate text and visual observations and the demands of each problem. Evaluated across 20 spatial reasoning benchmarks spanning a broad range of static and dynamic 3D/4D spatial reasoning tasks, SpatialClaw achieves 59.9% average accuracy, outperforming the recent spatial agent by +11.2 points, with consistent gains across six VLM backbones from two model families without any benchmark- or model-specific adaptation.

AI.tldr: SpatialClaw is a training-free framework that uses code as an action interface for spatial reasoning, achieving 59.9% average accuracy across 20 benchmarks, outperforming prior spatial agents by +11.2 points consistently across six VLM backbones.
AI.motivation: Existing spatial reasoning agents are limited by rigid action interfaces—single-pass code execution commits to a full strategy before seeing results, while structured tool-call interfaces lack flexibility for open-ended, complex 3D/4D tasks. The goal is to design a more flexible interface that enables adaptive, compositional spatial reasoning.
AI.method: SpatialClaw maintains a stateful Python kernel pre-loaded with input frames and perception/geometry primitives. A VLM-backed agent writes one executable code cell per step, conditioned on all prior intermediate outputs (text and visual), enabling flexible composition and adaptive analysis tailored to each problem.
AI.result: Across 20 spatial reasoning benchmarks covering static and dynamic 3D/4D tasks, SpatialClaw achieves 59.9% average accuracy, outperforming the prior spatial agent by +11.2 points with consistent gains across six VLM backbones from two model families, without any benchmark- or model-specific adaptation.
AI.conclusion: Adopting code as a flexible, stateful action interface significantly enhances VLMs' capability for open-ended spatial reasoning, and the framework generalizes well across diverse backbones and tasks without requiring fine-tuning.

title: Is It You or Your Environment? A Bayesian Inference Framework for Genomically-Anchored Personalized Physiological Interpretation
comment: 24 pages, 8 figures, 3 tables. Conceptual framework paper
summary: Personalized health AI systems face a fundamental cold-start problem: machine learning models for physiological interpretation require weeks of individual behavioral data before they can distinguish constitutional variation from environmentally driven deviation. We propose a solution grounded in causal inference and Bayesian prior design. An individual's genomic profile serves as an exogenous genetic anchor -- a domain-informed, personalized prior that is fixed at conception, immune to reverse causation, and available before a single behavioral observation is collected. The anchor initializes a Bayesian belief state over an individual's physiological set point G-hat = mu + sum(beta_i * g_i), where beta_i are GWAS-derived effect sizes and g_i are risk-allele counts. Each incoming physiological measurement P produces a non-constitutional deviation delta = P - G-hat that separates the signal attributable to environment and state from the constitutionally fixed baseline. As behavioral data accrue, the prior decays according to G-hat_t = w(t)*G-hat_genomic + [1-w(t)]*P-bar_t, transitioning from genome-dominated to empirical-baseline-dominated inference. The same observed HRV of 55 ms generates a suppression hypothesis for a person whose prior predicts 80 ms, and an enhancement hypothesis for a person whose prior predicts 30 ms -- a reversal impossible without a personalized anchor. We develop this architecture across six physiological domains, grading genomic priors by evidence strength, distinguishing robustly replicated anchors (FTO, FADS1/2, FKBP5) from contested candidate genes (SLC6A4, MAOA, DRD2). We address the inference boundary between association, Mendelian randomization, and individual token causation, and define four constraints for deployment: evidence-graded priors, dynamic decay, ancestry-matched effect sizes, and attribution rather than deterministic output.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a causal Bayesian framework that uses genomic profiles as personalized priors to solve the cold-start problem in health AI, enabling physiological deviation detection from the first measurement without requiring weeks of behavioral data.
AI.motivation: Health AI systems require extensive behavioral data before they can distinguish normal individual variation from abnormal deviations, creating a cold-start problem that delays personalized physiological interpretation.
AI.method: Genomic profiles serve as exogenous personalized priors (G-hat = mu + sum(beta_i * g_i)) using GWAS-derived effect sizes. Incoming measurements produce deviation scores (delta = P - G-hat), and the prior decays over time via G-hat_t = w(t)*G-hat_genomic + [1-w(t)]*P-bar_t, transitioning from genome-dominated to data-dominated inference.
AI.result: The same HRV value (55 ms) generates opposite interpretations depending on genomic prior (suppression if prior=80 ms, enhancement if prior=30 ms), demonstrating anchor-dependent reversal. Genomic priors are graded by evidence strength across six domains, distinguishing robust anchors from contested candidates.
AI.conclusion: Deployment requires four constraints: evidence-graded priors, dynamic decay weighting, ancestry-matched effect sizes, and attribution rather than deterministic output, with clear boundaries among association, Mendelian randomization, and individual-level causation.

title: RepWAM: World Action Modeling with Representation Visual-Action Tokenizers
summary: This work presents RepWAM, a representation-centric world action model (WAM) built on representation visual-action tokenizers. Existing WAMs typically inherit reconstruction-oriented video tokenizers from pretrained video generation models. Although these tokenizers preserve visual fidelity, pixel reconstruction alone provides limited guidance for learning instruction-following dynamics that connect future prediction with robot control. To address this, we explore a semantic visual-action latent space for representation-centric world action modeling. Specifically, we train a representation visual-action tokenizer that maps visual inputs into aligned visual and latent action tokens. We then pretrain our WAM to jointly model future visual states and the latent actions that connect them under language instructions, followed by adaptation to real robot trajectories for closed-loop manipulation. Experiments on real-world manipulation tasks and simulation benchmarks show that RepWAM delivers strong performance across diverse manipulation settings, while ablations highlight the value of semantic visual-action tokenization over reconstruction-oriented alternatives. These results establish representation visual-action tokenization as a promising foundation for world action models and a step toward generalist robot policies. Code and weights will be available at https://github.com/wdrink/RepWAM.

AI.tldr: RepWAM is a representation-centric world action model using semantic visual-action tokenizers instead of reconstruction-oriented ones, achieving strong performance in robot manipulation by jointly modeling future visual states and latent actions under language instructions.
AI.motivation: Existing world action models rely on reconstruction-oriented video tokenizers that preserve visual fidelity but fail to learn instruction-following dynamics connecting future prediction with robot control. This paper aims to address that limitation.
AI.method: 1) Train a representation visual-action tokenizer that maps visual inputs into aligned visual and latent action tokens. 2) Pretrain the WAM to jointly model future visual states and latent actions under language instructions. 3) Adapt to real robot trajectories for closed-loop manipulation.
AI.result: Strong performance across diverse real-world manipulation tasks and simulation benchmarks; ablations confirm semantic visual-action tokenization outperforms reconstruction-oriented alternatives.
AI.conclusion: Representation visual-action tokenization is a promising foundation for world action models and a step toward generalist robot policies.

title: Authority, Truth, and Citation Bias: A Large-Scale Multi-Domain Benchmark for Studying Epistemic Susceptibility in Large Language Models
comment: 10 pages, 5 figures. Accepted to AI4GOOD and EIML at ICML 2026
summary: Large language models are increasingly deployed in citation-augmented settings, yet the effect of citation presence on model behavior independent of factual content remains poorly understood. We introduce AuthorityBench, a 220,564-prompt multi-domain benchmark that isolates how citation-based authority signals influence epistemic behavior in LLMs. The benchmark uses a fully balanced 2x2 factorial design crossing claim veracity with citation veracity, the first to do so, across four domains (general knowledge, science, law, and medicine), with controlled variation over 40 prompt templates, four venue prestige tiers, and a country-coded author name dataset. Evaluating seven models on 12 structured research questions, we find that citation presence, whether real or fabricated, consistently increases hallucination rates relative to a no-citation baseline. The effect is strongest when fabricated citations accompany true claims, raising hallucination rates by 3 to 22 percentage points and reaching 35 to 77% in the general knowledge domain, while legal claims are comparatively robust and venue prestige and author demographics show negligible impact. All datasets and evaluation code are available at: https://github.com/floating-reeds/AuthorityBench

AI.tldr: AuthorityBench is a 220k-prompt benchmark revealing that citation presence (real or fabricated) consistently increases hallucination rates in LLMs, with the strongest effect when fake citations accompany true claims (3-22 percentage point increase), while venue prestige and author demographics have negligible impact.
AI.motivation: To understand how citation-based authority signals influence LLM epistemic behavior independent of factual content, an effect that remains poorly understood despite increasing deployment of LLMs in citation-augmented settings.
AI.method: Created AuthorityBench with 220,564 prompts using a balanced 2x2 factorial design (claim veracity × citation veracity) across four domains (general knowledge, science, law, medicine), with 40 prompt templates, four venue prestige tiers, and country-coded author names. Evaluated seven models on 12 structured research questions.
AI.result: Citation presence consistently increases hallucination rates. The effect is strongest with fabricated citations on true claims (3-22 percentage point increase), reaching 35-77% hallucination in general knowledge. Legal claims are most robust; venue prestige and author demographics show negligible impact.
AI.conclusion: The mere presence of citations—regardless of their veracity—degrades LLM reliability, highlighting a fundamental vulnerability in citation-augmented LLM systems that warrants caution in deployment.

title: A Three-Layer Framework for AI in Scientific Discovery
summary: Current discussions of AI in scientific discovery are often dominated by two visible capabilities: search over existing knowledge and execution through optimization, simulation, and automation. Both are important, but neither fully captures the central act of discovery: the formation and evolution of models. This paper proposes a three-layer view of AI in discovery. Layer 1 is search and retrieval by large language models. Layer 2, as the main innovation of this paper, is model formation through qualitative reasoning: the capacity to recognize when a current framework is structurally inadequate and to understand the problem within a broader representational space, not through trial and error, but through structural insight into what is missing and where it can be found. Layer 3 is execution, optimization, and refinement. The main claim is that Layer 2 is both the most important and the least developed. Search without model formation remains confined to inherited frameworks, while execution without conceptual revision only amplifies an existing formulation. We illustrate Layer 2 reasoning through three case studies: S. S. Chern's intrinsic proof of the Gauss-Bonnet theorem, the resolution of the Nesterov Accelerated Gradient convergence problem via Lyapunov functions, and the autonomous disproof of the Erdos unit distance conjecture by OpenAI in 2026. Each case exhibits the same structural signature: a framework that had become inadequate, a missing conceptual object, and a resolution found in an unexpected neighboring field.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a three-layer framework for AI in scientific discovery, arguing that Layer 2 (qualitative model formation through structural insight) is the most critical yet underdeveloped capability, illustrated via three mathematical case studies.
AI.motivation: Current AI-in-discovery discourse emphasizes search (Layer 1) and optimization/execution (Layer 3), but neglects the core act of scientific discovery—recognizing when a conceptual framework is inadequate and forming new models through structural reasoning rather than trial and error.
AI.method: A three-layer framework is proposed: Layer 1 (search/retrieval by LLMs), Layer 2 (qualitative reasoning and model formation via structural insight), and Layer 3 (execution/optimization). The claim is validated through three case studies showing the common pattern of framework inadequacy, missing conceptual object, and cross-domain resolution.
AI.result: The analysis reveals a shared structural signature across all three cases: an inherited framework becomes inadequate, a missing conceptual object is identified not by exhaustive search but by structural insight, and the resolution is found in an unexpected neighboring field. Layer 2 remains the least developed in current AI systems.
AI.conclusion: Layer 2 reasoning—model formation through qualitative structural insight—is the most important capability for genuine scientific discovery in AI, and its current absence limits both search and optimization to operating within inherited, potentially inadequate frameworks.

title: Modality Forcing for Scalable Spatial Generation
summary: Text-to-image (T2I) models contain rich spatial priors. Synthesizing photorealistic, cluttered scenes requires an understanding of geometry, including perspective and relative scale. Prior works adapt T2I models to leverage this prior for depth prediction, but they require dense depth data and involve complex recipes. We propose Modality Forcing, a simple, scalable post-training recipe for joint image-depth generation using a single DiT trained on sparse depth data. Modality Forcing enables conditional and joint generation of image and depth in any permutation by assigning separate noise levels per modality. Per-modality decoders let us train on sparse, real-world depth and achieve strong, generalizable depth prediction. We further show that Modality Forcing inherits the scalability of T2I pre-training: by training a set of T2I models from scratch (370M to 3.3B parameters), we find that larger models trained on more image data produce more accurate depth. Our strongest model is competitive with state-of-the-art monocular depth estimators and reduces AbsRel by 57% relative to existing joint image-depth generative models. These results provide strong evidence that image generation is a scalable pre-training objective for spatial perception. https://modality-forcing.github.io/

AI.tldr: Modality Forcing is a simple post-training method that enables joint image-depth generation from a single Diffusion Transformer (DiT) trained on sparse depth data, achieving state-of-the-art depth prediction by leveraging the spatial priors of T2I models.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for adapting T2I models to depth prediction require dense depth data and complex training recipes. The authors aim to develop a simpler, more scalable approach that can leverage sparse real-world depth data while inheriting the scalability of T2I pre-training.
AI.method: Modality Forcing assigns separate noise levels per modality (image and depth) within a single DiT model, using per-modality decoders to train on sparse depth data. This enables conditional and joint generation in any permutation (image→depth, depth→image, or joint generation).
AI.result: Larger models (370M to 3.3B parameters) trained on more image data produce more accurate depth. The strongest model is competitive with SOTA monocular depth estimators, reducing AbsRel by 57% relative to existing joint image-depth generative models.
AI.conclusion: Image generation serves as a scalable pre-training objective for spatial perception tasks like depth estimation, and Modality Forcing provides a simple yet effective recipe to extract these spatial priors.

title: Disparate Impact in Synthetic Data Generation
summary: We revisit the fairness notion of disparate impact for synthetic data generation (SDG), that assesses whether the utility of generated records is the same across sensitive groups. Our approach departs from existing work on fair SDG, that address the problem of correcting for undue biases in the observed distribution, hence redefining SDG as learning a distribution that is not that of the real data. By contrast, non-disparate impact is notably achieved when the synthetic and real distributions are the same. We expose reasons why SDG may fail to reach that solution and discuss why approximation and estimation errors occur and can be disparate across groups. We notably look into the expressive power of SDG methods relative to distribution complexity, sampling errors due to group proportions, and estimation errors induced by differential privacy mechanisms. We illustrate cases of disparate impact on both artificial and real-world data, focusing on SDG methods that rely on probabilistic graphical models. We also introduce a strategy of learning group-wise SDG models and illustrate how it can improve both the overall utility and its parity in many settings.

AI.tldr: This paper examines disparate impact in synthetic data generation, arguing that fairness should mean the synthetic distribution matches the real distribution, not correcting biases. It identifies causes of unfairness (expressiveness, sampling, privacy errors) and proposes group-wise models to improve both utility and parity.
AI.motivation: Existing fair SDG methods try to correct biases in observed data, redefining SDG as learning a distribution different from the real one. The authors argue that non-disparate impact should instead require the synthetic and real distributions to be the same, and they explore why SDG methods may fail to achieve this.
AI.method: The authors analyze sources of disparate impact in SDG: (1) expressive power of models relative to distribution complexity, (2) sampling errors due to group proportions, (3) estimation errors from differential privacy. They test on synthetic and real data using probabilistic graphical models, and propose learning group-wise SDG models as a mitigation.
AI.result: The paper demonstrates cases of disparate impact in both artificial and real-world data. It shows that group-wise SDG modeling can improve both overall utility and parity across groups in many settings.
AI.conclusion: Fairness in SDG should be framed as preserving the real data distribution rather than correcting it. Disparate impact arises from technical limitations (model capacity, sampling, privacy), and group-wise modeling offers a promising mitigation strategy.

title: Multiagent Protocols with Aggregated Confidence Signals
comment: 22 pages and 5 figures, 9 pages and 2 figures before the appendix
summary: Confidence is used for reliability, oversight, and a range of downstream decision tasks in Natural Language Processing (NLP), yet no existing method produces or evaluates a confidence for the output of a multiagent system. Prior work uses confidence within multiagent debate (MAD) to weight messages, trigger debate, or calibrate individual agents, but it never aggregates these into a single confidence for the system itself. We introduce three protocols that produce a final answer along with a single aggregated confidence by first transforming raw confidence signals to make them comparable across models, then combining them via soft voting or a probability fusion we call Bayesian fusion. This aggregated confidence is substantially more discriminative (AUARC) than that of the best single agent or the standard debate baselines, while correctness (F1-score) stays stable and recovers the losses MAD incurs on more ambiguous tasks. Analyzing two estimators, sequence probability and self-report, alongside parametric and non-parametric calibrators, we find that calibration improves F1 for both estimators while AUARC is less reliant on it. We evaluate six homogeneous and heterogeneous debating pairs per benchmark, across five benchmarks and four task types, spanning a range of model capabilities and sizes.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces three protocols to aggregate confidence in multiagent debate systems, producing more discriminative confidence signals while maintaining stable correctness across diverse benchmarks and model configurations.
AI.motivation: No existing method produces or evaluates a single aggregated confidence for multiagent system outputs, despite confidence being critical for reliability and downstream tasks in NLP.
AI.method: Three protocols that transform raw confidence signals across models to make them comparable, then aggregate via soft voting or Bayesian fusion.
AI.result: Aggregated confidence achieves substantially higher AUARC than single-agent or standard debate baselines, while F1-score remains stable and recovers losses from MAD on ambiguous tasks.
AI.conclusion: Calibration improves F1 for both estimators (sequence probability and self-report) but AUARC is less reliant on calibration, and the approach generalizes across diverse benchmarks and model sizes.

title: InterleaveThinker: Reinforcing Agentic Interleaved Generation
summary: Recent image generators have demonstrated impressive photorealism and instruction-following capabilities in single-image generation and editing. However, constrained by their architectures, they cannot achieve interleaved generation (text-image sequence), which has crucial applications in visual narratives, guidance, and embodied manipulation. Even the latest open-source Unified Multimodal Models (UMMs) exhibit limited performance in this regard. In this paper, we introduce InterleaveThinker, the first multi-agent pipeline designed to endow any existing image generator with interleaved generation capabilities. Specifically, we employ a planner agent to organize the image-text input sequence, instructing the image generator on the required execution at each step. Subsequently, we introduce a critic agent to evaluate the generator's outputs, identify samples that deviate from the planned instructions, and refine the instructions for regeneration. To implement this pipeline, we construct the Interleave-Planner-SFT-80k and Interleave-Critic-SFT-112k to perform a format cold-start. Then we develop Interleave-Critic-RL-13k to reinforce the step-wise instruction correction capability within a generation trajectory using GRPO. Since a single interleaved generation trajectory may involve over 25 generator calls, optimizing the entire trajectory is computationally impractical. Therefore, we propose accuracy reward and step-wise reward, allowing single-step RL to effectively guide the entire generation trajectory. The results show that InterleaveThinker improves performance across various image generators. On interleaved generation benchmarks, it achieves performance comparable to Nano Banana and GPT-5. Surprisingly, it also significantly enhances the base model on reasoning-based benchmarks; for example, on 4-step FLUX.2-klein, we observe substantial gains on WISE and RISE.

AI.tldr: InterleaveThinker is a multi-agent pipeline that adds interleaved generation (text-image sequences) to any existing image generator using a planner agent and a critic agent with reinforcement learning, achieving performance comparable to GPT-5 and Nano Banana on benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing image generators cannot produce interleaved text-image sequences despite their photorealism and instruction-following abilities, limiting their application in visual narratives, guidance, and embodied manipulation. Even current Unified Multimodal Models (UMMs) perform poorly on this task.
AI.method: A multi-agent pipeline with (1) a planner agent that organizes image-text sequences and instructs the generator, (2) a critic agent that evaluates outputs and refines instructions for regeneration. Training uses Interleave-Planner-SFT-80k and Interleave-Critic-SFT-112k for cold-start, then Interleave-Critic-RL-13k with GRPO using accuracy and step-wise rewards for efficient single-step RL.
AI.result: InterleaveThinker achieves performance comparable to Nano Banana and GPT-5 on interleaved generation benchmarks, and significantly improves base models on reasoning-based benchmarks (e.g., substantial gains on 4-step FLUX.2-klein for WISE and RISE).
AI.conclusion: The proposed multi-agent pipeline effectively endows any image generator with interleaved generation capability, and the step-wise reward strategy makes RL training computationally feasible for long generation trajectories.

title: Demystifying Hidden-State Recurrence: Switchable Latent Reasoning with On-Policy Reinforcement Learning
summary: Latent chain-of-thought compresses reasoning by replacing visible reasoning traces with continuous hidden-state recurrence, but existing formulations are difficult to optimize with standard on-policy reinforcement learning (RL) and hard to interpret causally. Our key insight is that a single pair of explicit boundary tokens can address both issues at once: discrete entry and exit anchors make the latent block compatible with standard on-policy RL, and the same anchors offer a natural foothold for mechanistic analysis. Motivated by this, we propose SWITCH, a switchable latent reasoning framework. The model emits <swi> to enter latent mode and </swi> to exit. Because the boundaries are ordinary discrete tokens, the GRPO policy ratio is well-defined at every decision point. The same anchors also expose the latent steps to direct probing and causal intervention. We train the model with a visible-to-latent curriculum and a Switch-GRPO objective that propagates gradients through recurrent latent computation. SWITCH consistently outperforms prior hidden-state-recurrence latent reasoning approaches at similar scale. Mechanistic analysis through the boundary tokens further reveals three findings: (i) <swi> is a sharply localised, learned switching policy rather than a stylistic artefact; (ii) the latent step it opens performs problem-specific, causally important computation rather than acting as an inert placeholder; and (iii) that computation is concentrated at a single hidden-state transition on entry. Together, these results show that hidden-state-recurrence latent reasoning is both RL-trainable and open to direct mechanistic analysis, including of how on-policy RL itself improves the model from the inside.

AI.tldr: SWITCH uses two discrete boundary tokens (<swi> and </swi>) to make latent chain-of-thought reasoning compatible with standard on-policy RL (GRPO) and amenable to mechanistic analysis, outperforming prior latent reasoning methods and revealing that latent steps are causally important and concentrated at entry.
AI.motivation: Existing latent chain-of-thought formulations are difficult to optimize with standard on-policy RL and hard to interpret causally; the authors aim to solve both issues simultaneously.
AI.method: Proposes SWITCH, which uses discrete entry (<swi>) and exit (</swi>) boundary tokens to make latent reasoning compatible with GRPO; trains via a visible-to-latent curriculum and Switch-GRPO objective.
AI.result: SWITCH consistently outperforms prior hidden-state-recurrence latent reasoning approaches at similar scale.
AI.conclusion: Hidden-state-recurrence latent reasoning is both RL-trainable and open to direct mechanistic analysis, revealing that the latent computation is localized, causally important, and concentrated at entry.

title: Reward Modeling for Multi-Agent Orchestration
summary: Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) built on Large Language Models (LLMs) require effective orchestration to coordinate specialized agents, yet training such orchestrators is hindered by limited supervision and high computational cost. We propose Orchestration Reward Modeling (OrchRM), a self-supervised framework for evaluating orchestration quality without human annotations. OrchRM leverages intermediate artifacts from multi-agent executions to construct win-lose pairs for Bradley-Terry reward model training. Unlike existing MAS test-time scaling and orchestrator training frameworks that rely on costly sub-agent rollouts, OrchRM operates directly at the orchestration level, enabling efficient and high-performing reward-guided orchestrator training and MAS test-time scaling. OrchRM improves training efficiency by up to 10x in token usage while improving MAS test-time scaling performance by up to 8% in accuracy. These gains consistently transfer across multiple domains, including mathematical reasoning, web-based question answering, and multi-hop reasoning, demonstrating orchestration-level reward modeling as a scalable direction for robust multi-agent orchestration. Code will be available at https://github.com/Wang-ML-Lab/OrchRM.

AI.tldr: OrchRM is a self-supervised framework that uses reward modeling at the orchestration level to train multi-agent orchestrators without human annotations, achieving 10x training efficiency gains and up to 8% accuracy improvements in test-time scaling.
AI.motivation: Training orchestrators for LLM-based multi-agent systems is hindered by limited supervision and high computational cost from sub-agent rollouts.
AI.method: OrchRM constructs win-lose pairs from intermediate multi-agent execution artifacts to train a Bradley-Terry reward model at the orchestration level, avoiding costly sub-agent rollouts.
AI.result: Up to 10x improvement in token efficiency during training; up to 8% accuracy improvement in MAS test-time scaling; consistent gains across mathematical reasoning, web-based QA, and multi-hop reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Orchestration-level reward modeling is a scalable and efficient direction for robust multi-agent orchestration without human annotations.

title: MP3: Multi-Period Pattern Pre-training forSpatio-Temporal Forecasting
summary: Spatio-Temporal forecasting is crucial in diverse fields, such as transportation, climate, and energy. Urban spatio-temporal data exhibits temporal mirage: similar short-window inputs have divergent future trends, and vice versa. Existing spatio-temporal graph neural networks (STGNNs) cannot effectively identify such mirages. We argue that the core reason lies in the short-window inputs that have incomplete period observation, heterogeneous global spatial correlation, and cross-period superposition causality. To bridge this gap, we develop a novel Multi- Period Pattern Pre-training (MP3), a plug-and-play pre-training plugin for distinguishing temporal mirages. MP3 presents two core innovations: (1) The multi-period pattern learning is designed to learn multi-period patterns from long time series. Specifically, multi-period temporal modeling leverages edge convolution to identify different multi-period patterns. Multi-period spatial modeling uses a bottleneck project and a global memory bank to capture heterogeneous global spatial relations efficiently. Cross-period pattern interaction employs a causality-enhanced Transformer to capture dependencies across different period patterns. (2) This plugin can seamlessly integrate into existing STGNN backbones to strengthen their forecasting performance. The experiment on five STGNN baselines across five real-world datasets (including a large-scale dataset CA) verify the effectiveness, superior scalability and strong adaptability of MP3, which brings consistent and robust performance improvements across all evaluated baselines. On average, MP3 reduces the MAE 4.7% and the RMSE 5.0%. The code can be available at https://github.com/YAN-outlook/MP3.

AI.tldr: MP3 is a plug-and-play pre-training plugin that helps spatio-temporal graph neural networks distinguish "temporal mirages" (where similar short-term inputs lead to different future trends) by learning multi-period patterns through temporal edge convolution, global spatial memory banks, and causality-enhanced Transformers, achieving 4.7% MAE and 5.0% RMSE improvement across five datasets and five STGNN backbones.
AI.motivation: Existing STGNNs fail to identify "temporal mirages" in urban spatio-temporal data—where similar short-window inputs have divergent future trends—due to incomplete period observation, heterogeneous global spatial correlation, and cross-period superposition causality.
AI.method: Multi-period temporal modeling uses edge convolution to identify multi-period patterns; multi-period spatial modeling uses a bottleneck project and global memory bank for heterogeneous global spatial relations; cross-period pattern interaction employs a causality-enhanced Transformer. The plugin integrates seamlessly into existing STGNN backbones.
AI.result: Tested on five STGNN baselines across five real-world datasets (including large-scale CA dataset). MP3 brings consistent improvements: average 4.7% MAE reduction and 5.0% RMSE reduction. Demonstrates effectiveness, scalability, and strong adaptability.
AI.conclusion: MP3 effectively addresses the temporal mirage problem in spatio-temporal forecasting via multi-period pattern pre-training, and serves as a plug-and-play solution that robustly improves existing STGNN models.

title: EpiBench: Verifiable Evaluation of AI Agents on Epigenomics Analysis
summary: We introduce EpiBench, a verifiable benchmark for short-horizon epigenomics analysis. EpiBench evaluates whether agents can make well-defined analysis decisions from realistic workflow states and return deterministically gradable answers. The benchmark includes 106 evaluations across CUT\&Tag/CUT\&RUN, ATAC-seq, ChIP-seq, and DNA methylation workflows. Across 5,088 valid trajectories from 16 model-harness pairs, no system passed a majority of attempts: GPT-5.5 / Pi led at 45.0\% (143/318 attempts; 95\% confidence interval (CI), 36.3--53.7), followed by GPT-5.5 / OpenAI Codex at 39.9\% (127/318 attempts; 95\% CI, 31.6--48.3). Claude Opus 4.8 Max / Pi and GPT-5.4 / Pi each passed 39.0\% (124/318 attempts; 95\% CI, 30.2--47.8 and 31.0--47.0, respectively). Performance varies across assay types, and many failed runs still contain parts of the correct answer. Agents often found the right files and computed useful intermediate results, but failed when the task required deeper, assay-specific scientific judgment.

AI.tldr: EpiBench is a benchmark for epigenomics analysis that tests AI agents on realistic workflows. Across 5,088 trajectories from 16 model-harness pairs, the best system (GPT-5.5/Pi) achieved only 45% success, with agents often finding correct files but failing on deeper scientific judgment.
AI.motivation: To create a verifiable benchmark for evaluating AI agents on short-horizon epigenomics analysis tasks, assessing whether they can make well-defined analysis decisions from realistic workflow states.
AI.method: The authors introduce EpiBench with 106 evaluations across CUT&Tag/CUT&RUN, ATAC-seq, ChIP-seq, and DNA methylation workflows. They tested 16 model-harness pairs, collecting 5,088 valid trajectories for analysis.
AI.result: No system passed a majority of attempts. GPT-5.5/Pi led at 45.0% (143/318; 95% CI 36.3-53.7), followed by GPT-5.5/Codex at 39.9%, and Claude Opus 4.8 Max/Pi and GPT-5.4/Pi at 39.0%. Performance varied across assay types.
AI.conclusion: Current AI agents can find relevant files and compute useful intermediate results, but consistently fail when tasks require deeper, assay-specific scientific judgment, highlighting a key gap in current capabilities.

title: Select and Improve: Understanding the Mechanics of Post-Training for Reasoning
summary: Reinforcement learning has rapidly emerged as a key component in the training of reasoning and coding models, yet it remains poorly understood from a mechanistic perspective. We study how and through what underlying processes capabilities are acquired or enhanced via reinforcement learning post-training. Our analysis, based on controlled math reasoning experiments with Qwen-2.5-1.5B, reveals two core mechanisms: strategy selection and strategy improvement. Our results highlight the role of SFT data and reinforcement learning data in activating these mechanisms, in particular showing how supervising the model on diverse reasoning strategies can enable strategy selection and how increasing difficulty in reinforcement learning data can enable strategy improvement. Taken together, our results provide mechanistic insight into RL training and suggest practical interventions to continue scaling reasoning capabilities.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how reinforcement learning post-training enhances reasoning capabilities in language models, identifying two core mechanisms: strategy selection and strategy improvement, using controlled math experiments with Qwen-2.5-1.5B.
AI.motivation: Reinforcement learning is widely used in training reasoning/coding models, but its mechanistic underpinnings—how capabilities are actually acquired or enhanced—are poorly understood.
AI.method: The authors conduct controlled math reasoning experiments using Qwen-2.5-1.5B, analyzing how SFT data (diverse reasoning strategies) and RL data (increasing difficulty) activate strategy selection and improvement mechanisms.
AI.result: Two core mechanisms identified: (1) strategy selection—enabled by supervising the model on diverse reasoning strategies in SFT data; (2) strategy improvement—enabled by increasing difficulty in RL data.
AI.conclusion: The findings provide mechanistic insight into RL training and suggest practical interventions for continuing to scale reasoning capabilities.

title: Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning from Delayed Marketplace Feedback for Objective-Weight Adaptation in Three-Sided Dispatch
comment: Accepted at ICML 2026 Workshop on Reinforcement Learning from World Feedback (RLxF)
summary: Dispatch in three-sided marketplaces provides a natural setting for reinforcement learning from world feedback: decisions are evaluated by delayed operational outcomes such as delivery speed, courier utilization, and merchant congestion. We present a deployed reinforcement learning system at DoorDash that adapts dispatch objective weights in a large-scale food-delivery marketplace using delayed signals. Rather than replacing the combinatorial assignment optimizer, a store-level policy learned from logged marketplace data selects a discrete multiplier that shifts the dispatch optimizer's tradeoff between delivery quality and batching efficiency. This interface enables offline policy learning under noisy, delayed, and coupled feedback while preserving production feasibility constraints and operational safeguards. We train a shared value function using centralized offline data and decentralized store-level execution, with Double Q-learning targets and a conservative regularizer to reduce out-of-distribution value overestimation. In a production switchback experiment, the offline-trained policy increases batching and reduces courier-side time costs without degrading customer-facing delivery quality. Results illustrate how world feedback from a live economic and logistics system can be used to safely adapt decision policies online.

AI.tldr: DoorDash deploys a reinforcement learning system that learns dispatch objective weights from delayed operational feedback in a three-sided food-delivery marketplace, improving batching efficiency and courier utilization without degrading delivery quality.
AI.motivation: To adapt dispatch policies in three-sided marketplaces (consumers, couriers, merchants) using delayed operational outcomes (delivery speed, courier utilization, merchant congestion) as feedback, while maintaining production feasibility and safety.
AI.method: A store-level RL policy selects discrete multipliers to shift the dispatch optimizer's tradeoff between delivery quality and batching efficiency. Uses Double Q-learning with conservative regularization, offline learning from logged data, centralized value function training with decentralized execution.
AI.result: Production switchback experiments show increased batching and reduced courier-side time costs without degrading customer-facing delivery quality.
AI.conclusion: World feedback from live economic/logistics systems can safely adapt decision policies online, and RL can interface with existing combinatorial optimizers via learned parameter adjustments.

title: MiniPIC: Flexible Position-Independent Caching in <100LOC
summary: Retrieval-augmented and agentic workloads repeatedly prefill recurring predictable structured inputs (which we call "spans") such as documents and code files. Yet, prefix caching in engines such as vLLM cannot reuse their KV entries unless they share identical prefixes with another request, while Position-Independent Caching (PIC) implementations within production-grade inference servers typically either require substantial server code changes or keep KV state outside the server, incurring host-to-device transfer overhead. We present Minimalistic PIC (MiniPIC): a minimal, flexible and fast vLLM design built from two ingredients: positional-encoding-free KV cache and user-controlled cache-reuse primitives. MiniPIC stores unrotated K vectors in the KV cache, applies RoPE to K tiles inside attention using per-request logical positions, and exposes three user-facing and token-level primitives: block-aligned padding, span separator (SSep), and prompt depend (PDep), that modify hashing behavior and effective block-level causal attention structure. With fewer than 100 lines of core-engine changes plus a custom attention backend, these primitives are sufficient to realize multiple PIC methods, including Block-Attention, EPIC, and Prompt Cache, within the same running vLLM instance, while natively integrating with KV cache CPU offload implementations. On 2WikiMultihopQA, MiniPIC with interleaved scheduling improves prefill throughput by 49% over baseline vLLM, reduces cached-span time-to-first-token by up to two orders of magnitude, preserves the linear prefill scaling of uncached spans, and incurs only 5.7% worst-case overhead.

AI.tldr: MiniPIC is a minimal, flexible, and fast vLLM design for position-independent caching (PIC) that uses unrotated K vectors, RoPE applied per-request, and three user-facing primitives to achieve efficient KV cache reuse across recurring spans like documents/code, improving prefill throughput by 49% and reducing time-to-first-token by up to two orders of magnitude.
AI.motivation: Retrieval-augmented and agentic workloads frequently process recurring predictable structured inputs (spans) such as documents and code files, but existing prefix caching cannot reuse KV entries unless prefixes match exactly, and current PIC implementations require substantial server code changes or incur host-to-device transfer overhead.
AI.method: MiniPIC stores unrotated K vectors in the KV cache, applies RoPE to K tiles inside attention using per-request logical positions, and exposes three user-facing token-level primitives: block-aligned padding, span separator (SSep), and prompt depend (PDep), which modify hashing behavior and causal attention structure.
AI.result: On 2WikiMultihopQA, MiniPIC improves prefill throughput by 49% over baseline vLLM, reduces cached-span time-to-first-token by up to two orders of magnitude, preserves linear prefill scaling for uncached spans, and incurs only 5.7% worst-case overhead.
AI.conclusion: With fewer than 100 lines of core-engine changes plus a custom attention backend, these primitives are sufficient to realize multiple PIC methods (Block-Attention, EPIC, Prompt Cache) within the same running vLLM instance, while natively integrating with KV cache CPU offload implementations.

title: Reasoning as Pattern Matching: Shared Mechanisms in Human and LLM Everyday Reasoning
comment: 13 pages main text, 51 pages supplementary text
summary: When large language models (LLMs) fail to generalize or make haphazard errors in reasoning, it is often taken as evidence that LLMs are not truly reasoning, but rather performing a kind of pattern matching. The implication is that people's behavior does not exhibit the same types of failures because human reasoning uses principled and abstract world models. We evaluate human participants and 25 LLMs on their ability to engage in common-sense reasoning about a variety of everyday situations and observe similar patterns of errors in both people and models. We then identify the set of attention heads driving LLM responses and find that these heads implement a form of pattern-matching. These attention heads allow us to predict seemingly inexplicable reasoning errors in people caused by ostensibly irrelevant prompt details. Taken together, our results suggest that everyday causal reasoning in people and LLMs is more consistent with a form of pattern-matching than with abstract world models.

AI.tldr: Both humans and LLMs exhibit similar pattern-matching errors in common-sense reasoning, challenging the assumption that human reasoning relies on abstract world models while LLMs only pattern-match.
AI.motivation: To challenge the common assumption that human reasoning uses principled abstract world models while LLM failures are due to pattern-matching, by directly comparing error patterns between humans and LLMs.
AI.method: Evaluated human participants and 25 LLMs on common-sense reasoning tasks; identified attention heads driving LLM responses and linked them to predictable reasoning errors in both groups.
