AI.motivation: Existing multimodal VAEs struggle with a trade-off between generating realistic/diverse samples and maintaining semantic consistency across modalities.
AI.method: (i) First exact implementation of Hölder pooling (no approximation) for multimodal VAEs; (ii) Architecture with distinct shared and private (modality-specific) representations (Hölder+); (iii) Hierarchical inference to further disentangle shared and private representations (Hölder++).
AI.result: Hölder++ consistently improves the generative quality-coherence trade-off, yields more structured latent spaces, and learns shared representations that are informative for downstream tasks.
AI.conclusion: Exact Hölder pooling plus disentangled shared/private representations with hierarchical inference effectively resolves the quality-coherence dilemma in multimodal VAEs.

title: Accelerating Speculative Diffusions via Block Verification
summary: Speculative decoding speeds up LLM inference by using a draft model to generate tokens, with an acceptance-rejection scheme that ensures that the output matches the target distribution. Adapting this to continuous diffusions is difficult because speculative sampling requires drawing from a residual distribution. While straightforward in discrete spaces, efficiently sampling this residual in continuous space is non-trivial. Consequently, existing diffusion adaptations either use computationally inefficient sampling techniques or rely on an alternative scheme. In this work, we introduce a novel scheme that efficiently implements the original speculative sampling mechanism for diffusion models. Our approach offers a critical advantage over current methods: it enables us to adapt block verification from LLMs to diffusions -- which provably improves the acceptance rate of drafts. Furthermore, we formalize and analyze the Free Drafter, a heuristic self-speculative drafter for diffusions that requires no training. By enabling block verification, our Free Drafter yields up to a 6.3% speedup over existing speculative methods with no additional training and negligible overhead beyond the existing parallel verification pass.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a novel speculative decoding scheme for diffusion models that efficiently implements the original speculative sampling mechanism, enabling block verification that improves draft acceptance rates. The method achieves up to 6.3% speedup over existing speculative methods with no additional training.
AI.motivation: Existing adaptations of speculative decoding to diffusion models either use computationally inefficient sampling techniques or rely on alternative schemes, because efficiently sampling the residual distribution in continuous space is non-trivial.
AI.method: The authors propose a novel scheme that efficiently implements the original speculative sampling mechanism for diffusion models, which enables adapting block verification from LLMs to diffusions. They also formalize and analyze the Free Drafter, a training-free self-speculative drafter.
AI.result: The Free Drafter yields up to a 6.3% speedup over existing speculative methods with no additional training and negligible overhead beyond the existing parallel verification pass.
AI.conclusion: Block verification provably improves acceptance rate of drafts in diffusion models, and the proposed approach enables this without computational inefficiency.

title: How Much Memory Do We Need? Adaptive Memory Gate for Neural Operators
summary: Neural operators have emerged as a powerful data-driven approach for solving time-dependent PDEs. Among recent advances, memory-augmented neural operators explicitly incorporate past states and have achieved remarkable performance under low-resolution observation settings. However, existing approaches apply a fixed memory weight regardless of observation conditions, such as resolution or physical parameters, limiting their adaptability. Our preliminary experiments reveal that optimal memory weight varies with resolution and viscosity, implying that a fixed memory weight cannot simultaneously optimize performance across diverse settings. We propose AMGFNO, which dynamically modulates memory weight through a learnable gate. On the Kuramoto-Sivashinsky and Burgers' equations, AMGFNO achieves 55-79% nRMSE reduction over at low resolution, with the learned gate value automatically decreasing from $\bar{g} \approx 0.7$ to near-zero as resolution increases.

AI.tldr: AMGFNO dynamically adapts memory weights in neural operators via a learnable gate, achieving 55-79% nRMSE reduction on PDEs by adjusting to varying resolutions and viscosities.
AI.motivation: Existing memory-augmented neural operators use fixed memory weights regardless of observation conditions (resolution, viscosity), limiting their adaptability; optimal memory weight varies with these conditions.
AI.method: Proposes AMGFNO, which dynamically modulates memory weight through a learnable gate that automatically adjusts based on observation conditions.
AI.result: AMGFNO achieves 55-79% nRMSE reduction over existing methods at low resolution on Kuramoto-Sivashinsky and Burgers' equations; learned gate value decreases from ~0.7 to near-zero as resolution increases.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic memory weighting via a learnable gate significantly improves neural operator performance across varying observation conditions.

title: Clustering Node Attributed Networks with Graph Neural Networks and Self Learning
summary: Graph clustering - partitioning the node set of a graph into disjoint subsets that reflect some latent information - is a fundamental problem as it finds applications in a myriad of different scenarios. While this classic problem has been tackled for decades by different communities, a recent variation of the problem driven by real data considers the scenario where nodes have attributes that are also informative. This has triggered novel methods that simultaneously leverage network information (edges) and node information (attributed) in the design of novel clustering algorithms. This work proposes a novel framework that builds on prior works that have applied graph neural networks (GNN) to graph clustering. The proposed framework operates in rounds of self learning in a fully unsupervised setting. In each round, a GNN generates representations for nodes that are used to cluster the nodes. This clustering influences the graph used to generate the node representation in the next round. Moreover, a context graph built in each round using the original graph is used to generate the node representations. Empirical results show that the proposed methodology extracts information from both network edges and node attributes in synthetic data, outperforming algorithms focused solely on the network or attributes when neither are very informative. Multiple rounds of learning also improve the performance and always outperforms a long single round of training (i.e., classic GNN graph clustering). When considering real datasets, empirical results indicate that the proposed methodology is competitive to state-of-the-art methods when cluster sizes are balanced.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a novel unsupervised graph clustering framework that uses Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) in iterative rounds of self-learning. Each round generates node representations for clustering, which then refines the graph for the next round. A context graph built from the original graph aids representation learning. The method outperforms baselines on synthetic data and is competitive on real data with balanced cluster sizes.
AI.motivation: Graph clustering traditionally focuses only on network edges, but real-world data often includes informative node attributes. The authors aim to simultaneously leverage both graph structure and node attributes in a fully unsupervised manner, addressing limitations of prior works that rely on single-round GNN training.
AI.method: The framework operates in multiple rounds of self-learning. In each round, a GNN generates node representations, which are then clustered. The resulting clustering influences the graph used for representation learning in the next round. A context graph (built from the original graph in each round) is also used to generate node representations, enabling the model to extract information from both edges and attributes.
AI.result: On synthetic data, the proposed method outperforms algorithms that use only network or only attribute information when neither is very informative. Multiple rounds of learning improve performance and consistently outperform a single long round (classic GNN clustering). On real datasets, the method is competitive with state-of-the-art when cluster sizes are balanced.
AI.conclusion: The iterative self-learning framework effectively integrates network and attribute information for unsupervised graph clustering. It demonstrates clear advantages over single-round GNN approaches and is competitive on real data under balanced cluster conditions.

title: Uncertainty Estimation for Molecular Diffusion Models
summary: Diffusion models have seen wide adoption for 3D molecular generation, yet they offer no principled signal of when a generated molecule is likely to be of low quality. We propose a post-hoc method for estimating per-sample uncertainty in pretrained molecular diffusion models. Building on a Laplace approximation of the denoising network, we measure the variability of the noise prediction across the generation trajectory. Empirically, we show that the resulting uncertainty score is informative of sample quality, exhibiting a negative correlation with established sample-level quality metrics. We further study how the proposed uncertainty score can be used to filter generated samples, improving model performance via test-time scaling.

AI.tldr: Proposes a post-hoc uncertainty estimation method for 3D molecular diffusion models using Laplace approximation, showing negative correlation with sample quality and enabling test-time filtering to improve performance.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models for 3D molecular generation lack a principled way to identify low-quality generated molecules.
AI.method: Uses Laplace approximation on the denoising network to measure noise prediction variability across the generation trajectory, producing a per-sample uncertainty score.
AI.result: The uncertainty score negatively correlates with established sample-level quality metrics and can be used to filter out low-quality samples.
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty estimation improves model performance through test-time scaling by filtering generated samples.

title: MaxProof: Scaling Mathematical Proof with Generative-Verifier RL and Population-Level Test-Time Scaling
summary: We present MaxProof, a population-level test-time scaling framework for competition-level mathematical proof in the MiniMax-M3 series. M3 first trains three proof-oriented capabilities -- proof generation, proof verification, and critique-conditioned proof repair -- using a defense-in-depth generative verifier engineered for low false-positive rate. These capabilities are merged into a single released M3 model. At test time, MaxProof treats the model as a generator, verifier, refiner, and ranker, searches over a population of candidate proofs, and returns one final proof through tournament selection. With MaxProof test-time scaling, the M3 model reaches 35/42 on IMO 2025 and 36/42 on USAMO 2026, exceeding the human gold-medal threshold on both.

AI.tldr: MaxProof is a test-time scaling framework for mathematical proof that combines proof generation, verification, and repair, enabling the M3 model to exceed human gold-medal thresholds on IMO 2025 (35/42) and USAMO 2026 (36/42).
AI.motivation: To improve competition-level mathematical proof capabilities by scaling inference-time computation through population-level search, surpassing human expert performance on olympiad-level problems.
AI.method: Trains three proof-oriented capabilities (generation, verification, critique-conditioned repair) using a defense-in-depth generative verifier, then merges them into a single M3 model. At test time, MaxProof treats the model as generator, verifier, refiner, and ranker, performing population-level search over candidate proofs with tournament selection.
AI.result: M3 with MaxProof achieves 35/42 on IMO 2025 and 36/42 on USAMO 2026, exceeding the human gold-medal threshold on both.
AI.conclusion: Test-time scaling via population-level search significantly boosts mathematical proof capabilities, enabling models to surpass human gold-medal-level performance on olympiad mathematics.

title: SupraBench: A Benchmark for Supramolecular Chemistry
summary: Supramolecular chemistry, which includes the study of non-covalent host-guest assemblies, has advanced various applications. However, designing host-guest systems remains time-consuming, requiring days of dry-lab verification per candidate pair. Although LLMs have emerged as a fast alternative with strong performance on molecular binding tasks, no benchmark currently systematically evaluates LLMs for host-guest reasoning across fundamental supramolecular chemistry tasks, e.g., binding affinity prediction. To this end, we collaborate with domain experts to release the first Supramolecular Benchmark, called SupraBench, to evaluate LLMs in chemistry reasoning. Specifically, we design four fundamental tasks, i.e., binding affinity prediction, top-binder selection, solvent identification, and host-guest description, plus an auxiliary vision-based task for molecular identification. We also release SupraPMC, a curated 16M-token corpus of Supramolecular chemistry articles distilled from Europe PMC, to support the adaptation to the supramolecular domain. We benchmark a broad range of open and proprietary LLMs and find that LLMs leave substantial headroom across all tasks. Domain adaptation pretraining over SupraPMC transfers cleanly to in-distribution regression but trades off against strict letter-format output. Moreover, the difficulty profile differs sharply across task families, revealing distinct failure modes that indicate specific gaps in current supramolecular chemistry reasoning. Our source codes and benchmark datasets are available at https://github.com/Tianyi-Billy-Ma/SupraBench.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SupraBench, the first benchmark for evaluating LLMs on supramolecular host-guest chemistry reasoning, covering four core tasks (binding affinity, top-binder selection, solvent identification, host-guest description) and a vision task, along with a curated 16M-token corpus (SupraPMC). Evaluation shows LLMs have substantial room for improvement, with domain adaptation helping in-distribution tasks but hurting strict formatting performance.
AI.motivation: Designing host-guest systems in supramolecular chemistry is time-consuming (days per candidate pair). While LLMs offer fast alternatives for molecular binding tasks, no systematic benchmark exists to evaluate their reasoning capabilities across fundamental supramolecular chemistry tasks.
AI.method: The authors collaborate with domain experts to design SupraBench with four fundamental tasks (binding affinity prediction, top-binder selection, solvent identification, host-guest description) plus an auxiliary vision-based molecular identification task. They also curate SupraPMC, a 16M-token corpus from Europe PMC, for domain adaptation pretraining. They benchmark multiple open and proprietary LLMs on these tasks.
AI.result: LLMs show substantial headroom across all tasks. Domain adaptation pretraining on SupraPMC transfers cleanly to in-distribution regression tasks but trades off against strict letter-format output. Different task families exhibit sharply different difficulty profiles, revealing distinct failure modes and specific gaps in current supramolecular chemistry reasoning.
AI.conclusion: The paper releases the first systematic benchmark for supramolecular chemistry LLM reasoning, demonstrating that current models have significant room for improvement and that domain adaptation involves trade-offs. The benchmark and corpus are publicly available to drive future research.

title: CRAFTIIF: Cross-Resolution Analytic Four-Type Interpretable Isolation Forest for Multivariate Time Series Anomaly Detection
comment: 14 pages, 4 figures, 2 appendices. Submitted to IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering (TKDE). Code: https://github.com/smitswil/craftiif
summary: Anomaly detection in multivariate time series is challenged by four structurally distinct anomaly types -- point (isolated spikes), distributional (level shifts), temporal (rhythm changes), and collective (inter-sensor correlation breakdowns) -- each requiring different feature representations. Most unsupervised methods target only one or two types and provide limited interpretability. We present CRAFTIIF (Cross-Resolution Analytic Four-Type Interpretable Isolation Forest), a fully unsupervised framework targeting all four types without dataset-specific tuning. CRAFTIIF generates K=500 random analytic wavelet feature draws across four families (Morlet, DOG, Haar, Coiflet), each targeting a specific anomaly type, feeding five structured Isolation Forests -- one per type plus a meta-IF for compound anomalies. An adaptive Otsu/MAD threshold calibrates detection automatically across anomaly rates from 0.1% to 69.2%. Because each IF is trained exclusively on type-specific features, branch firing provides direct anomaly-type attribution by construction, without post-hoc explanation. Evaluated on all 19 datasets of the mTSBench benchmark (Zhou et al., TMLR 2026), CRAFTIIF achieves mean F1=0.228 (all 19 datasets) and F1=0.322 (13 detectable datasets), ranking first among all 25 evaluated methods on VUS-PR (0.463 vs. previous best 0.329, +40.7%). A diagnostic framework -- oracle F1, detectability limits, and branch separation ratios -- identifies 6 of 19 datasets as fundamentally undetectable by any unsupervised method. Ablation over 11 conditions confirms adaptive thresholding (+38% F1), four-branch structure (+20%), and meta-IF (+23%) are each essential. Code: https://github.com/smitswil/craftiif

AI.tldr: CRAFTIIF is a fully unsupervised anomaly detection framework for multivariate time series that targets all four anomaly types (point, distributional, temporal, collective) using 500 random analytic wavelet features across four families, five structured Isolation Forests, and adaptive Otsu/MAD thresholding, achieving state-of-the-art results on the mTSBench benchmark (mean F1=0.228, VUS-PR=0.463, +40.7% over previous best).
AI.motivation: Existing unsupervised anomaly detection methods in multivariate time series target only one or two of the four structurally distinct anomaly types (point, distributional, temporal, collective) and lack interpretability, motivating the need for a unified framework.
AI.method: CRAFTIIF generates K=500 random analytic wavelet feature draws across four families (Morlet, DOG, Haar, Coiflet), each targeting a specific anomaly type, feeds them into five structured Isolation Forests (one per type plus a meta-IF for compound anomalies), and uses adaptive Otsu/MAD threshold calibration for detection across anomaly rates from 0.1% to 69.2%.
AI.result: On the mTSBench benchmark (19 datasets), CRAFTIIF achieves mean F1=0.228 (all 19), F1=0.322 (13 detectable datasets), and ranks first on VUS-PR (0.463 vs. previous best 0.329, +40.7%). Ablation shows adaptive thresholding (+38% F1), four-branch structure (+20%), and meta-IF (+23%) are each essential.
AI.conclusion: CRAFTIIF is the first fully unsupervised method to target all four anomaly types simultaneously with built-in type attribution, and a diagnostic framework identifies 6 of 19 datasets as fundamentally undetectable by any unsupervised method.

title: A2D2: Fine-Tuning Any-Length Discrete Diffusion for Adaptive Decoding
summary: Discrete diffusion models offer a simple and stable likelihood-based framework for sequence generation, recently extended to any-length settings via token insertion. Principled reward-guided fine-tuning for any-length discrete diffusion, however, remains largely unexplored. We introduce Fine-Tuning Any-Length Discrete Diffusion for Adaptive Decoding (A2D2), a unified framework for reward-guided fine-tuning of any-length discrete diffusion models via joint optimization of the insertion and unmasking policies together with a quality-based inference schedule. We derive the Radon-Nikodym derivative for the joint insertion-unmasking path measures, enabling theoretically guaranteed convergence to the intractable reward-tilted sequence distribution without requiring target samples. Building on this, we establish unmasking and insertion quality as tractable approaches for minimizing decoding error and introduce the Adaptive Joint Decoding (AJD) loss, which provably yields the optimal path measure that generates the reward-tilted distribution. Empirically, A2D2 improves reward optimization while enhancing generation flexibility and accuracy over prior fixed-length fine-tuning and inference-time guidance methods.

AI.tldr: A2D2 is a unified framework for reward-guided fine-tuning of any-length discrete diffusion models, jointly optimizing insertion and unmasking policies with a quality-based inference schedule, achieving provable convergence to reward-tilted distributions and improved empirical performance.
AI.motivation: Reward-guided fine-tuning for any-length discrete diffusion models is largely unexplored despite the framework's potential for sequence generation.
AI.method: Derives the Radon-Nikodym derivative for joint insertion-unmasking path measures, establishes unmasking and insertion quality as tractable approaches for minimizing decoding error, and introduces the Adaptive Joint Decoding (AJD) loss that provably yields the optimal path measure.
AI.result: A2D2 improves reward optimization while enhancing generation flexibility and accuracy over prior fixed-length fine-tuning and inference-time guidance methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides theoretically grounded, sample-free reward-guided fine-tuning for any-length discrete diffusion with superior empirical results.

title: Adjusted Cup-Product Neural Layer
summary: Many important observables in physics and geometry are cup products of cochains. The adjusted cup product neural layer has been introduced in this paper. It is a neural primitive that hard wires the cup product with an adjustment term from higher gauge theory. This creates a readout that is gauge invariant by design. Their main theoretical result shows that on a closed cycle the output relies entirely on the adjustment coefficient. Setting this coefficient to zero removes the output completely regardless of other parameters. Thus the adjustment is the only source of gauge invariant signal. They prove this observable is a nonzero quadratic form and is exactly invariant under one and two gauge transformations.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces an adjusted cup product neural layer that hard-wires gauge invariance into neural networks, proving that on closed cycles the only gauge-invariant signal comes from an adjustment term from higher gauge theory, which yields a nonzero quadratic form invariant under one and two gauge transformations.
AI.motivation: Many important observables in physics and geometry are cup products of cochains, and there is a need for neural architectures that respect gauge invariance by design rather than learning it.
AI.method: They introduce the adjusted cup product neural layer, a neural primitive that hard-wires the cup product with an adjustment term from higher gauge theory, creating a readout that is gauge invariant by design.
AI.result: On a closed cycle the output relies entirely on the adjustment coefficient; setting this coefficient to zero removes the output completely. The observable is a nonzero quadratic form and is exactly invariant under one and two gauge transformations.
AI.conclusion: The adjustment term is the only source of gauge invariant signal in this architecture, providing a principled way to encode physical symmetries directly into neural network layers.

title: Existence Precedes Value: Joint Modeling of Observational Existence and Evolving States in Time Series Forecasting
summary: Real-world time series are often highly incomplete and irregular due to sensor dormancy, transmission delays, and event-driven sampling, making reliable forecasting fundamentally challenging. Existing methods have evolved from impute-then-forecast pipelines to continuous-time models such as Neural ODEs and continuous-time graph networks. While these approaches improve the modeling of historical irregularity, they still rely on an implicit oracle assumption at inference time: the timestamps of future valid observations are presumed to be known in advance. This assumption limits practical relevance, since in many real systems the more fundamental question is not only what the future value will be, but also whether a valid observation will occur at all. In this paper, we propose Timeflies, a unified framework that reformulates forecasting as a joint problem of future observability inference and value estimation. To explicitly model the interaction between observation dynamics and state evolution, Timeflies adopts an observation stream and a value stream, coupled through three dedicated modules for reliability-aware embedding, observation-guided dependency modeling, and joint prediction. We further construct Shadow, a benchmark that combines natural missingness from public datasets with real-world industrial data, and introduce the Observation-Value Joint Entropy (OVJE) metric to comprehensively evaluate this coupled predictability. Extensive experiments show that Timeflies consistently outperforms existing methods, highlighting the importance of explicitly modeling future observability in time series forecasting with missing values. Code and dataset are available in https://github.com/ant-intl/Timeflies.

AI.tldr: Timeflies proposes a unified framework for time series forecasting that jointly predicts future observability (whether a valid observation will occur) and future values, addressing the limitation of existing methods that assume future timestamps are known in advance.
AI.motivation: Existing time series forecasting methods for irregular/incomplete data assume future observation timestamps are known at inference time (oracle assumption), which is unrealistic for many real-world systems where it's uncertain whether a valid observation will occur at all.
AI.method: Timeflies uses a two-stream architecture (observation stream and value stream) coupled via three modules: reliability-aware embedding, observation-guided dependency modeling, and joint prediction. Also introduces the Shadow benchmark and OVJE metric.
AI.result: Timeflies consistently outperforms existing methods on joint observability and value prediction tasks, validated on the introduced Shadow benchmark combining public and industrial data.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling future observability is crucial for realistic time series forecasting with missing values; the joint prediction framework significantly improves performance over prior approaches.

title: Learning with Simulators: No Regret in a Computationally Bounded World
summary: Understanding the minimal assumptions necessary for generalization is the fundamental question in learning theory. Unfortunately, most results rely heavily on independence (or some proxy thereof) of the data-generating process, while results for strongly dependent data are far more limited. Towards addressing this gap, we introduce the framework of simulatable processes, where the learner has access to a simulator that approximates the distribution generating the data (which may be an arbitrarily complex and dependent process). Surprisingly, given access to such a simulator, we show that we can recover the same learning guarantees as in the classical setting with independent data, namely, error bounds that depend on the VC dimension. Further, we use this framework to study the power of conditional sampling and show strict statistical and computational advantages in this setting. As a highlight of our framework, we exhibit a single algorithm that simultaneously learns any given VC class under all processes samplable in bounded polynomial time, with regret controlled by the time-bounded Kolmogorov complexity of the process. This provides a significant conceptual broadening of the classical PAC model.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces "simulatable processes" where learners access a simulator approximating the data distribution, recovering classical VC-dimension-based generalization guarantees even for strongly dependent data, and achieving universal learning under bounded polynomial-time processes with regret tied to time-bounded Kolmogorov complexity.
AI.motivation: Classical learning theory heavily relies on data independence, but many real-world processes involve strong dependencies. The authors aim to bridge this gap by identifying minimal assumptions for generalization with dependent data.
AI.method: The paper proposes the framework of "simulatable processes," where the learner has access to a simulator approximating the true (possibly complex/dependent) data distribution. Using this framework, they analyze learning guarantees and conditional sampling advantages.
AI.result: (1) Recovering classical VC-dimension-based error bounds even under strongly dependent processes. (2) Demonstrating strict statistical and computational advantages via conditional sampling. (3) A single algorithm learning any VC class under all processes samplable in bounded polynomial time, with regret controlled by time-bounded Kolmogorov complexity.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a significant conceptual broadening of the classical PAC model, showing that with access to a simulator, strong generalization guarantees are achievable even for arbitrarily complex dependent processes.

title: Simplex-Constrained Sparse Bagging: Transitioning from Uniform Priors to Sparse Posteriors in Ensemble Learning
summary: We present Simplex-Constrained Sparse Bagging (SCSB), a mathematically rigorous framework for post-training compression and probability calibration of bootstrap-based bagging ensembles. Standard bagging ensembles (such as Random Forests, Bagged SVMs, and Bagged Neural Networks) assign uniform voting power to all constituent estimators. However, this naive uniform prior ignores the varying local competence of base estimators and contributes to model overconfidence. We formulate ensemble pruning and calibration as a joint optimization problem over the probability simplex by minimizing the Out-Of-Bag (OOB) loss. To induce sparsity, we address the theoretical "L1-simplex paradox" -- the mathematical reality that the L1 norm is constant on the simplex and fails to prune -- by introducing a concave quadratic penalty. SCSB is model-agnostic and achieves up to 96% ensemble compression, yielding linear inference speedups and superior probability calibration (lowered Expected Calibration Error) while preserving or enhancing generalization accuracy.

AI.tldr: SCSB is a framework that compresses bagging ensembles by up to 96% via simplex-constrained optimization with a concave quadratic penalty, improving calibration and speed while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: Standard bagging ensembles assign uniform voting weights to all estimators, which ignores varying local competence of base models and leads to overconfident predictions.
AI.method: Formulates ensemble pruning and calibration as a joint optimization over the probability simplex by minimizing OOB loss, using a concave quadratic penalty to bypass the L1-simplex paradox and induce sparsity.
AI.result: Achieves up to 96% ensemble compression with linear inference speedups and improved probability calibration (lowered Expected Calibration Error), while preserving or improving generalization accuracy.
AI.conclusion: SCSB provides a model-agnostic, mathematically rigorous solution for efficient and well-calibrated bagging ensembles.

title: Beyond the Commitment Boundary: Probing Epiphenomenal Chain-of-Thought in Large Reasoning Models
summary: Chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning is the dominant paradigm for inference-time scaling in language models, yet the causal influence of individual steps on the final answer poorly understood. We estimate each step's causal importance via early exit and use this measure to study how answers form across the reasoning traces of several model families. Across diverse tasks, we find that reasoning typically crosses a \emph{commitment boundary} -- a sharp transition from transient intermediate guesses to a stable, high-confidence answer. This transition often happens in a single step, well before the model's reasoning block ends, and is followed by \emph{epiphenomenal} CoT steps that leave the final answer probability unaltered. Using attention probes, we show that answer-formation stages can be linearly decoded from intermediate reasoning steps with high accuracy and generalize robustly to unseen reasoning tasks. We exploit this signal to early-exit reasoning blocks at the commitment boundary, reducing the length of CoTs up to 55\% on average with negligible impact on model performance.

AI.tldr: This paper discovers that chain-of-thought reasoning has a "commitment boundary" where the model's final answer becomes stable early, and subsequent reasoning steps are epiphenomenal. The authors can detect this boundary using attention probes and early-exit at this point, reducing CoT length by up to 55% without harming performance.
AI.motivation: To understand the causal influence of individual chain-of-thought reasoning steps on the final answer, which is currently poorly understood despite CoT being the dominant paradigm for inference-time scaling in language models.
AI.method: The authors estimate each step's causal importance via early exit, identify a "commitment boundary" where answers become stable, use attention probes to linearly decode answer-formation stages from intermediate reasoning steps, and apply early-exit at the commitment boundary.
AI.result: Reasoning crosses a sharp commitment boundary in a single step where transient guesses become stable answers, followed by epiphenomenal CoT steps. Answer-formation stages can be linearly decoded with high accuracy and generalize to unseen tasks. Early-exiting at the commitment boundary reduces CoT length by up to 55% with negligible performance impact.
AI.conclusion: Many chain-of-thought steps after the commitment boundary are causally unnecessary for the final answer, enabling efficient early-exit strategies that significantly reduce computational cost while maintaining model performance.

title: The Stable Recovery Manifold: Geometric Principles Governing Recoverability in Continual Learning
summary: Catastrophic forgetting is often viewed as the destruction of previously learned knowledge during sequential learning. Building on the Accessibility Collapse framework, we investigate the geometric structure of recoverability in continual learning. Using Split CIFAR-100 and a sequentially trained ResNet-18, we analyze recoverability, representational drift, and recovery complexity across ten tasks. We introduce Recovery Subspace Dimensionality (k_t), a measure of the minimum number of singular directions required to preserve 90 percent of full probe performance. Contrary to our Recoverability Diffusion hypothesis, recovery dimensionality remains stable throughout training (mean k_t = 8.0) despite substantial representational drift. Principal-angle drift strongly predicts recoverability (r = -0.862), and a simple geometric model explains 82.2 percent of recoverability variance. These findings support the Stable Recovery Manifold hypothesis, suggesting that forgotten knowledge remains compactly decodable despite representational reorganization. The results indicate that catastrophic forgetting is primarily an accessibility and manifold-alignment problem rather than information destruction.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that catastrophic forgetting is not about destroying learned knowledge but about accessibility problems. Using Recovery Subspace Dimensionality (k_t), the authors find that forgotten information remains recoverable from a compact subspace (mean k_t=8.0) despite representational drift, supporting the Stable Recovery Manifold hypothesis over the Recoverability Diffusion hypothesis.
AI.motivation: To challenge the prevailing view that catastrophic forgetting destroys previously learned knowledge, and to better understand the geometric nature of knowledge recoverability in continual learning.
AI.method: The authors use Split CIFAR-100 with a sequentially trained ResNet-18, introduce Recovery Subspace Dimensionality (k_t) to measure the minimum singular directions needed for 90% probe performance, and analyze principal-angle drift and recovery complexity across ten tasks.
AI.result: Recovery dimensionality remains stable (k_t ≈ 8.0) despite representational drift. Principal-angle drift strongly predicts recoverability (r = -0.862), and a simple geometric model explains 82.2% of recoverability variance.
AI.conclusion: Catastrophic forgetting is primarily an accessibility and manifold-alignment problem, not information destruction. The Stable Recovery Manifold hypothesis is supported over the Recoverability Diffusion hypothesis.

title: Dense Supervision, Sparse Updates: On the Sparsity and Geometry of On-Policy Distillation
summary: On-policy distillation (\textsc{OPD}) has recently become a prominent post-training recipe as it combines two desirable ingredients: on-policy student trajectories and dense teacher supervision, yet how this hybrid changes a model's parameters remains unclear. Across several language and vision-language model pairs and use cases, our analysis yields two main findings. On sparsity, \textsc{OPD}-style updates are small and coordinate-sparse. They are distributed across layers and are usually FFN-heavy. This sparse structure is operationally useful: training only the discovered subnetwork recovers nearly the same performance as full \textsc{OPD}. However, the sparsity-inducing SGD optimizer underperforms AdamW in our optimizer ablation, likely because dense teacher supervision preserves heterogeneous coordinate-wise gradient scales where AdamW's adaptive scaling remains useful. On geometry, the updates are numerically full-rank but spectrally concentrated; they lie mostly away from the principal singular subspaces of the source weights and fall disproportionately on coordinates where the source weights are close to zero. These findings suggest that dense teacher supervision does not turn \textsc{OPD} into ordinary dense parameter rewriting; instead, \textsc{OPD} retains important geometric signatures of on-policy post-training.

AI.tldr: On-policy distillation (OPD) combines on-policy student trajectories with dense teacher supervision, and its parameter updates are sparse, FFN-heavy, and spectrally concentrated, falling on coordinates where source weights are near zero, making OPD retain geometric signatures of on-policy post-training rather than dense rewriting.
AI.motivation: To understand how the hybrid of on-policy student trajectories and dense teacher supervision in OPD changes a model's parameters, as this remains unclear despite OPD becoming a prominent post-training recipe.
AI.method: The authors analyze OPD across several language and vision-language model pairs and use cases, focusing on sparsity (update magnitudes, coordinate-wise structure, layer distribution, optimizer ablation with SGD vs AdamW) and geometry (numerical rank, spectral concentration, relationship to source weight subspaces and coordinate magnitudes).
AI.result: OPD updates are small, coordinate-sparse, FFN-heavy, and distributed across layers. Full performance can be recovered by training only the discovered subnetwork. AdamW outperforms SGD because dense teacher supervision preserves heterogeneous gradient scales. Updates are numerically full-rank but spectrally concentrated, lying away from principal singular subspaces of source weights and falling on coordinates where source weights are near zero.
AI.conclusion: Dense teacher supervision does not make OPD equivalent to ordinary dense parameter rewriting; OPD retains important geometric signatures of on-policy post-training.

title: Understanding Truncated Positional Encodings for Graph Neural Networks
summary: Positional encodings (PEs) enhance the power of graph neural networks (GNNs), both theoretically and empirically. Two of the most popular families of PEs - spectral (e.g., Laplacian eigenspaces, effective resistance) and walk-based (polynomials of the adjacency matrix) - are theoretically equivalent in expressive power, with expressivity between the 1-WL and 3-WL tests. However, this equivalence assumes the GNN uses the "complete" version of these PEs, which requires $O(n^3)$ time and space complexity. Instead, practitioners commonly use truncated variants of these encodings, such as the first $k$ eigenspaces or powers of the adjacency matrix. However, the theoretical properties of these truncated PEs are unknown. In this work, we initiate the study of these truncated PEs. Theoretically, we show that, under truncation, several families of PEs are fundamentally different in expressive power. As a corollary, we show that truncated spectral PEs are no longer stronger than the 1-WL test. We also study a family of spectral PEs, the $k$-harmonic distances, to highlight the differences in expressive power of even closely related truncated PEs. Finally, we experimentally show that a mix of truncated PEs is preferable to any single family on real-world datasets.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates truncated versions of positional encodings for GNNs, revealing that unlike their complete forms, truncated PEs differ fundamentally in expressive power, with spectral PEs losing strength beyond 1-WL. Experiments suggest mixing truncated PEs outperforms using any single family.
AI.motivation: While complete spectral and walk-based PEs are theoretically equivalent in expressive power (between 1-WL and 3-WL), their practical truncated variants (e.g., first k eigenspaces) lack theoretical understanding. This work aims to bridge this gap by studying the expressive power of truncated PEs.
AI.method: The paper theoretically analyzes truncated versions of spectral and walk-based PEs under the Weisfeiler-Lehman hierarchy, introduces k-harmonic distances as a novel spectral PE family, and validates findings with real-world dataset experiments.
AI.result: Under truncation, different PE families exhibit fundamentally different expressive power. Truncated spectral PEs are no stronger than 1-WL. Closely related truncated PEs (e.g., k-harmonic distances vs. standard spectral PEs) also differ. Mixing truncated PEs yields better empirical performance.
AI.conclusion: Truncation breaks the theoretical equivalence of positional encodings, and practitioners should consider using mixtures of truncated PEs rather than relying on a single family for optimal performance.
